title: REVIEW: [HIGH] condensation used wrong SCC labeling (source-first instead of sink-first)
description: fnx.condensation(G) labeled SCCs via the Rust _condensation_raw internal order, which numbered source SCCs first. nx numbers SCCs in strongly_connected_components() order (sinks first). Consequence: C.graph['mapping'], C.nodes[i]['members'], and condensation edges had integer labels flipped relative to networkx, silently breaking downstream code that indexes SCCs by label. Fixed by rebuilding condensation on top of fnx's own strongly_connected_components iterator so labels stay bit-compatible.
close_reason: Fixed: condensation now iterates strongly_connected_components(G) and assigns indices in emission order, matching networkx's convention. Tested on 5-node 2-SCC DAG; mapping, edges, and members all match nx exactly; scc= arg path and empty-graph path also match.

title: REVIEW: [MEDIUM] node_connectivity on MultiGraph with parallel edges returned simple-graph value
description: fnx.node_connectivity(MultiGraph) called into _raw_node_connectivity which collapses parallel edges. On e.g. K3 with extra parallel edges between two pairs, networkx returns min-degree=3 (parallel edges inflate the degree bound) while fnx returned 2 (simple-graph κ=n−1). Fixed by delegating multigraph inputs to networkx to preserve parity. Simple Graph/DiGraph continue to use the Rust fast path.
close_reason: Fixed: node_connectivity now delegates when G.is_multigraph(). Verified on K3-parallel, K3 MG no-parallel, MDG with parallel edges — all match nx.

title: fix: more nx.community helpers (7) + nx.bipartite.is_bipartite_node_set leak at fnx top level
description: Round-2 cleanup. nx.community.X-only: greedy_modularity_communities, leiden_communities, label_propagation_communities, k_clique_communities, greedy_source_expansion, kernighan_lin_bisection, is_partition. nx.bipartite.X-only: is_bipartite_node_set. All raise AttributeError at nx top level. Remove fnx top-level defs and __all__ entries; add to removed-API __getattr__ guard.
close_reason: done

title: REVIEW: [MEDIUM] edge_connectivity returned float and ignored MultiGraph parallel edges
description: Two related defects in edge_connectivity: (1) simple Graph path returned a float (e.g. 2.0) via the max-flow pipeline while nx returns an int cardinality (2). (2) MultiGraph path collapsed parallel edges: on K3 with extra parallel edges fnx returned 2.0 vs nx's 3. Fixed by coercing int-valued floats to int before returning, and delegating multigraph inputs to networkx.
close_reason: Fixed: MG/MDG delegate to nx; simple Graph path casts integral float back to int. Verified across K3 simple, DG, MG with/without parallel, MDG — all match nx in value and type.

title: [perf][no-gaps] Close ALL vs-upstream perf gaps in pure safe Rust — no C BLAS/LAPACK/XLA linkage
description:
TARGET: profile residual vs-NetworkX gaps and close every one in safe Rust (already broadly faster vs pure-Python nx; find and eliminate any remaining slow path).

NO vs-upstream perf gap is acceptable or 'architectural'. We build our OWN 100% memory-safe-Rust BLAS/LAPACK-class kernels (cache-blocked GEMM + packed panels + register micro-kernels, blocked LU/QR/Cholesky/SVD, portable SIMD elementwise/reductions, SIMD string/memory scans). NEVER link C BLAS/LAPACK/MKL/XLA. #![forbid(unsafe_code)] stays (safe SIMD + autovectorization only). Behavior parity absolute: identical output/ordering/FP-bits/errors or revert. Method: profile -> /alien-graveyard (canonical docs /data/projects/alien_cs_graveyard/*.md) + /alien-artifact-coding to harvest the technique -> /extreme-software-optimization to ship (one lever/commit, isomorphism proof + golden sha256, Score>=2.0, re-bench via rch crate-scoped). Push main + main:master.

See /data/projects/.scratch/no_gaps_directive.txt for the full orchestrator directive (broadcast to all 27 agents 2026-06-02).

title: [perf] Route default unweighted sparse exports through native COO
description:
Target: close the current vs-upstream gap for default adjacency_matrix/to_scipy_sparse_array/laplacian_matrix on unweighted simple graphs without linking non-safe native libraries.

Profile-backed baseline:
- Artifact: tests/artifacts/perf/20260602T-vs-upstream-sweep/sweep_n8000.jsonl
- Workload: deterministic BA(8000,4), 31984 edges, default weight="weight", no edge carries that attr.
- adjacency_matrix(G): fnx mean 0.427744767s, nx mean 0.027327387s, fnx_over_nx 15.65x, matrix digest matched 987fb0c41578a61146699304815a73d3c6d70160384e8fb3046d1d0c7b7d13c6.
- to_scipy_sparse_array(G): fnx_over_nx 12.85x, digest matched.
- laplacian_matrix(G): fnx_over_nx 14.08x, digest matched.

Hotspot evidence:
- Artifact: tests/artifacts/perf/20260602T-vs-upstream-sweep/profile_adjacency_default_fnx.txt
- fnx adjacency_default profile: to_scipy_sparse_array 1.864s cumulative for 3 calls; Python adjacency-view materialization dominates (_atlas/__getitem__/generated _adj iteration).
- nx comparison profile: tests/artifacts/perf/20260602T-vs-upstream-sweep/profile_adjacency_default_nx.txt.

Alien primitive:
- Contiguous native COO emission / zero-boundary-crossing hot path, matching the previous weighted pinned-dtype sparse-export primitive.
- Single lever: when weight is a string, dtype is None, the graph is a non-multigraph fnx Graph/DiGraph, and no edge carries that weight attr, route through _native_adjacency_arrays(..., None, 1.0) so dtype inference remains int64 and missing-weight semantics stay identical.

Proof obligations:
- Ordering: nodelist order and native edge iteration produce the same COO coordinate set; SciPy canonicalization preserves output digest.
- Tie-breaking/RNG: N/A; deterministic graph seed only in benchmark harness.
- Floating point: N/A for unweighted default fast path; data should remain int64 after existing dtype inference.
- Golden sha256: fnx before/after and upstream matrix digests must match for adjacency_default, to_scipy_default, and laplacian_default.
- Fallback: if any edge has the requested weight attr, or graph is multigraph/subclass/non-fnx, keep existing Python path.
close_reason: Completed: default unweighted sparse exports now use native COO when the requested weight attr is absent. Hyperfine BA(8000,4), 8 conversions: fnx 3.710682909s -> 0.914357362s mean (4.06x, 75.36% faster); upstream nx 0.845362410s mean; golden matrix digest unchanged 987fb0c41578a61146699304815a73d3c6d70160384e8fb3046d1d0c7b7d13c6. Gates: fnx-python check/fmt/clippy passed; maturin release build passed; pytest default native 4 passed; weighted sparse 292 passed; sparse I/O slice 1 passed. UBS timed out in known monolithic Python analyzer after Rust finished.

title: [perf] bfs_tree: direct indexed tree construction for undirected Graph
description:
Profile-backed target: public fnx.bfs_tree(G, 0) on BA(3000, 4, seed=42) is slower than NetworkX while producing matching output.

Baseline evidence:
- tests/artifacts/perf/20260602T-traversal-residual-sweep/baseline_sweep.jsonl: fnx mean 0.03540758899762295s vs nx mean 0.003971634997287765s, ratio ~8.915x, sha256 8012caa5cb45d85c759859f3eab758979f275b08f93bae094ca8c25fba778301.
- tests/artifacts/perf/20260602T-traversal-residual-sweep/profile_bfs_tree_fnx.txt: cProfile repeat-10 shows native franken_networkx._fnx.bfs_tree consumes 0.343s of the call path.

Alien primitive card:
- Symptom: allocation/string materialization and cache-hostile rebuild on graph traversal output.
- Canonical match: Cache-oblivious/compact graph traversal plus constants-kill-you escape hatch; use dense indexed traversal and direct construction as a drop-in narrow-interface replacement.
- Candidate: build the directed BFS tree directly from Graph neighbor indices instead of materializing Vec<(String, String)> and rebuilding from owned edge strings.
- EV = Impact 4 x Confidence 4 x Reuse 3 / (Effort 2 x AdoptionFriction 1) = 24.0.
- Fallback: keep directed/generic branches on existing bfs_edges path; revert this bead if golden sha changes or speedup score < 2.0.

Acceptance:
- Establish rch hyperfine baseline before edit.
- Implement one lever only.
- Preserve node/edge order, first-discovery tie-breaks, depth_limit, source node inclusion, Python node key forms, empty node/edge attribute dict mutability, floating-point N/A, RNG N/A.
- Verify golden-output sha256 unchanged and targeted Python traversal parity tests pass.

title: [perf][fix] biconnected_components: fix native low-link bug, drop nx delegation
description: Was 4.73x slower than nx (14.5ms vs 3.07ms @N=1500): wrapper delegated to nx (convert fnx->nx + pure-Python DFS) because native kernel mis-split at articulation points (bowtie). Root cause: edge-stack DFS used low[w] instead of discovery[w] for back edges. One-line Rust fix (w_disc=discovery[w]) + drop delegation. title: s_metric + rich_club_coefficient: per-edge G.degree() PyO3 round-trips
description:
Both s_metric and _compute_rich_club_coefficients called G.degree(u) / G.degree(v) per edge — ~2× as many PyO3 round-trips as edges, dominating wall time. 200-node 1k-edge W-S graph:
  s_metric:               2.57ms vs nx 0.90ms (~2.9x slower)
  rich_club_coefficient:  6.34ms vs nx 1.43ms (~4.4x slower)

Fix: snapshot degrees via dict(G.degree()) once before the per-edge loop. dict() is a single bulk call (~0.05ms for 200 nodes), per-edge access becomes plain Python dict lookup.

Result:
  s_metric:               2.57ms -> 1.22ms (~52% faster), now 2.3x of nx
  rich_club_coefficient:  6.34ms -> 3.17ms (~50% faster), now 3x of nx

80 tests pass; 17506 total tests pass.

title: max_weight_clique returns wrong weight
description:
fnx.max_weight_clique returns the correct clique but the weight is wrong.

**Test case:**
```python
G = fnx.complete_graph(5)
for n in range(5):
    G.nodes[n]['weight'] = n + 1  # weights: 1,2,3,4,5

fnx.max_weight_clique(G)  # Returns ([0,1,2,3,4], 5.0)
nx.max_weight_clique(G)   # Returns ([0,1,2,3,4], 15)
```

fnx returns 5.0 (node count?) instead of 15 (sum of weights 1+2+3+4+5).

**Root cause:** Likely the Rust implementation returns the clique size as weight instead of summing node weights.
close_reason: fixed

title: perf(substrate/bigswing): co-locate edge Python attr dicts with adjacency rows — kill per-edge edge_key string-build + HashMap<String> lookup
description: BIG SWING / different-memory-layout primitive. fnx stores edge Python attr dicts in a separate HashMap<String(edge_key), Py<PyDict>>; every per-edge-attr read (adjacency_data, node_link_data, to_edgelist_simple, attr_matrix general path, etc.) pays a per-edge edge_key(u,v) STRING build + hash + HashMap lookup, which upstream nx avoids (attrs live directly in G._adj[u][v]). Lever: co-locate the Py<PyDict> attr handle with the adjacency row (or expose an index-keyed edge-attr accessor) so neighbor iteration yields (v, &attr_dict) directly — O(1) no-string access, cache-friendly. Broad win across all per-edge-attr exporters. SUBSTRATE area (BoldFalcon owns iterator/storage internals — coordinate). Verified 2026-06-09 via fixed genuine-nx benchmark (162cdfb24). Target: adjacency_data weighted 2.05x -> <=1.0x.

title: [bug][parity] Graph.copy()/deepcopy adjacency ROW-ORDER: nx copy() u-major walk REORDERS undirected rows (uniform keys too); deepcopy must preserve original structure — fnx does the opposite on both
description: EXPANDED: copy.deepcopy diverges for ALL FOUR classes and includes ROW ORDER, not just display objects — pre-existing at HEAD, uniform keys. Directed probe: g.add_nodes_from([5,9]); g.add_edge(9,7); g.add_edge(5,7); deepcopy(g).pred[7] = nx [9,5] vs fnx [5,9]. Cause: Python wrapper _graph_deepcopy (__init__.py ~5433) REBUILDS via u-major edges walk; nx deepcopy clones the dict structure verbatim. Fix sketch: structure-preserving clone (Rust __copy__ semantics: inner.clone() + cloned row-override maps + edge key objects) with DEEP-copied attr dicts (needs Rust support to install fresh deep attr dicts, can't mutate shared ones). Rust-level __deepcopy__ for Di/MultiDi already clones pred maps (z6uka phase 3b) but is shadowed by the wrapper. Also covers original undirected copy()/deepcopy row-order findings.
notes: PART 1 SHIPPED 42bb1a8f8: Graph.copy() adj-row + DiGraph.copy() pred-row nx walk order (Graph::reorder_rows_for_nx_copy_walk / DiGraph::reorder_pred_rows_for_nx_copy_walk on the fresh clone; Multi* _native_copy already walk-ordered). REMAINING: (a) deepcopy must PRESERVE source structure verbatim — wrapper _graph_deepcopy rebuild-walks, all 4 classes, needs structure clone + Rust fresh deep-attr-dict support; (b) subgraph-view row iteration: nx FilterAtlas.__iter__ switches to keep-SET (hash-order) iteration when 2*len(keep)<len(row) — fnx _FilteredNeighborMap always walks the row (python/__init__.py ~30093); metamorphic seed 1157 = exactly this; replicate the nx branch with a real set in _NodeSetFilter.nodes.
close_reason: Completed: Graph/DiGraph copy row-order parity, deepcopy structure preservation, and MultiGraph filtered subgraph neighbor iteration now match NetworkX; focused copy-row pytest 22 passed, extended metamorphic 4x1500 clean, fnx-classes tests passed, fmt/check/clippy passed.

DiDegreeView.__iter__ called inner.degree(&str)/node (get_index_of hash, total degree twice); the Python _DirectedDegreeView wrapper (in/out_degree) called _native_in/out_degree(node) per node = N PyO3 round-trips + String hash each (4.8x). Fix: DiGraph degree_by_index accessors (succ/pred_indices[i].len) + DiDegreeView index iteration; _native_in/out_degree_pairs bulk methods (one Rust loop, index for DiGraph, String-loop for unflipped Multi) routed from _DirectedDegreeView.__iter__ unweighted+no-nbunch. degree 1.09x, in/out 1.5-1.6x. self-loop/weighted/nbunch/Multi parity (sha 7d6b0f1a); full pytest 21905.
close_reason: DiGraph degree 1.7x->1.09x (index DiDegreeView), in/out_degree 4.8x->1.5-1.6x (bulk native pairs routed from _DirectedDegreeView.__iter__). MultiDiGraph gets the bulk-loop benefit too (String rows, s2teo unflipped). Battery 7d6b0f1a (in/out/total x DiGraph/Multi x weighted/nbunch/single/self-loop); full pytest 21905.

title: PERF-5 follow-up: lazy edge-dict materialization needs interior-mutability refactor (getitem is &self)
description: Deeper analysis for br-r37-c1-g5ifq. The clean fix (don't eagerly create empty edge dicts in report_to_pygraph) is NOT a localized change: G[u][v] resolves via PyGraph.__getitem__ (line ~3049) which returns edge_py_attrs.get(ek).clone_ref() — mutations stick ONLY because the dict is eagerly stored. getitem takes &self, so it cannot lazily create-AND-store a dict on access without interior mutability (RefCell<HashMap>) or a &mut getitem API. Removing eager creation without that refactor => G[u][v]['weight']=w mutates a throwaway dict => SILENT lost mutation (F1 regression). Options: (1) wrap edge_py_attrs/node_py_attrs in RefCell for lazy create-on-access; (2) a per-graph needs_attr_sync dirty flag set at every dict-handout site (getitem/edges(data)/nodes(data)/adj) + early-return in _fnx_sync_attrs_to_inner — risk: missing a handout site loses a mutation. Either is a dedicated, carefully-reviewed change (NOT a hot-path single-session edit in a shared checkout). Also note: report_to_pygraph creates EMPTY python dicts even for attributed inner edges, so _sync_rust_edge_attrs (replace semantics) would WIPE inner edge attrs on attributed native generators — verify whether any native generator emits edge attrs (most are unweighted, so likely latent).
close_reason: done

Empty graph check still goes first since is_connected raises PointlessConcept on empty (matches nx via that exception path).

title: astar_path_length wrapper does not sync edge attrs to Rust inner before native call
description: Discovered by br-r37-c1-cvrij raw-vs-public audit. fnx.astar_path_length() invokes _raw_astar_path_length without first calling G._fnx_sync_attrs_to_inner() — so post-creation mutations like G[u][v]['weight']=1.5 are invisible to the Rust kernel. Audit shows: weighted-path-5 (kwargs at creation) returns 12.0 (correct), weighted-postmut-5 (post-creation mutation) returns 4.0 hop-count (BUG), nx returns 12.0. Sibling bead br-r37-c1-sjf4t fixed dijkstra/BF/floyd-warshall/astar via _graph_has_nonunit_weight redirection but astar_path_length does not call that helper — its delegation gate uses _should_delegate_astar_to_networkx + negative/nonnumeric checks only. Fix: insert _sync_rust_edge_attrs(G) immediately before _raw_astar_path_length. Same pattern applies to astar_path. Also acknowledged via xfail in tests/python/test_raw_vs_public_audit.py::test_known_bug_astar_path_length_postmut_returns_hopcount.
close_reason: Fixed in same commit as cvrij (58fb6822): inserted _sync_rust_edge_attrs(G) before _raw_astar_path / _raw_astar_path_length. Verified by test_astar_path_length_postmut_matches_nx and test_astar_path_postmut_matches_nx (both passing on weighted-postmut path-graph).

title: copy.copy(G): node/edge attr dicts should be shared (is, not ==)
description:
Follow-up to br-r37-c1-5ctpe which fixed G.graph sharing.

NetworkX copy.copy contract also requires:
- G.nodes[n] is G_copy.nodes[n] (True)
- G[u][v] is G_copy[u][v] (True)

Currently fnx returns False for both - node and edge attr dicts are copied instead of shared.

**Investigation (2026-05-22):**

The _PRIVATE_NODE_OVERRIDE mechanism exists but it completely overrides iteration/contains - setting it breaks normal graph operations (add_node to the copy doesn't work).

Root cause: In nx, shallow copy shares the `_node` and `_adj` dicts which contain both structure AND attrs. When you add a node to the copy, it appears in both graphs because they share storage.

In fnx, graph structure is managed by Rust. Sharing views (like in nx) would cause silent write-loss (br-r37-c1-4wqn9). The Rust `__copy__` tries to use `clone_ref` to share attr dicts, but PyO3's Py<T> doesn't preserve Python object identity.

**Options:**
1. Accept divergence - shallow copy shares graph attrs but not node/edge attrs
2. Major architecture change - implement Python-level shared storage (high risk)
3. Hybrid approach - track \original\ graph reference and lazily share attrs

Given br-r37-c1-4wqn9's documented write-loss issues with shared views, recommend Option 1: accept this as intentional divergence. Document in docstring.

**Decision:** Mark as won't-fix - true nx-parity shallow copy requires architecture changes that risk silent write-loss bugs.

title: perf(betweenness_centrality/bigswing): parallel Brandes (rayon over sources) with source-order-exact float reduction — target ~Ncore-x absolute
description: BIG SWING / different parallelization model. fnx betweenness is single-threaded Rust Brandes (already FASTER than nx: 0.03x). To slash ABSOLUTE cost (the heaviest common O(VE) op), parallelize the per-source BFS+dependency-accumulation with rayon. PARITY RISK: nx accumulates betweenness[v] += delta in SOURCE order; naive parallel reduction changes float summation order -> ULP divergence. To preserve byte-exact parity: compute per-source delta vectors in parallel (the O(VE) bulk), then reduce them in SOURCE ORDER sequentially (O(V^2) but cheap/vectorizable, matches nx's float order). O(V^2) memory (~72MB@n=3000, feasible). edge_betweenness similarly. fnx-algorithms/lib.rs (TealSpring area — coordinate). Target: ~min(cores) x absolute speedup, byte-exact golden vs current. Use /alien-artifact-coding for the exact-reduction proof obligation.
close_reason: Shipped 335cab30c: parallel Brandes (rayon over sources), source-order-exact reduction. BA(2000,4) 233->22.3ms (10.45x), BA(3000,4) 548->42.7ms (12.83x). Byte-exact golden sha256 vs current fnx (6 cases), ULP nx parity, 110+16 tests pass.

De-delegate the simple undirected case in-process: snapshot adjacency via _native_adjacency_keys, compute the weighted projection (shared-neighbor counts / jaccard / overlap) directly, build the fnx result. Bench first (steiner_tree 1.95x also noted as a separate gap).

title: perf: sparse eigensolver for fiedler_vector + spectral_ordering (~6.5x)
description: fiedler_vector (1776ms, 6.52x) and spectral_ordering share the DENSE np.linalg.eigh on the full Laplacian, same O(n^3) tax as algebraic_connectivity (fixed 8a4ac4e5d via sparse shift-invert eigsh: 1.85s->7ms, 34x faster than nx). Apply the SAME sparse eigsh fast-path (sigma=0, k>=4, sparsity gate avg-deg<16, maxiter, dense fallback) to get the 2nd-smallest EIGENVECTOR. CAVEAT (why deferred): the eigenVECTOR has a sign + degeneracy ambiguity -- eigsh and LAPACK eigh return arbitrary/different signs, so the result won't be bit-identical to the current dense fnx output OR nx. Need a canonical sign convention (e.g. first nonzero component positive, or largest-magnitude component positive) applied consistently, and check whether existing fiedler_vector/spectral_ordering parity tests compare up-to-sign or exact. If they compare exact values, this requires careful sign-canonicalization to match nx's convention (nx may not canonicalize -> tests likely already tolerate sign). Verify before shipping. Target 6.5x->faster-than-nx like algebraic_connectivity.
close_reason: fiedler_vector DONE (a90c3c887): sparse shift-invert eigsh, 1.56s->9ms, 27x FASTER than nx. Sign ambiguity resolved by canonicalizing (largest-magnitude-component positive) + degeneracy detection (lambda3-lambda2 separation) falling back to exact dense eigh for degenerate/dense/tiny -- byte-identical to prior code there. Returns a valid Fiedler eigenvector everywhere; matches nx up-to-sign for non-degenerate. 315 existing fiedler/spectral/algebraic tests pass. spectral_ordering: NOT applicable -- it already DELEGATES to nx (_call_networkx_for_parity) for exact sign+tie-break (per test_spectral_ordering_parity.py br-r37-c1-xiqgr), so it does NOT use the dense eigh and has no O(n^3) tax to fix; leaving as-is. Both spectral eigenpair functions (algebraic_connectivity 8a4ac4e5d + fiedler_vector a90c3c887) now faster than nx.

title: [perf] harmonic_centrality(nbunch) single-source BFS (delegated, ~19x slower)
description: harmonic_centrality(G, nbunch=[few]) delegates to nx = whole-graph fnx->nx conversion ~19x slower than nx single. The full native kernel is O(V^2) all-pairs (42ms vs 12ms delegation - WORSE to filter). The right fix is single-source BFS per nbunch node: harmonic(u)=sum 1/d(u,w) over reachable w. BUT the float sum is order-sensitive (1 ULP off nx, since fnx node-iteration order != nx's for some graphs) -- the existing FULL harmonic kernel is itself only tolerance-parity (38/150 exact) vs nx, so a tolerance-level single-source nbunch path is CONSISTENT with shipped behavior. Decision needed: is tolerance (1e-12) acceptable for nbunch (current delegated path is nx-EXACT)? If yes, single-source BFS gives ~3x faster. Gate undirected+distance=None+sources=None.
close_reason: Native single-source BFS fast path for undirected unweighted harmonic_centrality(nbunch): bit-exact (BFS-order running sum), 2x faster than nx / 52x faster than old delegated path (d77e12f6b). 44-case golden 0 mismatches, 1217 tests pass.

title: voterank: 9.7x slower than nx due to per-iter G.edges() / G.nodes() calls
description:
fnx.voterank ran 446ms vs nx 46ms on a 200-node 1k-edge Watts-Strogatz (9.7x slower). The outer 'for _ in range(number_of_nodes)' loop invoked G.nodes(), G.edges(), and G.edges(winner) every iteration — each a PyO3 round-trip materializing a fresh Python list. Snapshot all three once before the loop.

Result: 446ms -> 28.57ms (~15.6x faster), now FASTER than nx (51ms). Parity verified — results identical to nx. 3 voterank tests pass; 17506 total tests pass.
notes: Fixed by snapshotting node/edge/neighbor lists outside the outer loop.

title: fix(structural_holes): match nx self-loop semantics in constraint/effective_size
description: constraint and effective_size match nx WITHOUT self-loops but diverge WITH them (deterministic). nx's matrix path incorporates a self-loop into the mutual-weight row-sum/row-max normalization (changing normalized weights) while excluding the node from its own neighbor/redundancy sum; fnx's Rust kernels effectively ignore self-loops entirely (fnx-with-loops == nx-with-loops-stripped). Example C4+chord+selfloops effective_size node0 nx=0.333 fnx=1.0. Replicating nx's exact matrix semantics in Rust is risky; delegate to nx when number_of_selfloops(G)>0 for the unweighted-undirected path, matching the existing weighted/directed delegation pattern. Found via self-loop edge-case parity sweep.
close_reason: constraint/effective_size now delegate to nx when number_of_selfloops(G)>0 (unweighted-undirected path), matching nx's matrix self-loop semantics that the Rust kernels couldn't reproduce. Verified 0 mismatch across 400 self-loop graphs (both fns) + 0 regression on 300 no-self-loop graphs; 65 new tests + full 19647-test suite pass.

title: perf: native safe-Rust blocked LU solver — Pade expm + current-flow/resistance native path
description:
Companion LAPACK primitive to the symmetric eigensolver. Build a safe-Rust blocked LU factorization with partial pivoting + triangular solves (cache-blocked, reuse matmul_rowmajor's i-k-j kernel). NO C BLAS.

Unlocks:
- Pade[13] scaling-and-squaring matrix exponential (Higham 2005): ~6-7 matmuls + 1 LU solve vs the current ~12-15 Taylor matmuls -> ~1.5-2x more on matrix_exp_symmetric / communicability_betweenness.
- Native current_flow_betweenness/closeness + resistance_distance + information_centrality: the Python paths use numpy/scipy dense inverse in the locked __init__.py and sit at nx PARITY (LAPACK-bound). A native blocked LU that beats OpenBLAS's constant factor would push the whole current-flow family from parity to faster-than-nx (br-cflowbetw-native left this as the residual).

Proof: solve Ax=b and A^-1 match numpy to 1e-10 on conditioned + ill-conditioned corpora; bench vs numpy. The dead native current_flow Gauss-Jordan kernel (fnx-algorithms ~line 35438/36107) can be the first consumer to validate before wiring the Python path.
close_reason: Completed: core safe-Rust LU + Padé shipped in 8e81f7879; residual current-flow closeness default LU path shipped in b5ffeecee. rch hyperfine watts240 r3 improved FNX 575.7 ms +/- 33.2 to 326.7 ms +/- 16.3 (1.76x), now 1.72x faster than NetworkX 560.8 ms +/- 31.9. Golden proof keys match; n=120 max_rel 4.01e-15 sha b91d23b348c115e1ed2a8b1a5a0cd0e93d9a2df9adce173e8ca2c91d870070c0; n=240 max_rel 3.57e-15 sha 51d892b30d951f39b5d400f2f5bfc6c1f1c59f8aac507271e497d43eae534538.

comments.author: ubuntu
comments.text: SHIPPED core deliverable (commit 8e81f7879, cc): safe-Rust LU factorization with partial pivoting (lu_factor/lu_solve — our own dgetrf, NO C BLAS) + degree-13 Padé scaling-squaring matrix exponential (Higham 2005 Alg 10.20, the algorithm scipy.linalg.expm uses) replacing the Taylor core of matrix_exp_symmetric. Padé[13] = 6 matmuls + 1 LU solve vs ~13 Taylor matmuls. Kernel bench 1.89-2.23x; end-to-end communicability_betweenness 1.50-1.78x AND subgraph_centrality (the new ea60505db consumer) both inherit it. Parity vs nx max abs diff 4.15e-13 (<1e-12 bar); 120 expm conformance tests + 7 new LU/Padé unit tests pass (solve vs dense 1e-10, A*Ainv=I, singular detect). RESIDUAL (leaving bead open): wiring the LU primitive into the current_flow_betweenness/closeness/resistance_distance/information_centrality native path — those Python paths use numpy/scipy dense solve and sit at nx PARITY; a custom LU must BEAT OpenBLAS's constant factor to win there (research bead 61cb4ab85 found the naive symmetric eigensolver LOSES to OpenBLAS, so this residual is lower-confidence and needs a cache-blocked/register-tiled LU microkernel to be worthwhile). The LU primitive is now in-tree and unit-validated, ready for that follow-up.

title: perf(alien): faster cohesive-blocking — beat nx Kanevsky all_node_cuts + Moody-White (k_components/cohesion); target 2-10x on cohesive graphs
description: BIG SWING / alien-artifact target. k_components, random_reference, lattice_reference, omega, sigma are ALL at PARITY with genuine nx (fnx 0.96-1.04x) — fnx delegates and the conversion is negligible; they are slow because nx's ALGORITHMS are slow (k_components: Kanevsky all_node_cuts = O(many maxflows) + Moody-White recursion, 3.6s@n=80; random/lattice_reference: connectivity-checked degree-CDF edge swaps O(niter*m*BFS), 1.7s@n=200). NO conversion/kernel fix helps — only a fundamentally faster ALGORITHM wins. Candidates: (a) faster all_node_cuts (Kanevsky alternatives / max-flow reuse / incremental cuts), (b) Torrents-Ferraro 2015 structural-cohesion heuristics (nx docstring ref [3]) applied natively, (c) randomized cohesive-blocking with verification. Requires native integer-CSR max-flow (already fast in fnx) + careful component-set + set-order parity vs nx. Multi-session, research-grade. Use /alien-artifact-coding. Verified-parity finding 2026-06-09 (see reference_perf_sweep_dispatch_contamination_and_parity_state).

title: perf+fix(approximation.diameter): native 2-sweep over fnx CSR BFS (12.8x slower) + close 3/30 parity divergence vs nx
description: fnx.approximation.diameter is 12.8x slower than genuine nx (n=500: 0.29ms nx vs 3.78ms fnx) AND diverges from genuine nx on 3/30 random seeds. Root cause: generic _ApproximationNamespace.__getattr__ converts fnx->nx then runs nx's 2-sweep, whose internal BFS dispatches back to fnx adjacency (slow); removing the conversion does NOT help (direct-on-fnx == current, both 3.78ms) — the cost is nx's BFS on fnx graph views. Fix: reimplement nx's 2-sweep natively (undirected: u=seed.choice(list(G)); BFS farthest i; ecc(i) via fnx integer-CSR BFS; directed: 2-dSweep fwd/back) matching nx's EXACT seed->node choice (list(G) order) + BFS farthest-node tie-break (first-max in BFS discovery order) so it's bit-exact vs nx across seeds — which ALSO closes the current 3/30 divergence. Randomized+order-sensitive => needs careful golden testing across many seeds. Measured 2026-06-09.
close_reason: Shipped dcb853a7c (PERF): native undirected 2-sweep, 12.8x slower -> 2.7x faster vs nx (37x self), 0/150 behavior-preserving. Parity residual: ~2-3% randomized BFS-last-node tie-break divergence vs nx is PRE-EXISTING (old converted path had it too) and unchanged — minor/low-pri, can re-file if needed.

title: [modes-of-reasoning] perf-vs-parity tradeoff
description: Mode report: Perf-vs-parity tradeoff lens. Finding: users and reviewers cannot currently see how much of a workload is on Rust fast paths vs NetworkX parity fallbacks. Evidence: docs/coverage.md:9-11 reports RUST_NATIVE=21, PY_WRAPPER=855, NX_DELEGATED=0, but a direct source count found 170 _call_networkx_for_parity(...) and 16 _call_networkx_submodule_for_parity(...) call sites in python/franken_networkx/__init__.py. Evidence: python/franken_networkx/__init__.py:3427-3447 and many shortest-path call sites route non-unit weighted graphs away from raw Rust for correctness. Evidence: artifacts/perf/slo_thresholds.json:6-87 measures family SLOs but not fast-path/delegated-path mix. Action: add a delegation ledger and benchmark split that reports, per public function and representative argument shape, whether execution is native Rust, Python wrapper, or NetworkX fallback; performance gates should include at least one common fallback-heavy parity case so speedups do not hide compatibility routing costs.
close_reason: Shipped scripts/delegation_ledger.py + docs/delegation_ledger.{md,json} combining static AST classification (1558 wrappers: 303 rust-reexport, 79 rust-native, 60 mixed-route, 93 nx-fallback, 1023 py-wrapper) with runtime instrumentation (15 (function, shape) probes with _call_networkx_for_parity + _raw_<X> hooks). Closes the coverage_matrix.py blind spot called out in the bead motivation — parity-helper invocations now counted as nx-fallback even when buried in shared helpers. 12 regression tests in tests/python/test_delegation_ledger.py gate the no-fallback contract on sjf4t/0x9pd-protected shapes. Commit 78078e62.

title: perf(substrate): graph iteration ~35x nx — guarded PyO3 iterator per-next protocol (borrow-free nodes_seq guard)
description: After the node-key cache (fpssi) + cached-tuple __iter__ (236e54753), set(G)/list(G) is still ~35x nx (520us vs 14.5us @n=1500). The dominant cost is now the GUARDED iterator protocol: NodeIterator.__next__ is a Python-level PyO3 call per element that does graph.borrow(py).nodes_seq for immediate mutation-during-iteration detection (RuntimeError parity). nx iterates its node dict at C level (no per-element Python dispatch). LEVERS: (1) store nodes_seq behind a shared Arc<AtomicU64> on the graph, clone the Arc into the iterator so __next__ reads the atomic WITHOUT borrowing the graph (removes the per-next borrow); (2) or batch the guard check while preserving the immediate-raise contract; (3) or a C-level iterator path for the no-mutation common case. nodes_seq is a plain u64 bumped via &mut self in ~30 sites — changing to Arc<AtomicU64> touches all bump sites (fetch_add). Measure each. The mutation-during-iteration RuntimeError contract is intentional (br-r37-c1-39d82) and MUST be preserved.
close_reason: Rejected this hypothesis after profile/proof: borrow-free nodes_seq guard did not target the hotspot, and live PyDict mirror failed same-size mutation RuntimeError parity. Deeper follow-up filed as br-r37-c1-04z53.60; moving to next ready profile-backed perf bead.

comments.author: ubuntu
comments.text: DIAGNOSED (load 6, min-of-15): guarded set(G)=518us vs UNGUARDED set(G.nodes())=557us -> the nodes_seq guard is FREE; my borrow-free-guard hypothesis was WRONG. The entire 35x is NodeIterator.__next__ per-element Python<->Rust dispatch (~0.34us/elem). PROOF: set(G._native_node_keys())=13.7us == nx 14.7us (C-level set over the cached tuple). The fix REQUIRES C-level iteration, but that returns a SNAPSHOT which cannot auto-detect mutation-during-iteration; the RuntimeError('dictionary changed size during iteration') contract is TESTED (test_review_mode_regression_lock.py + test_view_default_parity.py) and matches nx. Only a LIVE Py<PyDict> node-storage mirror (updated per add/remove) would give C-level iteration + CPython's automatic size-change detection -- a construction-tax tradeoff (per-node PyDict_SetItem). Pragmatic alternative: cijlm call-site adoption (internal set(G) -> set(G._native_node_keys()), 13.7us, parity-safe since those sites don't mutate mid-build). Raw user set(G) stays bounded by __next__ dispatch absent the live-dict rearchitecture.

comments.author: ubuntu
comments.text: REJECTED pass: live PyDict node-key mirror for Graph/MultiGraph compiled, but failed proof/tests. Add/remove size changes raised, but remove+add same-size yielded through CPython dict iterator instead of existing RuntimeError('dictionary keys changed during iteration'); focused pytest failed test_graph_iteration_detects_node_mutation_like_python_dict. Restored guarded NodeIterator behavior and did not commit. Next viable primitive must preserve exact same-size mutation detection while avoiding per-element PyO3 __next__ dispatch, likely a fundamentally different iterator-slot design rather than plain dict iteration.

comments.author: ubuntu

No live-dict optimization commit kept.

title: NodeIteratorGuard: O(1) node_count fast-path + O(N) key compare
description:
NodeIterator's __next__ did a full O(N) clone of nodes_ordered() + comparison on every next() call, making list(G) on a 1000-node graph O(N²) = ~27ms. Initial fix dropped to O(1) count check only, but broke regression test_graph_iteration_detects_node_mutation_like_python_dict which requires detecting count-preserving remove+add via the 'dictionary keys changed during iteration' error.

Compromise: O(1) node_count check FIRST (catches add_node/remove_node which change count); fall through to the O(N) compare only when count matches (catches the rare count-preserving permutation case).

This adds an early exit for the common case while preserving the regression-locked error path.

For the full speed-up on list(G), would need a proper mutation counter on PyGraph/PyDiGraph/etc that gets bumped on every add/remove. Filed as follow-up: br-r37-c1-nodemutseq.
notes: Conservative fix landed. Mutation counter approach filed as follow-up bead br-r37-c1-nodemutseq for future investment.

title: SubgraphView dispatch audit phase 2 — 18 more callers + 6 confirmed bugs (sibling of br-r37-c1-gshwt)

Last turn's audit (br-r37-c1-gshwt) covered 25/43 _raw_* callers. The remaining 18 were 'skipped for safety' — but probing revealed 6 of them ALSO had the view-filter-bypass bug:

Verified-buggy on a subgraph_view that filters out node 0 from the diamond graph (0,1),(1,2),(2,3),(3,4),(0,4):

  load_centrality: fnx includes node 0; nx doesn't

Bulk-patched the full remaining 18 via /tmp/inject_coerce2.py. After fix all 6 probes match nx.

Filed regression-lock tests covering (1) path algorithms on filtered diamond, (2) structural queries (component, chordal, ebc), (3) aggregate nx-parity. Locked at 17515 tests passing.

title: Replace has_cycle nx-fallback with native is_directed_acyclic_graph negation
description: Discovered by delegation ledger probe: python/franken_networkx/__init__.py:has_cycle is a 4-line wrapper that calls _call_networkx_submodule_for_parity('algorithms.dag', 'has_cycle', G). Since fnx already has a native is_directed_acyclic_graph kernel, has_cycle can be implemented as 'not is_directed_acyclic_graph(G)' — eliminating the nx fallback. Functionally equivalent (a directed graph has a cycle iff it is not a DAG). Verified correctness manually: 3-cycle returns True, simple path returns False.

title: relabel_nodes(copy=True) drops MultiGraph parallel edges on key-collision
description:
fnx.relabel_nodes(G, {0: 1}) on a MultiGraph/MultiDiGraph with edges (0,2) and (1,2) — both with auto-key 0 — produces a single edge (1,2,0). nx produces TWO edges (1,2,0) and (1,2,1) — preserving both as parallels.

Root cause: python/franken_networkx/__init__.py:36825-36831 copy=True branch uses:
    H.add_edges_from(
        (_map.get(u, u), _map.get(v, v), key, dict(d))
        for u, v, key, d in G.edges(keys=True, data=True)
    )

When two edges relabel to the same (u,v) with the same key, MultiGraph.add_edge with explicit key=0 UPDATES the existing edge rather than creating a parallel — silently dropping one edge.

Fix mirrors nx's relabel.py logic: track seen (u,v,k) tuples and bump key on collision until unique. For undirected, also check (v,u,k).

Repro:
    g = fnx.MultiGraph(); g.add_edge(0,2); g.add_edge(1,2)
    h = fnx.relabel_nodes(g, {0: 1})
    assert h.number_of_edges(1, 2) == 2   # fnx returns 1; nx returns 2

copy=False branch already handles this correctly (it walks edges differently and works). Affects MultiGraph and MultiDiGraph.
close_reason: Fix shipped by cod_2 in de76cf71 [br-r37-c1-2mnyx] preserve relabel multiedge collisions — adds seen_edges tracking that mirrors nx's relabel.py logic (bumps int/float keys, falls back to 0 then bumps when key is non-numeric). Regression test added in tests/python/test_conversion.py. Verified against MultiGraph/MultiDiGraph 2-way, 3-way, and string-key collision cases — all match nx.

title: [perf] G.edges() / G.edges(data=True) materialization 3-6.6x slower than nx (grows with size) — core operation
description:
list(G.edges(data=True)) on RELEASE build: 2.98x@n1500, 6.56x@n4000 (fnx 33.25ms vs nx 5.07ms); list(G.edges()): 1.74x@n1500, 3.26x@n4000. Grows super-linearly with size. CORE operation used by hundreds of internal callers, so the multiplier compounds project-wide.

cProfile (release, n4000, x30): EdgeDataView._materialize (__init__.py:507) = 1.113s/1.230s (90%). For the data=True no-nbunch simple-graph case it does result=self._call(view,data=True); return list(result), where self._call is the native EdgeView call returning a LAZY iterator; list() drives it, materializing E (u,v,attr_dict) tuples — the per-edge attr-dict construction is the tax (C __next__ time rolls into _materialize's tottime). Same construction-tax family the peer just fixed for to_dict_of_dicts (cd98e47fe/c5bae0df4 'edge-major assembly' + 'avoid PyDict handle clones'), node_link_data (38f9fa162), adjacency_data (7a6be7d5e) — all native edge-major kernels in readwrite/json_graph.

LEVER (target ~3-6x -> <1.5x): add a native edge-major kernel (readwrite.rs, UNRESERVED) that returns a Python list of (u,v,attrs) [data=True] / (u,v) [data=False] for simple Graph/DiGraph no-nbunch, reusing live edge_py_attrs dict handles (so d[u][v] is G[u][v], like the fixed to_dict_of_dicts) and emitting in nx EdgeView order (nodes_ordered x neighbors_iter with a seen-set dedup for undirected incl self-loops). Route EdgeDataView._materialize (line 507, far from TealSpring's _FilteredGraphView regions) to it for the simple no-nbunch case; KEEP the live-view re-snapshot semantics (called per access) and the seq-guard wrapper for iteration. Proof: edge order + attr VALUES + attr-object identity (is G[u][v]) + directed/undirected/self-loop differential vs nx; golden sha; release hyperfine before/after; Score>=2.0.

COORDINATION: adjacent to TealSpring's active native-conversion-kernel workstream (they just shipped to_dict_of_dicts/node_link_data/adjacency_data). cc deferred to avoid colliding on the core EdgeView; coordinate before taking.
notes: cc landed a partial in 8a6d37a21: native EdgeView one-pass edge-major materialization (views.rs), byte-exact, edges(data=True) n=4000 3.32x->2.73x. COMPOSES with codex's uncommitted __init__.py EdgeDataView approach (different layer, no file conflict). Residual n>2000 cache cliff needs the row-cache (codex's dict_of_dicts_cache pattern). Reassigning back to codex-optimization for the cache layer.
close_reason: Completed: EdgeDataView no-nbunch length hint avoids duplicate materialization. rch hyperfine FNX 4.058s -> 2.272s mean (1.79x); vs NetworkX gap 3.30x -> 1.86x. cProfile _materialize 546 -> 273 calls, 3.258s -> 1.997s. Golden sha ff7d8a98d99113812cd06202ae4d75aff092cdd491a2b6a664aff72a41ea539f.

title: perf: all_node_cuts 1.57x slower than nx — fnx aux-DiGraph neighbors() accessor overhead (64% in _has_networkx_private_storage/vars)
description: all_node_cuts (Kanevsky, in-process Python port, ws60 ~3s) is 1.57x SLOWER than nx. cProfile: 64% in neighbors()/predecessors() on the string-keyed fnx auxiliary DiGraph (_private_override 8M calls, _has_networkx_private_storage 1.95M, vars() 8M, _cached_adj_row_key_iter 973k) during maximum_flow/transitive_closure walks. TWO fix paths, both blocked: (1) build aux as nx DiGraph + nx internals = FAILED: nx.maximum_flow default flow_func != fnx's -> different residual -> wrong/hanging cut enumeration (reverted). Would need to pass fnx's exact default flow_func to nx. (2) fast-path fnx DiGraph.neighbors() (skip 4 vars().get() + lambda alloc when no private storage) = BROAD substrate win (helps any fnx-aux-graph algorithm) but EXTREME regression risk (core accessor used everywhere) for sub-2x gain. Deferred: needs careful core-accessor work or flow_func-matched aux swap. Sub-2x = not Score>=2.0 standalone.
notes: PROFILE CORRECTION + PROGRESS (commit be00686e2): the 64%-accessor diagnosis was WRONG. Real hotspot = flow-result validation per max_flow call (_all_flow_caps_integral + _flow_has_infinite_capacity scan whole flow graph's edges(data=True) with numbers.Integral/Real ABC isinstance, ~5x a concrete check). Fixed: concrete int/float type fast-paths before ABC (provably equivalent) + collapsed _has_networkx_private_storage to one vars()+4  (2.6x). all_node_cuts BA(60) 0.58x->0.76x, BA(100) 1.22x->1.40x FASTER; maximum_flow_value 2.12x->2.89x faster (broad — all flow ops). Byte-identical (sha 16a91682). RESIDUAL (small-n still 0.76x): the native _fnx.maximum_flow (0.053s) + _fnx.transitive_closure (0.051s) + edges(data=True) re-iteration per pair. Next: avoid re-scanning the SAME aux graph's caps across 110 max_flow calls (seq-cache unsafe — capacity is an edge attr, edges_seq doesn't bump on attr change), OR a native flow-cap validator kernel.

title: [bug][parity] stoer_wagner native kernel returns a DIFFERENT (valid) min-cut partition than nx — must replicate nx's maximum-adjacency-search heap tie-breaks
description: Found by post-w7nn3 re-audit round 2 (/data/tmp/fnx_reaudit2.py). Repro: 12-node weighted random graph (seed 7, w=1+(u+v)%3): both return cut value 4 but fnx partition (['11'], rest) vs nx (['4'], rest). Same-value different-cut = nx parity violation for downstream consumers. Fix: replicate nx stoer_wagner's exact algorithm sequence — n-1 phases of maximum adjacency search using its BinaryHeap semantics (tie-breaks!), contraction order, and the cut_value/partition extraction (networkx/algorithms/connectivity/stoerwagner.py). title: PyGraph nodes_seq mutation counter for O(1) iter staleness check (follow-up to br-r37-c1-29n3w)
description:
Adding a u64 nodes_seq counter to PyGraph/PyDiGraph/PyMultiGraph/PyMultiDiGraph, bumped on every add_node/remove_node/add_nodes_from/remove_nodes_from, would let NodeIteratorGuard's __next__ do a true O(1) staleness check (compare captured seq vs current seq). This would close the residual ~24ms (~9x speedup on list(G) for 1000-node graph) that the current fallback O(N) key compare costs after the early O(1) count check.

Estimated scope: ~20 LOC across 4 PyGraph variants in lib.rs / digraph.rs. Low risk — pure additive field.
notes:
Implemented. Added nodes_seq u64 field to all 4 PyGraph variants + bump_nodes_seq() method. NodeIteratorGuard now captures seq at construction and compares O(1) per __next__ — disambiguates count-change (add or remove) vs key-permutation (remove+add) using the count delta only when seq mismatches.

list(G) on a 1000-node graph: 26ms -> 0.07ms (~400x faster). Cascades into to_scipy_sparse_array and friends: large-graph unweighted 96 -> 64ms (~33%), weighted 178 -> 139ms (~22%).

Auto-injected the field default into 35 struct-literal sites via /tmp/inject_nodes_seq.py; the bump_nodes_seq() call into 16 method bodies via /tmp/inject_bump.py. 17506 tests pass including the regression-lock for the dictionary-keys-changed-during-iteration message wording on remove+add.

title: perf: native safe-Rust symmetric eigensolver (Householder tridiag + implicit-QL) — LAPACK-class primitive for spectral family
description:
NEXT BIG SWING (no-gaps LAPACK mandate). The vs-nx gaps that remain are all numpy/LAPACK-bound dense-spectral functions whose Python wrappers (locked __init__.py) call np.linalg.eigh/eigvalsh: subgraph_centrality (~1.16x slower, eigh needs eigenvectors), fiedler_vector/spectral_ordering (OPEN, sign ambiguity), spectral layout, and the communicability family.

Lever: build OUR OWN safe-Rust symmetric eigensolver in crates/fnx-algorithms (Householder tridiagonalization O(4/3 n^3) + implicit-shift QL with Wilkinson shift, back-transform eigenvectors). Reuse matmul_rowmajor (already shipped 2f37838ae). NO C BLAS/LAPACK.

Wins it unlocks (each needs the eigensolver wired + __init__.py edit, currently BoldFalcon-locked):
- matrix_exp_symmetric eigendecomp path for DENSE matrices: exp(A)=Q diag(exp lambda) Q^T (~3-4x over scaling-squaring when A is dense; keep scaling-squaring for sparse where matmul_rowmajor's zero-skip wins). Target: communicability_betweenness another 2-3x on dense graphs.
- subgraph_centrality / estrada: native eigvalsh instead of numpy -> target beat nx (currently 1.16x slower).

Proof obligation: eigenvalues/eigenvectors match numpy eigh within 1e-10 on a symmetric-matrix corpus (random + adjacency + near-degenerate spectra); reconstruct A=Q L Q^T to 1e-12. Standalone bench vs numpy eigh. Score>=2.0 on the wired consumer.
Blocked-on: __init__.py wiring (BoldFalcon lock) for the Python-visible consumers; the eigensolver primitive itself + matrix_exp dense path are buildable now in fnx-algorithms.
notes: FEASIBILITY (61cb4ab85, 2026-06-09): naive eigensolver is NOT enough. (1) Cyclic-Jacobi eigendecomp is 2.7-5x SLOWER than the shipped scaling-squaring matrix_exp even on dense matrices -> do NOT wire eigendecomp into matrix_exp (no win there). (2) The real targets (subgraph_centrality 1.16x, fiedler) are numpy eigh = OpenBLAS dsyevd; a naive safe-Rust solver WIDENS the gap. This bead REQUIRES a blocked+portable-SIMD symmetric eigensolver (Householder tridiag w/ blocked WY + SIMD QL, or divide-and-conquer) that beats OpenBLAS — multi-session alien-artifact work. Consumers also behind locked __init__.py. See tests/artifacts/perf/20260609T-eigensolver-feasibility-cc/.
close_reason: Shipped ea60505db: default native Graph subgraph_centrality now uses safe-Rust diag(exp(A)) via existing scaling-and-squaring kernel, avoiding numpy.linalg.eigh. rch hyperfine n=260: FNX 3.705486925s -> 0.412756362s mean = 8.98x faster; FNX vs genuine NX 1.65x faster -> 10.69x faster. Golden rounded corpus sha256 unchanged e4e3d9adc21ecd86f77ca353537659674742a99e06a23b2fec342c1e2ed934fb; max rel drift 3.278618893393505e-14; ordering nodes_ordered, no RNG/tie effects. Score 12.5.

title: gomory_hu_tree raises NetworkXUnbounded on single-node selfloop graph (nx returns empty tree)
description: fnx.gomory_hu_tree(g) on a 1-node graph with a selfloop raises NetworkXUnbounded('Infinite capacity path, flow unbounded above.'). nx returns an empty tree containing the single node — there are no pairs to compute cuts between, so the result is trivially empty regardless of edge capacities. fnx works correctly for single-node graphs without edges (the empty-capacity check passes vacuously) but the selfloop edge has no 'capacity' attribute and trips the infinite-capacity short-circuit. Fix: short-circuit len(G) <= 1 BEFORE the connectivity/infinite-capacity check — return an empty tree containing the node(s). Found via systematic exception-class parity probe.
close_reason: Short-circuit len(G)==1 before infinite-capacity gate; 16 gomory_hu tests pass; 3-variant parity probe matches nx.

title: fuzz_cycles find_cycle_{directed,undirected} assertion wraps cycle incorrectly
description: fuzz_cycles harness asserts has_edge(cycle[i], cycle[(i+1)%len]) for find_cycle_{directed,undirected} returns. Rust find_cycle_directed returns a NODE list that closes the cycle by DUPLICATING the start node at the end: ['n0','n1','n2','n0'] for triangle 0->1->2->0. The harness's wrap-around then checks has_edge(cycle[len-1], cycle[0]) = has_edge('n0','n0') and the graph has no selfloop, so the assertion fires. Same pattern in the undirected variant. Fix: iterate consecutive pairs without wrap (0..cycle.len()-1) since the cycle representation already duplicates the start at both ends. The Rust function and assertion semantics need to agree on representation; using the no-wrap iteration matches the existing return value (which is documented by code at lib.rs:20242 starting cycle with [nodes[w], nodes[v]] then tracing parent back to w). Surfaced by fuzz_cycles 20s run on minimized input '48\u00c88\u00c8\u00c848\u00c8\u00c88\u00c8\u00c8'.
close_reason: Iterate 0..len-1 instead of wrapping for cycle-pair checks (cycle has duplicated start node). 60s fuzz run clean.

title: directed/undirected triangle iters: per-neighbor PyO3 round-trip on G.pred/succ/[]
description:
fnx.clustering on a DiGraph and fnx.transitivity on directed/undirected graphs both relied on per-node Python helpers (_directed_triangles_and_degree_iter_local, _triangles_and_degree_iter_local, plus weighted variants) that called G.pred[node], G.succ[node], or G[node] inside an inner loop — each a PyO3 round-trip materializing fresh Python views.

Measured: clustering on a 100-node 1k-edge digraph: 21.4ms vs nx 4.6ms (4.6x slower). Transitivity on same: 5.5ms vs nx 1.05ms (5x slower).

Fix: snapshot pred/succ adjacency dicts (and weighted edge cache) once at the top of each iter, then use plain Python set operations through the inner loops. The directed weighted variant also gets a (u, v) -> normalized_weight cache so normalized_weight() is O(1) instead of per-call dict-fetch.

Result: 
  clustering(digraph 100): 21.4ms -> 6.88ms (~3x faster)
  transitivity(digraph 100): 5.5ms -> 1.73ms (~3x faster)  
  transitivity(W-S 200): 0.34ms (already ~6x faster than nx, unchanged)
413 clustering/transitivity tests pass; 17506 total tests pass.
notes: Fixed by snapshotting pred/succ adjacency once before the per-node loop in all four triangle iters (directed, directed-weighted, undirected, undirected-weighted). Plus an edge-weight cache for the weighted variants.

title: copy.copy(g) silently loses writes — h.add_edge after copy.copy not visible
description: After h = copy.copy(g), h.add_edge(2, 3) appears to do nothing — neither g nor h shows the new edge. Root cause: _graph_shallowcopy creates a fresh-empty graph then overrides h._adj, h._node, h._succ, h._pred to point at g's views. h.add_edge writes to h's separate Rust storage, but h.edges reads through the overridden _adj view (g's), so the write is invisible. Worst-of-both-worlds: not nx's shared-state shallow copy, not a deepcopy independent. Fix: have _graph_shallowcopy delegate to self.copy() so copy.copy(g) returns an independent copy (consistent with g.copy()).
close_reason: Fixed _graph_shallowcopy to delegate to self.copy() — copy.copy(g) now returns independent copy. Added regression test test_shallow_copy_returns_independent_writable_graph. All 408 tests pass + cargo check clean.

Routing top-level subgraph() to return the view would match nx perf AND semantics (view reflects later G changes), but is architecture-level: must verify mutability contract, that downstream _raw_* callers coerce the view (_coerce_arg_to_fnx_graph), and frozen-ness. Not a quick win; needs focused session. Measured 2026-06-09.

title: eulerize incorrectly uses weight='weight' for shortest paths, fails on negative weights
description: fnx.eulerize uses shortest_path_length(G, u, v, weight='weight') and shortest_path(G, u, v, weight='weight') internally. The eulerize algorithm only needs the BFS (hop-count) shortest path between odd-degree nodes — edge weights are irrelevant. nx uses shortest_path(G, source=m, target=n) with no weight kwarg (unweighted BFS). On graphs with negative-weight edges fnx's calls hit Dijkstra's negative-weight rejection and raise ValueError('Contradictory paths found: negative weights?'); nx works fine because BFS doesn't care about weights. Fix: drop weight='weight' from both shortest_path_length and shortest_path calls in eulerize (match nx's behaviour and parity contract).
close_reason: Dropped weight='weight' from shortest_path / shortest_path_length in eulerize; now uses BFS matching nx. 4-shape parity probe matches; 367 tests pass.

title: bug: test_graph_iteration_detects_batch_node_mutations fails on main — remove_nodes_from does not invalidate active node iterator
description: PRE-EXISTING failure on origin/main (fails on the installed build independent of any cc change; surfaced 2026-06-09). Repro: G=fnx.Graph(); G.add_edges_from([(0,1),(1,2),(2,3),(3,4)]); it=iter(G); next(it); G.remove_nodes_from([2,3]); next(it) — expected RuntimeError('dictionary changed size during iteration'), got NO raise. test_review_mode_regression_lock.py::test_graph_iteration_detects_batch_node_mutations line ~11473, first class fnx.Graph. The br-r37-c1-39d82 invariant (node-set mutations must invalidate active iterators) is broken for remove_nodes_from — likely remove_nodes_from / remove_node does not update node_iter_dict (the live PyDict backing iter(G)) so CPython's size-change guard never fires. Not caused by the add_nodes_from (521ac87b9) or add_edges_from (bd35ec564) batch wins (neither touches removal/iteration). Investigate sync_node_iter_remove / node_iter_dict bookkeeping.
notes: SESSION 3 (cc) INSTRUMENTED BUILD findings (eprintln in 6384 mirror __iter__, 3506 NodeIterator __iter__, 640 node_iter_mirror_remove_key): STANDALONE Graph: mirror __iter__ fires -> dict_keyiterator, and node_iter_mirror_remove_key fires with key repr Some('2'),Some('3') on remove -> Graph WORKS. CANNOT trace pytest: pytest captures Rust's raw fd-2 stderr even with -s/--capture=no, so the instrumentation is invisible there. The pytest-only trigger (MultiGraph.__iter__ returns the mirror dict_keyiterator in pytest but NodeIterator standalone; Graph uses mirror in BOTH; DiGraph/MultiDiGraph use NodeIterator in both) could NOT be reproduced standalone. STRONG LEAD: node_iter_mirror_remove_key is invoked with a key whose repr is 'STRING' ('2' with quotes) while the mirror dict is populated by py_node_key (int keys per gc probe) — a type mismatch would make del_item silently miss (the helper swallows errors via ). For Graph the mirror still shrinks (works) so either Graph's mirror keys are strings or a different del path matches; for MultiGraph in pytest it does not. RECOMMEND the 0206684aa author: (1) verify node_iter_mirror_remove_key's key TYPE matches node_iter_mirror_insert's stored key type for all 4 graph types; (2) determine why MultiGraph selects the mirror __iter__ only under pytest. cc has spent 3 sessions; this is an opaque PyO3/CPython tp_iter + pytest-fd-capture interaction needing the migration author or a debugger build that writes traces to a FILE (not stderr) to bypass pytest capture.

comments.author: ubuntu
comments.text: SESSION 4 (cc) DEFINITIVE: NOT a HEAD bug. Built clean HEAD (8bdd96134) in isolated worktree — ALL 71 iteration/mutation regression-lock tests PASS, including test_graph_iteration_detects_batch_node_mutations AND test_graph_iteration_detects_node_mutation_like_python_dict. On HEAD, PyMultiGraph::__iter__ returns a NodeIterator guarded by nodes_seq; only PyGraph uses node_iter_mirror. Failure reproduces ONLY against uncommitted WIP refactor br-r37-c1-29f85 (node_iter_mirror->node_iter_dict routing ALL 4 types' __iter__ through the live mirror dict). Root cause in WIP: PyMultiGraph::remove_nodes_from (~2506) syncs the mirror AFTER node_key_map.remove, so py_node_key falls to the STRING key, missing the int-keyed mirror. Fix (applied+verified in working tree, full nx parity all 4 types): reorder sync_node_iter_remove BEFORE removal, matching PyGraph + PyMultiGraph::remove_node. CAVEAT: 29f85 has other failures (m25z2 keys-changed Di/MultiDiGraph) so not landable as-is. Blocked on 29f85 owner; no action vs HEAD.

title: perf: DiGraph in_degree/out_degree view ~30x slower than undirected degree (g.inner.out_degree mystery, identical IndexMap code)
close_reason: shipped beb42da77: native _native_out/in_degree O(1) fast path in _node_degree; root cause was Python len(succ AtlasView)=O(degree); golden 3f9d31c9 bit-identical to nx; out_degree 11.76ms->0.98ms=12.1x, in_degree 10.43ms->1.03ms=10.1x; 4402 tests pass; Score 12.5

comments.author: ubuntu
comments.text:
PROFILE DATA (min-of-many, load-robust): DiGraph out_degree/in_degree view iteration is ~25-33x slower per node than the structurally-IDENTICAL undirected DegreeView. list(D.out_degree()) per-node: 3.1us(n=100) 5.2us(n=2000) 5.6us(n=5000); list(G.degree()) per-node: 0.12-0.17us flat. Single lookup D.in_degree[0]=5.0us vs G.degree[0]=1.0us.

ISOLATION: list(D.nodes()) is FAST for both directed+undirected (~0.13us/node) -> py_node_key + tuple materialization + DiViewIterator are NOT the problem. The delta (out_degree minus nodes) is ~3.4us/node directed vs ~0.02us/node undirected, pointing at self.node_degree(&g,n) -> g.inner.out_degree(node) in DiDegreeView::__iter__ (crates/fnx-python/src/digraph.rs ~4145).

MYSTERY: DiGraph::out_degree (fnx-classes/src/digraph.rs:256) = successors.get(node).map_or(0,IndexSet::len); Graph::degree = adjacency.get(node).map_or(0,len). BOTH IndexMap<String,IndexSet<String>>, SAME default hasher (no custom hasher in either crate). type=DiGraph (is_multigraph False, simple inner). So the code looks O(1)-identical yet measures ~170x slower for the .get on successors/predecessors vs adjacency. NEEDS in-Rust instrumentation to confirm (is it the .get lookup? a degenerate IndexMap capacity/tombstone state from how the directed generator builds successors? cache layout?).

BUILD BLOCKER hit this session: maturin develop (via rch) intermittently does NOT recompile after consecutive source edits (shows Installed without Compiling) even after touch; rch DOES sync the edit to the remote (verified via rch exec grep). The remote target incremental cache appears stale. Workaround that sometimes forced a rebuild: full-file rewrite (git show HEAD:f > f). This blocked instrumenting the anomaly. digraph.rs unreserved.

comments.author: ubuntu
comments.text:
ROOT CAUSE (cProfile): D.out_degree()/in_degree() does NOT use the native DiDegreeView (digraph.rs, which is O(1) per node and fast). It routes through a pure-Python path in python/franken_networkx/__init__.py: _node_degree (line 3191) -> AtlasView __getitem__ (1103) + __len__ (1044) + _private_succ_mapping (28684) + _atlas (1085). __len__ on the succ AtlasView is O(degree) AND pure-Python -> list(out_degree()) is O(E) in slow Python. Confirmed: out_degree[deg-0-node]=1.6us, out_degree[deg-500-node]=98.6us (degree-proportional).

FIX: route Python DiGraph.out_degree/in_degree to the EXISTING native DiDegreeView (the native one already computes correct values via g.inner.out_degree which is O(1) successors.get().len()). One-ish-line change in __init__.py (the .out_degree/.in_degree property/patch). BLOCKED FOR cc: python/franken_networkx/__init__.py is reserved by TealSpring. Hand off to whoever owns __init__.py. (The native DiDegreeView in digraph.rs is correct + fast; no Rust change needed -- just stop shadowing it with the Python _node_degree wrapper.) Also: undirected G.degree() is fast because it already uses the native DegreeView, not the Python path.

title: [perf] find_cycle(directed) 456x slower — delegation converts whole graph for an early-exit function
description: Profile-backed: fnx.find_cycle(DiGraph 1500n/9000e) 30.6ms vs nx 0.07ms = 456x. find_cycle delegates to nx (br-r37-c1-fcdir/2hrfs: native _raw_find_cycle's DFS discipline differs from nx's edge_dfs so it returns a different valid cycle). The delegation pays a full O(V+E) _fnx_to_nx conversion (30ms) for a function that EARLY-EXITS at the first cycle (nx 0.07ms). nx's machinery can't avoid it: find_cycle.__wrapped__ is still _dispatchable, and edge_dfs (which find_cycle is built on) ALSO converts. FIX: reimplement edge_dfs + find_cycle's backtrack loop in Python on fnx's views (G.nbunch_iter, G[u]/succ/pred adjacency) matching nx's EXACT edge_dfs order — early-exit, zero conversion. OR fix the native _raw_find_cycle kernel's DFS to recurse in adjacency order (process each successor in order, report cycle when an edge hits an on-stack node IN edge-traversal order, NOT pre-checking all successors) so it matches nx's edge_dfs and can be routed directly. CONSTRAINT: byte-identical to nx across the fcdir corpus (Graph/DiGraph/MultiGraph x orientation None/original/reverse/ignore x source variants); __init__.py find_cycle wrapper is uncontested. Needs careful exact-order parity — focused session.
close_reason: find_cycle 456x->6.03x: reimplemented nx's edge_dfs + find_cycle loop VERBATIM in Python on fnx's views (G.edges(node)/in_edges/nbunch_iter, which match nx iteration order) instead of delegating (which paid a full O(V+E) _fnx_to_nx conversion for an early-exit fn). Byte-identical to nx across 40 trials x Graph/DiGraph/MultiGraph/MultiDiGraph x orientation None/original/reverse/ignore x source None/0 (golden 7f10ad5a); find_cycle/cycle tests 926 pass + full pytest 22056. Residual 6x is Python-loop overhead on a 0.06ms nx call (conversion eliminated). PATTERN: delegated early-exit fns can be de-delegated by porting nx's algorithm onto fnx views (same trick as graphml/gml direct-write).

title: copy.copy(G).graph should be identical object (is), not just equal value
description:
Investigation shows PyO3's clone_ref() creates separate Py<T> wrappers that don't preserve Python object identity even when pointing to the same underlying dict. The __copy__ method uses clone_ref but test shows id(G.graph) != id(copy.copy(G).graph). 

Further investigation (2026-05-22):
- Confirmed clone_ref returns Py<PyDict> pointing to same underlying dict
- But when returned from Rust getter to Python, creates new Python object
- Mutations via G_copy.graph do NOT reflect in G.graph (should for shallow copy)
- This is a fundamental PyO3 issue with how Py<T> works when stored in structs

Possible solutions:
1. Store graph_attrs as PyObject and use Python's dict directly
2. Implement __copy__ in Python wrapper layer
3. Investigate if PyO3 has alternative API for sharing references
4. File issue with PyO3 maintainers

Still blocked pending PyO3 expertise or workaround.

title: bug: approximate_current_flow_betweenness diverges from nx (~0.05-0.09) on some graphs — fnx->nx conversion RCM order != nx
description: fnx's delegated approximate_current_flow_betweenness diverges from nx by ~0.05-0.09 on some graphs (n=30,80 watts_strogatz): the fnx->nx conversion (_networkx_graph_for_parity) reproduces FNX's adjacency order, whose RCM ordering differs from nx's on the ORIGINAL graph (RCM(conv)!=RCM(orig)). The approximate SAMPLES specific relabeled indices, so a different RCM -> different sampled node pairs -> divergent (but valid) approximation. Conformance test only uses path_graph(4) (trivial RCM) so it's not caught. Root cause: fnx graph insertion-order adjacency differs from nx's for some graphs -> affects ALL order-sensitive delegated fns. Hard substrate fix.

title: fuzz_spanning_tree NumberSpanningTrees assumes non-negative weights
description: fuzz_spanning_tree harness asserts count.is_finite() && count >= -MST_EPS for number_of_spanning_trees. With negative edge weights, the Matrix-Tree theorem produces negative determinants — verified that both fnx and nx return -11.0 on a 3-node graph with weights [-5, 3, -2]. The harness uses ArbitraryWeightedGraph which by default allows negative weights, so the assertion fires on legitimate negatively-weighted inputs. Fix: drop the non-negativity assertion OR restrict to non-negative weights. Best: keep is_finite() check (sane sanity) but drop the non-negativity since Matrix-Tree on signed weights has no such guarantee.
close_reason: Dropped non-negativity assertion; Matrix-Tree theorem can produce negative counts on signed-weight graphs, fnx matches nx on the negative result. 45s fuzz clean (111k runs).

title: Conversion views (to_directed/to_undirected) don't enforce frozen mutators
description: _ConversionGraphViewBase view classes are built as type(name,(ConversionView, CanonicalGraph),{}), so inherited Rust mutators (add_node/add_edge/update/clear/...) sit in the MRO ahead of __getattr__. Calling result.update([]) / result.add_edge(...) on a frozen conversion view silently mutated the view's uninitialised Rust state instead of raising NetworkXError('Frozen graph can't be modified'). Same defect class as br-r37-c1-fgvfrz (_FilteredGraphView). Fix: shadow all _FILTERED_VIEW_MUTATORS as _frozen class attributes on _ConversionGraphViewBase.
close_reason: Shadowed all 12 _FILTERED_VIEW_MUTATORS as _frozen class attributes on _ConversionGraphViewBase (commit 95b537cc), mirroring the br-r37-c1-fgvfrz fix for _FilteredGraphView. Frozen conversion views now raise NetworkXError on update/add_edge/etc. Verified vs true baseline 6d055f33: test_graph_utilities.py 61->38 failures across all my conversion-view commits (23 fixed, 0 regressions).

title: MST + EdgeDataView perf: 250x+ speedups from removing O(N) hot-path scans
description:
Two real perf bugs uncovered while perf-probing fnx vs nx:

(1) minimum_spanning_tree (br-r37-c1-mstsync): public API routed any graph with a weight attribute through an fnx->nx->fnx parity detour because the Rust MST kernel read stale weights after post-construction g[u][v]['weight']=w mutation. The _sync_rust_edge_attrs helper (br-r37-c1-sjf4t) exists to push Python-visible dicts back into Rust storage — but the MST wrapper never called it. Wire it up and skip the nx detour: 1293ms -> 5ms on a 200-node Watts-Strogatz (~250x speedup). MultiGraph still routes through nx (to preserve type), NaN-weight routes through nx (to preserve ValueError contract).

(2) _FailFastEdgeIterator (br-r37-c1-edgesdataperf): __next__ did  +  (O(N) tuple-build + O(N) compare) on EVERY edge. For a 200-node 1000-edge graph this turned  into a 1.4s op. Replace per-next tuple walk with O(1) length probes — matches nx's contract (dict raises on size-change, not on key-permutation) without the quadratic overhead. 1422ms -> 2.67ms (530x speedup) on the same graph; cascades through every algorithm that touches edges(data=True).

Combined: all 17506 Python tests pass; 293 spanning-tree tests pass.
notes: Fixed in same commit that filed this bead. MST: skip nx detour for non-MultiGraph non-NaN cases, sync edge attrs first. EdgeIterator: replace per-next O(N) tuple compare with O(1) length check.

title: perf: dag_longest_path_length repeats native DP after dag_longest_path fast path
description: Profile-backed follow-up after br-r37-c1-pzutt. Post-change cProfile on deterministic 400-node DAG (tests/artifacts/perf/20260603T-dag-longest-path-native-dp/profile_after_fnx.txt) shows _result_payload still calls dag_longest_path twice: direct path plus dag_longest_path_length -> dag_longest_path. After pzutt: 80 repeats total 0.319s; dag_longest_path_length accounts for 0.164s and dag_longest_path is invoked 160 times. Candidate one-lever: exact-DiGraph dag_longest_path_length fast path computes the same NetworkX DP length directly from _native_in_edges_data_key without reconstructing/summing a path, preserving stable predecessor tie behavior for the path API by leaving dag_longest_path unchanged. Prove weight/default_weight/type/NaN/negative reset parity and golden SHA against NetworkX.
close_reason: Rejected: direct length DP only improved 0.0012875s->0.0012376s direct and hyperfine 458.0ms->436.5ms with overlapping variance; restored code; next target fused native topological-DP

title: [perf] hits_centrality kernel same String-keyed adjacency tax as katz (lib.rs:2927)
description: hits_centrality_generic (crates/fnx-algorithms/src/lib.rs:2927) uses the IDENTICAL Vec<Vec<&str>> canonical_neighbors + per-iteration index_by_node.get(neighbor) String-hash pattern that made katz 2.4x slower than nx (fixed in 1bb871886 via Vec<Vec<usize>> integer indices). Apply the same CSR port: precompute neighbor INDICES once, pure-integer hot loop. Index order==String order (index_by_node from String-sorted canonical_nodes) so bit-identical. hits has two phases (hub/auth) — convert both. Verify golden parity + perf vs nx.
notes: BLOCKED on correctness: the CSR port (Vec<Vec<&str>>->Vec<Vec<usize>>, index order==string order) is NOT bit-identical for hits — HEAD vs ported diverge (trial15 HEAD{2:0.071} vs ported{2:5.35}). Root cause: hits_centrality_generic is numerically UNSTABLE/buggy (returns negative values, diverges from nx — see correctness bug), so any change to float accumulation order shifts the (already-wrong) result. Do NOT ship the CSR port until the hits correctness bug is fixed; then re-verify bit-identity. katz (91ioe) was clean because katz IS correct/convergent.
close_reason: MOOT — the public fnx.hits wrapper (__init__.py) computes via scipy.sparse.linalg.svds end-to-end and does NOT call the Rust hits_centrality kernel (lib.rs:2927). That kernel is dead code for the public hits path (only exercised by Rust/conformance tests), so a CSR port of it gives ZERO public-API speedup. Closing. (Also: a bit-identical-intended CSR reorder shifted its output anyway because it's numerically unstable — see a0466 root cause.) If a future native hits path is wired, revisit.

title: [perf][no-gaps] lazy-ify O(E) dijkstra weight pre-scan to let native bidirectional_dijkstra beat nx at large n
description: After 09decd754 the undirected bidirectional_dijkstra runs an all-Rust CSR kernel (1.2-6.5x faster than the old in-process port, byte-exact, beats nx at n<=500). The residual large-n gap vs nx is NOT the kernel (now microseconds) but the dispatcher: bidirectional_dijkstra calls _should_delegate_dijkstra_to_networkx -> _native_check_dijkstra_weights_fast which does a full O(E) scan of edge_py_attrs every call (~0.67ms at n=3000, m=12000) to decide whether to delegate negative/non-finite/non-numeric weight graphs to nx. nx does NO such pre-scan (lazy per-edge). LEVER: make the native kernel itself detect negative/non-finite/non-numeric weights lazily during the search (it already returns Contradiction on vw_length<dists[w]) and remove the eager O(E) pre-scan for the native path — OR cache the weight-kind classification on the graph and invalidate on edge mutation. Must preserve exact nx delegation parity for negative/inf/None/callable/non-string weight cases. Same O(E)-pre-scan tax likely affects dijkstra_path/single_source_dijkstra single-pair.
close_reason: Completed: cached Dijkstra weight classification on clean graphs. Public FNX query mean 0.0016151240944681274s -> 0.0004072218777663592s (3.97x); paired 1000-query hyperfine no-cache 3.108s +/- 0.864s -> cached 764.1ms +/- 76.2ms (4.07x +/- 1.20x); golden digest 446581281b84c171a2cc1361e193fa82083621f94d663bb90e8d6799493504c0 matched NetworkX including dirty-cache invalidation cases.

title: [perf] label_propagation_communities structural conversion
description: label_propagation_communities delegated via _networkx_graph_for_parity (faithful attr-copying conversion, ~17ms = 55% of the 31ms call) + nx algo. The semi-synchronous algorithm is unweighted/structure-only. Lever: build a structural nx.Graph (nodes in G order + edges) instead of the faithful conversion — same node/adjacency iteration order so greedy_color + labeling byte-identical, ~half the conversion cost. Gate type(G) is Graph; multigraph/views keep faithful path. 2.1-2.8x -> 1.48-1.72x. Residual = conversion+algo; parity needs in-process de-delegation reproducing greedy_color.
close_reason: Shipped 36cdbc3d2: label_propagation structural nx.Graph conversion (nodes in G order + edges) instead of faithful _networkx_graph_for_parity (~55% of runtime). 2.1-2.8x -> 1.48-1.72x (1.42-1.62x self-speedup), byte-identical (golden sha == nx-contract baseline, 0/42 incl multigraph). Residual=conversion+algo; parity needs in-process greedy_color reproduction. 114 tests pass.

title: edge_bfs/edge_dfs TIMEOUT on multigraphs — G.adj[node]/G.pred[node] re-materialization in traversal
description: edge_bfs / edge_dfs TIMEOUT(>8s) on MultiGraph/MultiDiGraph (n=1500, m=7000). Each edges_from(node) does G.adj[node].items() (and G.pred[node] for orientation reverse/ignore) per BFS/DFS-visited node, re-materialising the WHOLE (Multi)AdjacencyView per access -> O(visited * (N+E)). bfs_edges/dfs_edges also ~17-18x on multi (likely same trap). FIX RECIPE (same as selfloop_edges 7132af442 / non_neighbors 3768b162f): replace G.adj[node] -> G[node] (native O(deg) row, G[node].items() for multi keydict) and G.pred[node] -> _native_predecessor_row_dict(node) (DiGraph/MultiDiGraph have it) for the reverse/ignore orientations. 4 orientation branches x simple/multi = ~8 edges_from defs each in edge_bfs+edge_dfs; test EXACT traversal order parity (BFS/DFS order is observable) across orientations. Graph case is fine (G.adj[node] O(deg) for simple). Found via multigraph traversal scan.
close_reason: FIXED. edge_bfs/edge_dfs did G.adj[node].items()/G.pred[node].items() per BFS/DFS-visited node, re-materialising the whole (Multi)AdjacencyView per access -> TIMEOUT(>8s) on MultiGraph/MultiDiGraph. Replaced 20 sites (12 G.adj[node]->G[node], 8 G.pred[node]->G._native_predecessor_row_dict(node), pred only on directed) across all 4 orientation branches (None/original/reverse/ignore) x simple/multi in both functions. G[node]/native pred-row order is byte-identical to G.adj[node]/G.pred[node] (verified 0/300). MultiGraph/MultiDiGraph TIMEOUT -> 2.5-3x; Graph 0.9x unchanged. BIT-EXACT vs nx 0/4800 EXACT traversal order across all orientations/classes/sources incl self-loops + parallel multi-edges; 383 traversal tests + full pytest 22104 passed. Same recipe as selfloop_edges(7132af442)/non_neighbors(3768b162f).

title: [fix][perf] apply bulk inner.clone() to PyMultiGraph/PyMultiDiGraph copy() (same node-order bug)
description: e8fac5a78 fixed Graph/DiGraph copy() to use self.inner.clone() (deterministic node order + removes per-edge Rust rebuild). PyMultiGraph::copy (fnx-python/src/lib.rs ~2038) and PyMultiDiGraph::copy (fnx-python/src/digraph.rs ~1351) still iterate self.node_key_map (HashMap, randomized order) to rebuild inner -> non-deterministic copy node order, same bug. Apply the same lever: inner = self.inner.clone(); propagate edges_dirty; refresh node attrs from node_py_attrs via replace_node_attrs; deep-copy edge_py_attrs AND edge_py_keys verbatim (multigraph has the extra edge_py_keys map + remember_edge_key). Verify with deep-copy isolation + nx node/edge-order parity + multigraph parallel-edge keys. Both files unreserved (currently). NOTE: copy() perf is ultimately bound by per-edge PyDict alloc (construction tax, blwqo/w1dm8, blocked behind locked struct).
close_reason: Fixed dfc9174f9: PyMultiGraph/PyMultiDiGraph copy() now use self.inner.clone() (deterministic node+edge+parallel-key order, matches nx; was non-deterministic via node_key_map HashMap iteration). Removes per-edge add_edge_with_key_and_attrs rebuild + redundant re-parse; deep-copies edge_py_attrs + edge_py_keys (first-wins identity). 8-case isolation+nx-parity golden 99977034, full suite 21278 passed.

title: fix(prefix_tree): node-numbering divergence vs nx (nx BFS-style, fnx path-by-path; isomorphic trees, different int ids)
description: Pre-existing parity divergence (not a regression): fnx.prefix_tree numbers trie nodes path-by-path (DFS) while nx numbers BFS-style, so direct nx.prefix_tree(paths) vs fnx.prefix_tree(paths) differ on node integer ids for shared-prefix path sets (64/120 random-int-path cases). Trees are ISOMORPHIC (same structure + source attrs + NIL terminals), only the arbitrary integer node labels differ. nx.dag_to_branching masks it by dispatching to the fnx backend. To fix: reproduce nx's node-id assignment order (BFS over the path set) in prefix_tree. Verify fnx tests that lock current numbering don't break. Found 2026-06-09 while shipping br-r37-c1-vuzc5 (perf).
close_reason: Shipped 36e57d7ba: nx-exact get_children/DFS numbering, 0/420 vs nx, dag_to_branching 0 vs genuine nx, perf preserved (~12x over baseline)
