comments.author: ubuntu
comments.text: After br-r37-c1-lcw4j fixed report_to_pygraph (the shared conversion), DENSE generators improved (complete 4.06x->2.16x) but SPARSE ones did not: grid_2d(60,60) 6.78x, watts_strogatz(2000,6) 3.99x, barabasi_albert(2000,4) 3.44x, random_regular(2000,4) 2.86x. These have few edges (~6-8k) so report_to_pygraph is a small fraction; the bottleneck is the native generator kernel in crates/fnx-generators/src/lib.rs (e.g. barabasi_albert_graph line 4015, watts_strogatz line 3962, grid_2d_graph line 3273). NOTE: gnp_random_graph WINS (0.40x) -> the kernel pattern that one uses is good; compare. Profile each kernel (FNX_GEN_TIMING-style split of kernel vs report) to localize. NOT reserved by TealSpring. crate fnx-generators.

comments.author: ubuntu
comments.text: ROOT CAUSE (investigated): the native fnx-generators kernels (barabasi_albert_graph etc.) are NOT used by the Python path -- fnx.barabasi_albert_graph (__init__.py:18922) is a pure-Python reimplementation that calls Graph.add_edges_from per node to match nx RNG. So the gap is the foundational CONSTRUCTION tax in PyGraph::add_edges_from -> add_edge (crates/fnx-python/src/lib.rs): per edge it does 2x node_key_to_string + ~5 String clones of the node names across node_key_map/node_py_attrs/inner/edge_key/remember_edge_key + 2-3 PyDict allocs + MultiGraph edge-key machinery (PyGraph is MultiGraph-backed). raw add_edges_from(20k edges)=64ms. TRIED + REVERTED (proven zero-change, golden 911ebb2b bit-identical to nx 7 cases incl mutation/independence): eliminating the per-edge 'merged' PyDict in add_edges_from for plain/key edges -- before 64.1ms after 64.9ms, no effect (merged was negligible vs add_edge). REAL FIX = a bulk add_edges_from fast-path (plain 2-tuples, no data) that resolves node keys once and batches inner+Python-map population, bypassing per-edge add_edge overhead -- larger/riskier effort (must preserve multigraph key assignment + isomorphism). Deferring as a bulk-construction rewrite. grid_2d (6.78x, biggest) additionally pays repr() per tuple node in node_key_to_string.

title: hyper_wiener_index error contract diverges on empty + disconnected graphs
description: fnx.hyper_wiener_index diverges from nx on two shapes: (1) empty graph — fnx returns 0.0, nx raises NetworkXPointlessConcept('Connectivity is undefined for the null graph.'); (2) disconnected graphs (isolated nodes, disconnected components, selfloop-only) — fnx raises NetworkXError('Graph is not connected'), nx returns float('inf'). The Rust binding's pre-check is too aggressive in both directions. Fix in the wrapper: raise NetworkXPointlessConcept on len(G)==0, return inf on disconnected, native path otherwise.
close_reason: Empty -> NetworkXPointlessConcept; disconnected -> inf; connected -> Rust path. 7-shape parity probe matches nx; 438 wiener tests pass.

title: [bug] add_edges_from drops valid prior edges before raising on malformed edge (nx persists them)
description: Graph.add_edges_from([(1,2),(3,4),(5,)]): nx adds edges (1,2),(3,4) THEN raises NetworkXError on (5,) -> graph has 2 edges. fnx validates the whole batch up front (3-pass) and raises before adding anything -> graph empty. nx processes edges INLINE so prior valid edges persist (and partial node state too: add_edges_from([(1,None)]) leaves node 1 in nx). Repro in /tmp/aef_probe.py (partial_then_bad case). Fix needs per-edge validate-then-add ordering or add-valid-prefix-before-raise. Low priority (error-path edge case). Discovered during tlfqe analysis.
close_reason: Fixed in a250e836a: add_edges_from now persists valid prefix + partial node before raising (nx inline parity). 50-case differential (Graph+DiGraph), 0 mismatches, golden sha 9b814ecd. Proof bundle in tests/artifacts/perf/20260604T-add-edges-from-inline-parity-cc/.

title: [bug] DiGraph.to_undirected diverges from nx on reciprocal-edge attrs (32/60)
description: The Python-visible DiGraph.to_undirected (binding: fnx-python/src/digraph.rs ~3563, TealSpring-locked) merges reciprocal directed edges a<->b with the WRONG winner: 32/60 random DiGraphs (with differing edge weights) diverge from networkx on the merged undirected edge's attrs, + 8/60 on edge order. nx iterates G.edges()=node->successor order and the LATTER direction's attrs win; the fnx binding iterates in a different (edges-map/insertion) order. FIX: iterate edges in node->successor (edges_ordered) order, like nx. The SUBSTRATE DiGraph::to_undirected was already canonicalised in 6c721e942 (so substrate is correct); the binding needs the same. Repro: a=DiGraph; a.add_edge(0,1,weight=1); a.add_edge(1,0,weight=2); a.to_undirected()[0][1]['weight'] should match nx. Probe reciprocal edges with differing attrs +/- a remove_node.
close_reason: Fixed cbffefac7: PyDiGraph::to_undirected now iterates inner.edges_ordered() (node->successor order, deterministic) and merges reciprocal a<->b with nx's latter-wins dict.update (was: edge_py_attrs HashMap order + first-wins -> random/seed-dependent winner, up to 32/60 divergence after remove_node). 200-case randomized differential vs nx (reciprocal+extra-keys+removes) 0 mismatches, deterministic, golden 39a131ad, full suite 21281 passed.

title: [perf][audit] 70 runtime_policy().clone() sites across bindings — unbounded-ledger clone tax class (bfs/dfs_tree fix proved 5.3x on identical structures)
description: wvbzw lever 1 (487e47636) proved the pattern: result-returning bindings that clone a source's RuntimePolicy clone its UNBOUNDED decision ledger (one entry per recorded op — per-edge ctor sources have E entries), often twice (clone + set_runtime_policy). bfs_tree went 6.22x->1.14x by switching to new_empty_with_mode + fresh ledger. AUDIT: algorithms.rs has 33 sites, digraph.rs 19, lib.rs 17, readwrite.rs 1. For each: result graphs need MODE not history -> RuntimePolicy::new(mode). EXCEPTIONS to judge: copy()/__copy__/pickle (does any consumer read the propagated ledger? memory says construction-time policy is overwritten anyway). Probe recipe: identical-structure native-built vs ctor-built source timing (tests/artifacts/perf/20260605T-tree-ledger-clone-tax-cc). Expect multiple Score>=2.0 wins; subgraph/copy/reverse/to_directed are the hottest candidates.
close_reason: Completed fresh-ledger cleanup for result graph constructors/copy/convert sites. Final helper closeout commit 713faf208 adds Graph/MultiGraph clone_with_fresh_policy after prior commits 693b6e408 and 166ed6a70. Evidence: MST ctor-built identical-structure source hit a 2.45x ledger-copy tax before; Graph.copy 282ms -> 28.6ms (4.99x), DiGraph.copy 391ms -> 170ms (2.75x), Graph.to_directed 525ms -> 236ms (2.22x). Proof: fmt/check/clippy, release maturin rebuild, focused copy/conversion pytest 123 passed, UBS touched Rust exit 0.

title: fix(astar): respect edge direction on DiGraphs + match nx FIFO tie-break
description: Two A* defects: (1) DIRECTION: the Rust _raw_astar_path kernel is undirected-only (astar_path(graph: &Graph)) and the PyO3 binding fed it gr.undirected() even for DiGraph/MultiDiGraph, so astar_path/astar_path_length silently ignored edge direction and returned paths over non-existent reverse edges (e.g. [0,4] when only 4->0 exists; nx returns the correct long route). dijkstra/bellman_ford honour direction. (2) TIE-BREAK: among equal f-scores the kernel broke ties on node identity, but nx uses FIFO insertion-counter (next(c)); on unit-weight grids astar_path returned a different valid shortest path than nx. Fix: directed graphs delegate to nx (no native directed astar kernel yet); undirected Rust kernel now carries a monotonic counter for FIFO tie-break. Found via weighted shortest-path tie-break probe.
close_reason: Fixed both: directed graphs delegate to nx (kernel was undirected-only, dropped direction -> invalid paths); undirected Rust kernel now uses FIFO insertion-counter tie-break matching nx. 0 mismatches vs nx (directed+undirected); 68 astar tests pass; clippy clean. Regression test added (8 cases). Follow-up: native directed A* kernel.

title: nested subgraph edges() yields divergent node-iteration order vs nx (orientation flips)
description: PRE-EXISTING (present on HEAD before br-r37-c1-kum9v fix; 14/80 exact-order + 6/80 even-sorted-orientation divergences on G.subgraph(outer).subgraph(inner).edges()). Undirected nested subgraph edges yield (u,v) with source picked by a node-iteration order that differs from nx for some nested cases -> orientation flips (fnx ('13','7') vs nx ('7','13')). Single-level subgraph edges are fine; only NESTED (subgraph-of-subgraph) diverges. Likely the nested _nbunch/node-order resolution. NOT a perf issue — pure ordering parity. Repro: build random Graph, subgraph(outer).subgraph(inner), compare list(.edges()).
close_reason: FIXED. Root cause: nx COMPOSES a subgraph-of-subgraph onto the ORIGINAL graph (nested view._graph IS the root), so node iteration uses root length for the node_ok_shorter threshold (2*len(keep)<len(root)) and yields keep-set order. fnx CHAINED (._graph = outer VIEW, length=outer) -> took the other branch -> outer-filtered node order -> divergent edge orientation. Fix: _subgraph_with_view collapses a pure node-set/default-edge-filter chain onto its concrete root (filt already only holds nodes visible up the whole chain). Nested node-order/edge/degree bit-exact 0/4000 (all 4 classes, self-loops, keys/data) + 0/1000 quadruple-nesting; edge_subgraph-then-subgraph (non-default edge filter) correctly does NOT compose; full pytest 22104. BONUS: composing to root lets the native-row fast paths fire on nested subgraphs too.

title: [parity] add_node(node, **attr) with attr keyed 'n' raised TypeError (param named 'n' collided) — broke read_graphml/gml/pajek for graphs with an 'n' attr
description: FOUND+FIXED this session. The Rust add_node binding used #[pyo3(signature=(n, **attr))]; an attr dict containing key 'n' (e.g. from _from_nx_graph's add_node(node, **attrs) on a read graph) bound the node positionally to n AND received n=<val> via kwargs -> 'multiple values for argument n'. Broke every readwrite reader (graphml/gml/pajek/...) for graphs carrying a node/edge attr literally named 'n'. Fix: rename the param to nx's public 'node_for_adding' across all 4 classes -> exact nx collision parity (nx only collides on a 'node_for_adding' attr). 18-case cross-impl readwrite probe + 4-class direct collision battery + full pytest 21841.
notes: FIXED+CLOSED candidate: param rename n->node_for_adding across all 4 add_node bindings (lib.rs x2, digraph.rs x2). Exact nx collision parity. Pinned test_add_node_attr_param_collision.py (9 cases). Probe artifacts in 20260607T-add-node-n-collision-cc. NOTE: audit other #[pyo3(signature=(<short>, **attr/**kwargs))] bindings for the same short-param-name collision class (add_edge u/v? edge attrs keyed 'u'/'v'?).
close_reason: param renamed n->node_for_adding (nx's public name) in all 4 add_node bindings; attr keyed 'n' now accepted with exact nx collision parity. Broke read_graphml/gml/pajek for any 'n'-attr graph. 18-case readwrite cross-impl probe clean + 9-case pinned battery + full pytest 21841. Follow-up noted: audit other short-named **attr bindings (add_edge u/v).

title: [perf] native mycielskian construction
description: mycielskian builds the result via convert_node_labels_to_integers + per-edge R.add_edge in _mycielskian_step (construction tax), 2.42x slower than nx. Result is integer-labeled (shadow u+n, apex 2n), deterministic node/edge order (no set dependency). Lever: native mycielskian_step kernel assembling edges in Rust (like the graph products) — node 0..n original + n..2n shadows + apex 2n; edges = old + (u,v+n) + (u+n,v) old-edge passes + apex-to-shadows. Gated simple/undirected. Target: 2.42x -> faster than nx.
close_reason: Shipped 57c7f1aa1: _mycielskian_step batched (per-edge add_edge loop -> add_nodes_from/add_edges_from). 2.95-3.33x -> 1.39-1.48x (2.06-2.6x self-speedup), byte-exact (golden sha 38648412 == nx, 0/24 incl attrs/string/iterations, identity+mutation verified). 8 tests pass. Python-only batching beat the native-kernel plan.

title: Rust _fnx weighted algos blind to add_weighted_edges_from / post-mutation edge attrs
description: The Rust algorithm layer reads edge weights via DiGraph::edge_attrs(u,v).get(attr).as_f64(). Edge attributes set by add_weighted_edges_from(...) or post-creation mutation G[u][v][attr]=val are visible to Python (G.get_edge_data == {'weight':5}) but NOT to Rust edge_attrs(): _fnx.dijkstra_path_length / bellman_ford_path_length / dag_longest_path_length all return hop-count (default_weight) for such graphs. add_edge(u,v,weight=5) and add_edges_from([(u,v,{'weight':5})]) DO sync correctly. This is a latent attr-sync gap in the Rust storage; today it is masked because most Python algorithm wrappers (dijkstra etc.) route around it, but it is a correctness landmine for any wrapper that trusts the Rust weighted fast path (see dag_longest_path_length). Repro matrix in br for dag_longest_path_length. Proper fix: ensure add_weighted_edges_from and edge-attr mutation propagate into the Rust-native edge attr store consumed by edge_attrs().
close_reason: Fixed raw Rust weighted bindings to sync Python edge attrs before native kernels

title: link-prediction (CN/RA soundarajan_hopcroft): per-pair G.neighbors PyO3 round-trip
description:
fnx.cn_soundarajan_hopcroft and fnx.ra_index_soundarajan_hopcroft called G.neighbors(u), G.neighbors(v), and (for RA) G.degree(w) inside the per-(u,v) iteration. Each PyO3 round-trip materializes a fresh neighbor view.

Fix: snapshot adj (dict of sets) and degrees once at generator entry; inner loop becomes pure-Python dict lookups.

Verified: 100 link-prediction tests pass; 17506 total tests pass. CN: 5.32ms (was ~similar; nx 4.35ms); RA: 4.29ms (nx 2.57ms). Same pattern (adj/degree snapshot) applied throughout the perf sweep this session.
notes: Fixed in same commit by snapshotting adj/degree at generator entry.

title: perf: replace MST hash-map DSU with indexed array DSU
description: Current rch public-algo sweep on weighted GNP(1000,0.01,seed=7) shows fnx.minimum_spanning_tree same golden SHA as NetworkX but slower: NX mean 0.014108146875514649s, FNX mean 0.029337620246224105s (2.08x). cProfile over 40 FNX calls: _fnx.minimum_spanning_tree 0.614s of 0.722s, graph_has_nonfinite_edge_weight 0.091s, sync 0.017s. One-lever target: replace string-keyed HashMap parent/rank union-find in crates/fnx-algorithms Kruskal with compact node-indexed parent/rank Vec arrays, preserving nodes_ordered collection, stable weight-only sort, first-seen undirected edge order, total weight, and result edge order. Alien primitive: Tarjan DSU locality / contiguous parent/rank arrays from alien-graveyard 7.13.
close_reason: Rejected: indexed-array DSU preserved golden SHA but failed the keep rule. Baseline hyperfine fnx-mst 469.0ms +/- 15.8ms; after 686.6ms +/- 65.9ms. cProfile native section improved only 0.614s -> 0.555s over 40 calls, not enough and process envelope regressed. Source hunk reverted; no MST code kept. Next target must be a deeper MST primitive, not DSU map micro-tuning.

title: contracted_nodes/contracted_edge copy=False returns a copy, not the input graph
description: identified_nodes (backing contracted_nodes / contracted_edge) with copy=False mutates the input graph G in place (G.clear() + re-add) but then returns _from_nx_graph(G). _from_nx_graph copies fnx graphs rather than returning them as-is, so the result is a distinct object. nx's contract for copy=False is in-place mutation returning the SAME object (result is G). Repro: g=fnx.Graph(); ...; fnx.contracted_edge(g,(0,1),copy=False) is g -> False. Fix: the copy=False branch must 'return G' directly (the input, mutated in place). The _from_nx_graph 'always return fnx type' goal (br-r37-c1-k3ksc) conflicts with copy=False identity semantics — identity wins. Surfaced by test_graph_utilities.py::test_contracted_edge_matches_networkx_without_fallback (6 copy=False param combos).
close_reason: identified_nodes copy=False branch now returns G directly instead of _from_nx_graph(G) (commit 02a3e6d4). _from_nx_graph copies fnx graphs, breaking nx's copy=False 'result is G' in-place contract. 76 contraction tests pass; 355 broader.

title: perf(find_cycle): native edge_dfs+find_cycle kernel to drop per-node fnx-view tax (~3.3x on cycle-dominated graphs)
description: find_cycle is a pure-Python verbatim port of nx edge_dfs+find_cycle on fnx views (br-r37-c1-fcyclnative). Residual ~3.3x slower than nx (cycle_graph n=4000: 13ms nx vs 42ms fnx; scales 2.4x->3.5x) because per-node G.edges(node)/in_edges(node) EdgeView generation crosses into Rust each node. Typical graphs early-exit so gap is small in practice; bites worst-case cycle-dominated traversals. Fix: native Rust kernel porting edge_dfs (DFS edge stack) + find_cycle's active_nodes/edges-pop cycle-trim, emitting cycle edges in nx's DFS-traversal orientation. MUST handle all 4 orientation modes (None/original/reverse/ignore), directed+undirected, multigraph (keys), source/nbunch, and exact edge_id semantics (frozenset for undirected, tuple for directed). Verify 0 mismatches vs existing 800-case corpus (directed/undirected x multigraph x orientation x sources) + NetworkXNoCycle parity. Template: the just-shipped biconnected_component_edges DFS port (br-r37-c1-bcedfs, 5101b77c4) and all_pairs/single_target ordered BFS bindings.
close_reason: Completed by existing native find_cycle_simple_dfs fast path in current main. Verified 2026-06-09: focused find_cycle parity tests 22 passed; direct hot-loop timer on deterministic DiGraph(1500,9000), repeats=1000: FNX 0.003029458s vs NetworkX 0.017666332s (5.83x faster); existing report tests/artifacts/perf/20260609T-find-cycle-native-dfs-cc/report.md records the original profile-backed lever, 0/209 proof, and broader 935-test cycle/edge_dfs parity. No duplicate source change made.

title: perf: BFS/CC traversal ~10-13x slower than nx
description: BFS and connected_components traversal are ~10-13x slower than nx on 1000-node graphs. Root cause is likely string-based HashSet lookups vs integer indices. See reference_failfast_iter_perf.md memory note.
close_reason: Connected_components is now 4x faster than nx (was 10-13x slower). Added integer-indexed adjacency with neighbors_indices() method. BFS traversal uses Vec<bool> visited bitmap. bfs_edges also 2x faster. SSSP still slow - filed br-r37-c1-bapbi.

comments.author: ubuntu
comments.text: Root cause: Uses HashSet<&str> for visited + string cloning for edges. Fix requires internal integer indexing - significant architectural work.

comments.author: ubuntu
comments.text: Deferring: requires architectural changes across fnx-core and fnx-algorithms. Potential approaches: (1) FxHashSet for faster string hashing (~20-30% speedup), (2) internal integer indexing with name<->index maps (would close the 10x gap). Both require significant refactoring. Moving to test coverage hardening per user request.

title: perf: native multigraph DFS/BFS walk to eliminate simple-graph conversion (dfs_tree/bfs_tree 6-9x residual)
description: After structure-only-ordered conversion routing (409aac6ea), dfs_tree/bfs_tree multigraph is 6-9x slower than nx; cProfile shows 100% of time is now INSIDE the native _fnx.dfs_tree kernel — the structure-only conversion (build whole simple Graph + apply_row_orders) + tree assembly (py_row_key/py_node_key per node into node_key_map) on EVERY call. nx pays no conversion: it walks the multigraph adjacency directly. LEVER: add native multigraph DFS/BFS edge kernels in fnx_algorithms that walk mg.neighbors(u)/mdg.successors(u) (DISTINCT neighbors, adjacency order) directly — no simple-graph materialization. dfs/bfs only need structure, so distinct-neighbor iteration suffices. Target: dfs_tree/bfs_tree multigraph 6-9x -> <2x. Also profile the tree-assembly py_row_key cost (node_key_map population).
notes: FIXED (b5e7d46ec): native integer-indexed DFS/BFS walks (dfs_edges_indexed/dfs_forest_indexed/bfs_edges_indexed) over an index adjacency built in one O(V+E) pass from the multigraph inner's distinct-neighbor string adjacency — ZERO simple-graph conversion. dfs_tree/bfs_tree multigraph now FASTER than nx (0.64-0.71x, was 6-9x); dfs_edges/bfs_edges 15-23x -> 0.96-1.27x. 71720-case BYTE-IDENTICAL golden proof + 1931 tests. RESIDUAL: dfs_predecessors/dfs_successors/dfs_preorder_nodes/dfs_postorder_nodes still convert (lower-frequency; same lever applies). bfs_edges slightly >1.0 (index-map build overhead).

title: [parity] multi_source_dijkstra + bellman_ford KEY ORDER diverges from nx on equal-distance ties (k9q6q-class, kernel emission order)
description: Typed corpus (random.Random(5), 40 trials mixed Graph/DiGraph int/float) shows: multi_source_dijkstra_path_length key order diverges on equal-distance groups (e.g. nodes 10/8/0 all dist=12: fnx emits 10,8,0 nx emits 8,0,10) — wrapper _reorder_by_distance stable sort preserves KERNEL adjacency-walk order which != nx heap push-seq tie-break; needs the k9q6q treatment (kernel emits finalize order with seq tie-break) for the multi-source kernel. single_source_bellman_ford_path_length trial-20 likewise order-only (value+type sets match; order diverges past index 8) — SPFA discovery order divergence in some shape. VALUES+TYPES are correct (per-path int propagation fixed srczero family). Repro script in tests/artifacts/correctness/20260607T-allint-cache-srczero-cc/.
notes: msd HALF FIXED+SHIPPED: multi_source_dijkstra_directed was missing the k9q6q finalize-order emission (undirected twin had it; directed emitted node-index order via 'for i in 0..n' + ordered_nodes). Added finalize_order tracking + finalize_names emission — 40-trial typed-order corpus clean incl. 2-source seeding (sha 42c975c0). REMAINING (bellman SPFA): minimal 9-edge DiGraph repro, source 3, edges [(3,11,1),(5,0,4),(3,10,4),(10,11,1),(2,6,1),(3,1,3),(11,5,4),(1,2,4),(3,11,7)] — note the (3,11) DOUBLE add (weight overwritten 1->7); fnx emits 0(13) before 6(8), nx emits 6 before 0 — UNEQUAL distances so NOT the tie class; nx's deque/in_q re-queue discipline ordering diverges in some way my hand-sim didn't resolve; needs side-by-side trace of nx _bellman_ford vs kernel on this repro. NOT shipped blind.
close_reason: BOTH parts fixed. Part 1 (82d97431c): multi_source_dijkstra_directed missing k9q6q finalize-order emission (twin drift). Part 2: nx SPFA's pred-in-queue SKIP heuristic + pred-list equality appends were missing from bellman_ford_spfa — deferred relaxations fired early, materializing stale distances nx never sees (minimal 9-edge repro traced line-by-line against nx _inner_bellman_ford). Fixed in the SHARED helper (both classes; twin-safe). recent_update neg-cycle accelerator deliberately not replicated (count==n backstop; documented). 60-trial corpus + neg weights + dup-add shapes sha 8dea3edc; typed-order 40-trial msd corpus 42c975c0; full pytest 21807.

title: PERF: dijkstra Python gating adds ~150ms overhead per call on 500-node graph
description: fnx.dijkstra_path is 64x slower than nx (159ms vs 2.5ms Rust kernel time + 150ms Python gating overhead). Gates _has_negative_edge_weight_for_dijkstra (63ms), _has_positive_infinity_edge_weight_for_dijkstra (50ms — added in br-r37-c1-z35td), and _has_nonnumeric_edge_weight (45ms) each scan every edge in Python. Total: ~150ms gating + 2.5ms algorithm. Fix options: (a) extend native Rust _has_negative_edge_weight helper to also detect +inf and non-numeric in a single pass, (b) remove the is_finite filter in Rust edge_weight_or_default and let inf flow through, eliminating the Python gates, (c) cache the gate result across calls when graph topology unchanged. (b) is the cleanest fix but touches ~50 callers; (a) is the most contained. Same hot path affects astar (now also gated for +inf) and bellman_ford. Pre-existing for negative-weight scan; my +inf gate landed in br-r37-c1-z35td amplified it.
close_reason: done

title: perf: reverse(DiGraph) 5.14x slower than nx
description: Interleaved sweep: m.reverse(g) on directed gnp(1000,0.02) ~10k edges = nx 27ms vs fnx 138ms (5.14x slower). Memory note reference_digraph_reverse_transpose says reverse was optimized to parity (DiGraph::reversed integer index space, 698e1fe50) gated on attr-mirror dicts all-empty — possible regression or the gate isn't firing for this graph shape. Investigate: is it taking the native integer-transpose path or a slow String/rebuild path? Re-verify with interleaved timing first (host-noise), then check the gate condition. Also balanced_tree(3,7) 1.52x, circular_ladder 1.56x, random_regular_graph 1.41x flagged in same sweep (likely per-edge add_edge construction tax -> bulk add_edges_from).
close_reason: Shipped c7846e62f (main+master). ROOT: top-level reverse(G,copy=True) called G.reverse() (native fast transpose, already fnx DiGraph) then redundantly _from_nx_graph(result) — double-build antipattern, byte-identical output ~5x slower. Fix: return G.reverse() directly. 5.4x SLOWER -> 9.9-13.2x FASTER than nx (n=1000 141ms->1.9ms; n=2000 306ms->5.4ms). Byte-exact vs nx (int/str/MultiDiGraph + node/edge/graph attrs + copy=False); undirected raises; validated on actual committed code via worktree build. Python-only. NOTE: the G.reverse() METHOD was already fast (0.67x); only the top-level function had the double-build.

title: perf: native safe-Rust network-simplex kernel to beat nx (residual ~2x on min_cost_flow)
description: After br-r37-c1-t6gfk, min_cost_flow runs nx's network-simplex in-process on the fnx graph = 1.6-2x slower than nx (the array-extraction reads the fnx graph + fnx's redundant pre-validation re-reads edges/nodes). To BEAT nx, port the network-simplex (spanning-tree pivots) to a native safe-Rust kernel reading integer CSR adjacency, OR (cheaper) trim the redundant Python pre-validation since nx's network_simplex already validates negative-cap/inf-demand/total-demand. Cost-only contract gives freedom.
notes: PARTIAL (Python port, parity) shipped d1abfbbc5: ported nx's network-simplex into _network_simplex_native.py (pivots verbatim) run over a _FastG shim that pre-extracts node/edge data via fnx native bulk readers + fast type-is-int integral check. min_cost_flow 2.05x->1.25x (this session), ported SOLVER itself at TRUE parity (1.03-1.10x). Golden e335234e cost-exact, 1072 flow/error tests pass. REMAINING to BEAT nx: the pivots are Python (same speed as nx) — needs NATIVE RUST network-simplex pivots (integer-CSR spanning-tree). Bead stays open for that.
close_reason: Shipped 64d11bc0a: native safe-Rust integer network-simplex kernel (crates/fnx-python/src/network_simplex.rs) — faithful BSD transcription of nx's primal network-simplex pivots specialised to i64. min_cost_flow now BEATS nx 1.5-2x (n=300 0.65x, n=600 0.50x); kernel-only solve ~25x faster than nx's Python pivots. Cost-exact: golden e335234e unchanged, 200-trial corpus 0 failures, 22252 tests pass, clippy/fmt clean. Float inputs keep byte-exact Python pivots. Journey: SSP 50-96x slower -> Python port 1.25x -> native kernel beats nx.

title: [perf] katz_centrality native kernel 2.4x slower than nx's Python power-iteration — likely String-keyed adjacency tax
description: Profile-backed: katz_centrality (standard case -> _raw_katz_centrality native Rust) = 28.5ms vs nx 11.9ms = 2.4x SLOWER on an 800-node graph. Suspicious: native Rust losing to nx's PURE-PYTHON power iteration suggests the kernel pays the String-keyed adjacency tax in its hot loop (same pattern as the BFS/centrality kernels fixed via integer CSR neighbors_indices). FIX: port _raw_katz_centrality's power-iteration to integer CSR adjacency (neighbors_indices, no String hashing per neighbor per iter), like global_efficiency/distance_measures. Bit-exact (same iteration math). katz_centrality_numpy is already 0.0x (fast). Check if the kernel file is reserved (fnx-algorithms).
close_reason: katz_centrality CSR port LANDED in HEAD (1bb871886 — swept into peer's dijkstra commit via git add -A; code is mine+complete). katz_centrality_generic precomputed Vec<Vec<&str>> neighbors + did index_by_node.get(neighbor) String-hash per neighbor per power-iteration (k*|E| hashes -> Rust lost 2.4x to nx Python). Fixed: precompute Vec<Vec<usize>> neighbor INDICES once (|E| lookups), pure-integer hot loop. Index order==String order (index_by_node built from String-sorted canonical_nodes) so summation is bit-identical. 0/60 golden parity (e6b78be4), 115x faster than nx on watts-800 (0.5ms vs 57ms), 1184 katz/centrality tests pass. NOTE: hits_centrality_generic (lib.rs:2927) has the SAME String-tax pattern — follow-up.

title: Several wrappers check empty/length before directed-type guard, leaking wrong exception class
description: fnx.eulerize / is_distance_regular / fiedler_vector / spectral_bisection check the empty/length guard before the directed-type guard. On an empty/length<2 DIRECTED graph nx raises NetworkXNotImplemented('not implemented for directed type') because the @not_implemented_for decorator runs first; fnx raises NetworkXPointlessConcept (or NetworkXError 'graph has less than two nodes'). Drop-in callers catching NetworkXNotImplemented miss the error class. For fiedler_vector and spectral_bisection the divergence extends to any directed graph with <2 nodes (di-single is also wrong). Fix: reorder guards in all four wrappers — directed check first, then empty/length, then is_connected.
close_reason: Directed guard moved ahead of empty/length guard in eulerize, is_distance_regular, fiedler_vector (spectral_bisection cascades). 20-case parity probe matches nx. 29 tests pass.

title: bellman_ford_path returns wrong path
description:
The Rust bellman_ford_path implementation returns the direct edge [0, 2] (weight=4) instead of the shorter path [0, 1, 2] (weight=3). The path length returned is 4 instead of the correct 3.

Test case:
```python
G = fnx.Graph()
G.add_weighted_edges_from([(0, 1, 1), (1, 2, 2), (0, 2, 4)])
fnx.bellman_ford_path(G, 0, 2)  # Returns [0, 2] (wrong)
nx.bellman_ford_path(G_nx, 0, 2)  # Returns [0, 1, 2] (correct)
```

dijkstra_path works correctly.

Fix: Always delegate bellman_ford_path and bellman_ford_path_length to nx until the Rust binding is fixed.
close_reason: done

description: Substrate view-materialization tax: list(G.nodes(data=True)) ~21x slower than nx, dict(G.adjacency()) ~73x, get_node_attributes PRESENT 0.70x. fnx reconstructs a fresh Python attr dict per node/edge from Rust(+overlay) where nx yields live dicts. Bounds get_node_attributes (present), and many copy/convert fns. DEEP lever: fast attr-dict view materialization (shared-empty for attr-less, or lazy). Attempted get_node_attributes_rust native kernel = UNSAFE (misses Python-overlay attrs even after _fnx_sync_attrs_to_inner, wrong order). Profile which hot callers hit the present-attr path before substrate work.
notes: PROGRESS (this session): NodeDataView.__len__ was len(self._materialize()) -> built the (node,data) list a 2ND time per list() (size hint + iter). Fixed to len(self._view) (bijection, O(1) native): list(nodes(data)) 2x self-speedup, len() 164x, byte-identical golden 0a66f137, 1172+ tests. Same view-wrapper double-materialize anti-pattern as EdgeDataView (ipm32). REMAINING: list(nodes(data=True)) still ~10x slower than nx (0.13ms vs 0.012ms) = the Rust NodeView __call__ tuple-materialization FLOOR (fnx builds (node,dict) across PyO3; nx iterates existing {node:dict}). Closing needs a Python {node:dict} mirror kept in sync (architectural, high-risk). adjacency()+edges(nbunch,data) already done. Audit OTHER views for len(self._materialize()): only line 654 (EdgeDataView fallback, rarely hit) remains.

title: to_directed/to_undirected conversion views drop graph-level attributes
description: fnx.to_directed(G) / to_undirected(G) return _ConversionGraphViewBase subclasses. __init__ stores graph.graph in self.__dict__['graph'], but the fnx Graph base class defines 'graph' as a _GraphAttrsDescriptor (a data descriptor), which takes precedence over instance __dict__ on attribute access. So view.graph invokes the descriptor and returns the VIEW's own empty Rust-backed attrs ({}), silently dropping the source graph's graph-level attributes (e.g. {'name':'base'}). nx.to_directed preserves them. Repro: g=fnx.MultiGraph(); g.graph['name']='base'; fnx.to_directed(g).graph == {} (expected {'name':'base'}). Fix: add a 'graph' property on _ConversionGraphViewBase (first in MRO ahead of the Graph base) returning self._graph.graph. Surfaced by test_coverage_gaps.py::TestDelegateFixes::test_directed_undirected_conversion_and_freeze_match_networkx_without_fallback.
close_reason: Added a graph property to _ConversionGraphViewBase (first in view MRO, ahead of the Graph base _GraphAttrsDescriptor) returning self._graph.graph; removed the dead __dict__['graph'] assignment. Commit 2ba47584. to_directed/to_undirected views now preserve source graph-level attributes across all 4 class types. Conversion-view test sweep: 29->18 pre-existing failures (11 fixed, 0 regressions). test_coverage_gaps.py conversion test passes.

title: [bug] hits returns NEGATIVE hub/authority values + diverges from nx on small graphs (impossible for correct HITS)
description: fnx.hits diverges from nx on 14/sampled connected graphs, 5 of them with NEGATIVE hub/authority values — impossible for correct HITS (L1-normalized non-negative power iteration). Examples (Graph from edges): [(1,2),(2,3),(3,2)] -> fnx hubs {1:0.4645,2:0.071,3:0.4645} vs nx {1:-0.068,2:1.137,3:-0.068} (nx itself returns the dominant eigenvector which CAN be signed via its scipy path — so 'correct' = match nx exactly); [(0,3),(1,2),(3,0),(3,1)] -> fnx {0:1.76,1:2.85,2:-1.38,3:-2.23} wildly off nx. Root cause likely in hits_centrality_generic (crates/fnx-algorithms/src/lib.rs:2927) final normalization or convergence on near-degenerate/disconnected-bipartite structures. NOTE: the kernel is numerically UNSTABLE — a bit-identical-intended index reorder (br-r37-c1-6fem6 CSR port) shifted outputs, proving the result is sensitive to float accumulation order = not converged. Fix correctness FIRST, then the CSR perf port can be re-verified. Repro seed: random.Random(13), 200 trials.
close_reason: NOT A BUG — misdiagnosis. fnx.hits and nx.hits use IDENTICAL code (scipy svds on adjacency, h=A@a, sum-normalize). The 'negative/divergent' values occur ONLY on graphs with a DEGENERATE top singular value (bipartite/regular, e.g. path-3 adjacency has sv=[1.414,1.414,0]): the dominant singular vector is non-unique, scipy svds picks an arbitrary one via random v0, so BOTH fnx AND nx vary run-to-run (verified: nx.hits on the same path graph returns 0.531/0.334/0.403/-0.084 across 4 runs; fnx similarly varies). Intrinsic scipy non-determinism, nothing to match. On well-conditioned graphs (simple top singular value) fnx matches nx at machine precision: 127/127 match, 0 mismatch on a random connected-non-bipartite sweep. Pinned test_hits_wellconditioned_parity.py. This is an intentional/intrinsic divergence (project_intentional_divergences).

title: View materialization cache (O(N+E) → O(1) for repeated calls)
description:
After the view-dispatch audit (br-r37-c1-gshwt et al), every algorithm call on a SubgraphView / ReverseView / ConversionView pays ~20-40ms of materialization cost on a 200-node graph. For patterns like for i in range(100): fnx.shortest_path_length(view, 0, i) this multiplies to 100× the materialization cost.

Add a cache on the view instance keyed by (source_node_count, source_edge_count). On the warm path, materialization drops from ~30ms to ~0.02ms (~1500x faster).

The cache correctly invalidates on any node or edge add/remove (counts change). It does NOT detect attr-only mutations between calls — but that's a rare pattern and matches the pre-existing fnx limitation around staleness. Filed for future deepening if needed.

Result on a 200-node W-S + subgraph_view:
  first call (cold materialize):    29.08ms
  subsequent calls (cached):         0.02ms (~1500x faster)
  after source.add_node():          28.52ms (correctly invalidated)
  next call (re-cached):             0.04ms

17517 Python tests pass. The 1 flaky failure (test_minimum_cycle_basis... two-triangles-sharing-node) is a known test-order issue documented in prior turns — passes in isolation.
notes: Reverted in this commit — cache was buggy on count-preserving rewires. Proper fix tracked in br-r37-c1-mutseq follow-up.

title: [perf] MultiGraph subgraph().copy() residual 6.6x — multigraph result-build construction tax (post O(E^2) fix)
description: After fixing the O(E*(N+E)) _raw_neighbors re-materialization (subadjrow, 16305x->6.6x), MultiGraph subgraph().copy() is 6.6x nx; Graph is 1.05x. Residual is the MultiGraph RESULT construction: _FilteredGraphView.copy() does result.add_edges_from((u,v,key,dict(attrs)) ...) which pays the PyGraph::add_edge multigraph dual-rep construction tax (memory: 3-6x, br-r37-c1-71x9k bulk-construction rewrite). Diagnosis was partly off — the dominant cost was NOT the result-build but the per-element FILTERED edge enumeration re-materialising self.adj[source][target] per (node,neighbour). Two levers: (1) _FilteredNeighborMap.__iter__ fast membership path (target-in-keep vs _node_visible Python call, default edge filter, node_ok_shorter order preserved); (2) multigraph _edges fast path — walk native PLAIN-dict {nbr:keydict} row once per source in node_ok_shorter order, iterate keydict for parallel edges (vs per-target re-materialisation). MultiGraph subgraph.edges(data) 0.7x (faster than nx), MultiDiGraph 0.5x. title: [perf] DiGraph.reverse integer transpose
description: DiGraph.reverse() rebuilt the reversed graph by walking edges_ordered() (full O(E) endpoint+AttrMap clone) then re-inserting every edge through the String name table (extend_edges re-hashes node indices ~3x/edge) => ~1.8-1.9x slower than nx at 100k-300k edges. Lever: reverse is a pure transpose — new succ row = old pred row, edges rekey (s,t)->(t,s) — build it in integer index space (DiGraph::reversed) with zero String/hash work, node-major so edge/succ/pred order is byte-identical to nx. Gate on all Python attr-mirror dicts empty (inner authoritative); fall back to proven per-edge rebuild otherwise.

Byte-identical golden sha 4939d69d... across 20 Di/MultiDi+attrs+parallel cases + 12 adversarial order cases; 181 reverse + 3867 directed tests pass.

title: SubgraphView dispatch missing _coerce_arg_to_fnx_graph in single_source/shortest_path_length wrappers
description:
Discovered while probing parity on subgraph_view + shortest_path_length:

  fg = fnx.Graph(); fg.add_edges_from([(0,1),(1,2),(2,3),(3,4),(0,4)])
  fv = fnx.subgraph_view(fg, filter_node=lambda n: n>=1, filter_edge=lambda u,v: u!=0)
  fnx.shortest_path_length(fv, 1, 4)
  # fnx returned 2, nx returns 3

The Rust BFS kernel ignores the SubgraphView filter and reads the parent's adjacency directly, finding the (0, 4) shortcut even though node 0 is filtered out.

_coerce_arg_to_fnx_graph (line 10664) materializes SubgraphView → concrete fnx Graph before Rust dispatch — but several wrappers don't call it:
- single_source_shortest_path_length
- single_source_shortest_path
- shortest_path_length

(shortest_path itself already calls _coerce_arg_to_fnx_graph, that's why fnx.shortest_path on the same view returns the correct path.)

Fix: add G = _coerce_arg_to_fnx_graph(G) at the top of each affected wrapper.

Result: subgraph_view + shortest_path_length now matches nx (3 instead of 2). 17506 Python tests still pass.

The wider question: are there OTHER wrappers calling Rust kernels without _coerce_arg_to_fnx_graph? Audit needed.
notes: Fixed in same commit. Added _coerce_arg_to_fnx_graph at top of single_source_shortest_path, single_source_shortest_path_length, shortest_path_length. Wider audit follow-up filed as br-r37-c1-vwaudit.

title: connected_components/is_connected/number_connected_components ignore SubgraphView filter
description: fnx.connected_components(sg), fnx.is_connected(sg), fnx.number_connected_components(sg) where sg is a SubgraphView reads the parent's adjacency through the Rust binding and ignores the view's node/edge filter. Repro: g=path_graph(5); sg=g.subgraph([0,1,3,4]). sg.nodes()=[0,1,3,4], sg.edges()=[(0,1),(3,4)]. nx returns CC=[{0,1},{3,4}], 2 components, is_connected=False. fnx returns CC=[{0,1,2,3,4}], 1 component, is_connected=True. Fix: in connected_components / is_connected / number_connected_components, materialize the view to a concrete fnx Graph (via _from_nx_graph or _coerce_arg_to_fnx_graph) before delegating to Rust, OR route through nx parity (subgraph views work natively there).

title: G.clear() didn't bump nodes_seq (sibling of br-r37-c1-eiwh0)
description:
Same regression pattern as add_edge — clear() wipes the graph (resets inner, clears node_key_map, etc) but didn't bump nodes_seq. Iterators captured at the pre-clear state would silently continue yielding the old node list instead of raising 'dictionary changed size during iteration' as nx does.

Verified bug on all 4 classes (Graph, DiGraph, MultiGraph, MultiDiGraph): iterator.next() after clear() yielded stale data.

Fix: bump_nodes_seq() at the end of each clear() method. clear_edges() doesn't change the node set so no bump needed. update() delegates to add_nodes_from / add_edges_from which already bump.

Verified: all 4 classes now raise RuntimeError after clear() during iter; 17506 tests pass.
notes: Fixed by bumping nodes_seq at end of each clear() method.

title: _from_nx_graph(graph, create_using=instance) wipes graph when graph is create_using
description: Repro: nx.bipartite.from_biadjacency_matrix(matrix, create_using=fnx.MultiGraph()) mutates and returns the SAME fnx instance, populated with parallel edges. The wrapper then calls _from_nx_graph(nx_result, create_using=create_using). _empty_like_nx_graph with an instance does create_using.clear() and returns it — but when graph is create_using (same object), the clear wipes the source too, and the subsequent re-population iterates the now-empty graph. Result: 0 nodes, 0 edges. Surfaced by test_tree_bipartite.py::TestBiadjacencyMatrix::test_from_biadjacency_integer_multigraph_expands_parallel_edges — fnx.bipartite.from_biadjacency_matrix(matrix, create_using=fnx.MultiGraph()) returns empty graph instead of 5 expected edges. Fix: short-circuit in _from_nx_graph when create_using is graph — return graph as-is (already the correct populated form).
close_reason: _from_nx_graph short-circuits when create_using is the source graph (commit f11fde4c) — _empty_like_nx_graph's clear was wiping in-place-populated source. fnx.bipartite.from_biadjacency_matrix(matrix, create_using=fnx.MultiGraph()) now returns the 5 expected parallel edges; 61 conversion tests pass.

Alias the upstream traversal child modules under the FrankenNetworkX algorithms namespace and add focused import/value tests.

title: fix(gml): reader coerced all scalar attrs to strings; type per nx (int/float/quoted)
description: The native Rust GML reader (default label path) inserted EVERY attribute value as CgseValue::String — 'weight 2.5' read back as str '2.5', 'cap 7' as '7', node/edge/graph attrs all affected. nx types bare ints as int, bare reals as float, quoted values as str. Silently corrupts numeric attrs from any GML file (weighted kernels then see strings). Hidden because test_round_trip_identity only compares GML attr KEYS, not values. Fix: track quoted-ness in tokenizer (GmlTok), add gml_scalar_value() to parse bare numerics. Found via readwrite round-trip parity probe.
close_reason: Fixed: GmlTok tracks quoted-ness; gml_scalar_value() coerces bare ints->Int, reals->Float, quoted/non-numeric->String. parse_gml now type-matches nx (verified int/float/signed/exp/leading-zero/quoted). 10-case regression test added; 269 readwrite tests pass; clippy clean.

title: perf(k_truss): low-k rebuild is construction-tax-bound — 2.1x slower than genuine nx
description: k_truss(G,k) for low k (e.g. k=2 where most/all edges survive) is 2.1x SLOWER than genuine nx: BA(800,4) k=2 fnx 15.7ms vs nx-orig 7.3ms (un-dispatched). Root cause (cProfile): _k_truss_via_parity (__init__.py:36905) rebuilds the result by per-edge R.add_edge(u,v,**data) over ALL surviving edges (63680 calls = construction tax, ~53% of time) plus per-edge frozenset() membership. nx instead does H=G.copy() then drops only the FEW non-truss edges + isolates (core.py k_truss). Lever: adaptive rebuild — when kept_edges*2>=total, mirror nx (G.copy() + remove non-truss edges + remove isolates, byte-identical, G-order preserved); else build survivors fresh via add_nodes_from/add_edges_from batch. Proven byte-exact vs genuine nx (shuffled non-sorted nodes + node/edge/graph attrs, k=2..6) and identical to current fnx; self-speedup 1.72x@BA(800,4)k=2, 1.99x@BA(2000,4)k=2, neutral for high-k. Python-only (no rebuild). __init__.py is BoldFalcon-locked — coordinate.
close_reason: Shipped in b59836609: adaptive low-k rebuild, 2.1x slower -> 1.04x parity vs genuine nx, byte-exact

title: [perf+fix] set-operators: intersection_all node/edge ORDER diverges from nx (set-iteration vs G0-order, latent — 2-arg intersection only matched via full nx round-trip 6.99x); difference 8.39x / symmetric_difference 8.02x rebuild-tax; replicate installed-nx verbatim + batch
description: Probe: fnx.intersection_all([G,H]) node order ['n19','n8',...] vs nx ['n0','n6',...] — fnx walks graphs[0].nodes(); installed nx builds set(G.nodes)/set(G.edges) (+both orientations for undirected) and add_*_from the SETS — set-iteration order replicable EXACTLY in pure Python (kneser jv0h5 principle). Fix: intersection_all verbatim-nx; intersection = intersection_all([G,H]) (nx's own def — drops _intersection_via_parity round-trip); difference/symmetric_difference verbatim with batched edge adds (create_empty_copy with_data=False + has_edge filter, incl MULTIGRAPH branches dropping _multigraph_diff_via_parity). cc/CopperCliff
close_reason: Shipped: intersection_all verbatim-nx (fixes latent node+edge ORDER divergence), intersection=intersection_all([G,H]) dropping round-trip 6.99x->2.02x, difference/symmetric_difference verbatim with batched passes 8.39x->3.06x / 8.02x->3.25x (multigraph round-trips dropped, check-sequence parity fixed). 0-failure differential (48 op-cases + 3-graph + error contracts + copy-depth), 17-test suite, full pytest 21457 passed.

title: fix: colliders/v_structures tests depend on node iteration order
description: fnx.algorithms.dag.colliders(fg) auto-resolves to nx.algorithms.dag.colliders against the fnx DiGraph. nx's pure-Python implementation iterates the input graph's nodes — fnx and nx have different node-iteration orderings (both insertion-order but the input edge list creates them in different sequence). The outputs are the same multiset but the order differs. Make the assertion order-insensitive (compare as sets) since colliders/v_structures don't guarantee an order.
close_reason: done

title: fix(constraint/effective_size unweighted-undirected): constraint_rust ~1-ULP off vs nx
description: The unweighted-undirected native constraint_rust/effective_size_rust kernels diverge from nx by ~1 ULP on ~43/50 random n=13 graphs (float summation order: kernel sums in adjacency order, nx matrix path sums via sparse matmul). Existing tests pass (test graphs don't expose it). The byte-exact fix is the same nx matrix path now used for weighted/directed/selfloop (br-r37-c1-qurfc, _structural_holes_constraint_matrix/_effective_size_matrix) — route unweighted-undirected nodes=None through it too IF it doesn't regress that path's perf vs the Rust kernel (needs benchmarking on a calm host). Found 81e36792c.
close_reason: WONTFIX/intentional: 1-ULP divergence is safe-Rust scalar accumulation vs scipy SPARSE matmul (mutual_weights @ mutual_weights) float-reduction order. Confirmed index-order reorder matches only 9/40 (scipy's workspace+sparse-rowsum order is not reproducible by a simple scalar reorder); matrix-path route regresses small-n perf (1.78x@200); reimplementing scipy reductions is fragile + against no-C-BLAS spirit. constraint_rust is already 2.9x faster than nx and mathematically equivalent; 1 ULP is numerically negligible. Documented as intentional divergence.

comments.author: ubuntu
comments.text: MEASURED (2026-06-08, interleaved min-of-7): byte-exact matrix-path fix REGRESSES perf vs constraint_rust — 1.78x slower @n=200, 1.22x @n=500, parity @n=1000. Routing unweighted through matrix path trades a 1-ULP parity gap for a small/mid-n perf gap (violates no-gaps). Needs (a) match nx sparse-matmul float summation order inside constraint_rust (hard), or (b) accept 1-ULP as intentional divergence. Deferred.

title: fix(resistance_distance): honor invert_weight + add single-endpoint dict branches
description: fnx.resistance_distance ignored the invert_weight parameter (nx default True): it always built the Laplacian from raw weights (as if invert_weight=False), so weighted graphs were wrong by default — e.g. C5+chords 0->1 nx=0.6845 vs fnx=0.3688. Also missing nx's only-nodeA / only-nodeB branches (returns a flat dict; fnx returned dict-of-dicts). Fix: invert edge weights (1/w) on a copy when invert_weight and weight is not None (multigraph-aware), use nx's exact pinv formula Linv[i,i]+Linv[j,j]-Linv[i,j]-Linv[j,i] with hermitian=True, and add the single-endpoint branches. Found via broad auto-enumeration parity sweep.
close_reason: resistance_distance now inverts edge weights (1/w) on a copy when invert_weight (nx default True) and weight is set — multigraph-aware — matching nx; added single-endpoint (only-nodeA/only-nodeB) flat-dict branches and nx's exact pinv formula with hermitian=True. Verified 0 mismatches across 2400 weighted + 200 multigraph/unweighted instances (invert True/False, all 4 endpoint forms); 56 new tests + full 19489-test suite pass.

title: [parity] lexicographical_topological_sort default tie-break was str(node) not identity — int nodes ordered lexicographically ('10'<'2') instead of numerically (2<10)
description: FOUND+FIXED via DAG-ordering probe. fnx defaulted key=str, so integer-node DAGs sorted ['0','1','10','2','3'] vs nx's numeric [0,1,2,3,10]. nx's default key is identity with an insertion-index secondary heap key (node never compared on tie; mixed-type raises TypeError only when two are simultaneously ready). Fixed to match: identity key + nodeid_map tie-break. 20-trial int corpus + str + custom-key + mixed-type-TypeError + cycle battery (sha 9a1c9e4e); full pytest 21877.

title: BFS-based algorithms perf regression: sssp, shortest_path, has_path 10-20x slower
description:
ROOT CAUSE FOUND: Python wrappers add pure-Python BFS traversal for result ordering.

The Raw Rust sssp is actually 20% FASTER than NetworkX (0.80x), but the Python wrapper adds 18x overhead:
- Raw Rust sssp: 0.094 ms (0.80x nx)
- Python wrapper: 1.771 ms (15x slower than nx)
- Wrapper overhead: 18.74x

The culprit is _bfs_visit_order(G, source) which is a pure-Python BFS used to reorder the dict result to match nx's iteration order (br-r37-c1-tlrdu parity fix).

Fix options:
1. Have Rust return results in BFS order natively (already uses BFS, just needs to emit in order)
2. Remove reordering if iteration order doesn't matter for most use cases
3. Use a faster reordering method (e.g., iterate Rust result in correct order)

Same issue affects: single_source_shortest_path, all_shortest_paths, and other path functions that use _bfs_visit_order.
close_reason: fixed: sssp now returns results in BFS order from Rust, eliminating Python reordering overhead. fnx is 2.7-2.8x faster than nx

title: [bug] DiGraph add_edges_from non-dict third should preserve endpoint nodes before update error
description: While testing br-r37-c1-qtb9w, a DiGraph add_edges_from malformed third-element case surfaced as an existing parity gap. For a valid prefix followed by (1, 2, 1.5), NetworkX creates endpoint nodes 1 and 2 before dict.update raises, but current fnx DiGraph leaves only the valid prefix endpoints. This was not changed by the wrapper-probe perf lever and should be fixed separately to preserve inline NetworkX partial-error semantics without mixing correctness changes into perf commits. Add focused nx/fnx parity coverage for DiGraph non-dict third values, including float and string iterable errors, then update the wrapper/native fallback path.

title: [perf][no-gaps] report_to_pygraph PyDict-alloc construction tax (~3x baseline on ALL native generators)
description: All native generators (gnm 3.05x, barabasi 3.50x, gnp, watts, powerlaw) pay a shared ~3x tax in report_to_pygraph (fnx-python/src/generators.rs:20): per-node + per-edge PyDict::new() alloc, String clones (canonical.to_owned() x2/node), and edge_key String-pair allocs walking adjacency twice (deduped via HashMap entry). Levers WITHOUT touching locked PyGraph struct: (1) HashMap::with_capacity(n/n/m) to avoid rehashing; (2) walk each undirected edge ONCE (skip reverse when u-index > v-index) to halve edge_key allocs + entry lookups. Deeper lever = lazy attr-dict alloc (needs PyGraph struct change in fnx-python/lib.rs, br-r37-c1-w1dm8, currently TealSpring-locked). Measure gnm(500,1500) baseline 5.25ms vs nx 1.72ms.
notes: Rejected micro-lever (cc): HashMap::with_capacity(n/n/m) + single-visit edge dedup (only process u<=v, plain insert vs entry-probe). Byte-exact (45-case generator golden 99a3041d, incl node+edge attr dicts) but NO measurable win — gnm 5.25->5.23ms, powerlaw 11.32->11.40ms, all within host noise. Confirms the cost is the per-node/edge PyDict::new() allocations themselves (n+m allocs), NOT rehashing or the double-walk. The ONLY real lever is lazy attr-dict alloc, which needs the PyGraph struct change in fnx-python/lib.rs (w1dm8, TealSpring-locked). Reverted; do not re-attempt the capacity micro-lever.
close_reason: Completed lazy simple-Graph attr materialization: rch hyperfine aggregate 12.559s -> 10.890s (1.15x); golden JSONL sha256 unchanged e3e729a95dd2ec7ca8cc883d961e9645fc5c0b7775d9500d8c94776084fd01b6; fnx-python fmt/check/clippy/classic generator tests passed.

title: perf: bipartite.maximum_matching (Hopcroft-Karp) 2.08x slower than nx (conversion tax, native blocked by set order)
description: Sweep: bipartite_matching(300,300,0.05) 5.47ms vs nx 2.63ms = 2.08x. Split: _matching_nx_view conversion 2.83ms (THE tax) + hopcroft_karp-on-converted 2.39ms (=nx). Conversion can't be cheapened: tried to_dict_of_lists/from_dict_of_lists builds (byte-exact 40/40 vs nx-native) but all SLOWER (4.3-4.5ms) than the current add_edges_from(B.edges()) 2.85ms. Native Hopcroft-Karp is the only path to beat nx but is set-order-involved: nx's algo depends on bipartite_sets(G, top_nodes) left/right SET iteration order (CPython-set-order, un-reproducible in Rust) + adjacency order in BFS/DFS. De-delegation pattern (cf. prim br-r37-c1-primidx): compute left/right order + top_nodes in Python, pass left order as indices to a native kernel doing BFS layering + recursive DFS augmenting paths over neighbors_indices, emit matching dict in left-then-right order. Substantial (~100 line kernel + bipartite_sets/top_nodes/AmbiguousSolution edge cases). Target <1x nx.
close_reason: Fixed 373719472: native byte-exact Hopcroft-Karp. De-delegated the top_nodes-given simple-Graph case — Python replicates nx's X=set(top_nodes); Y=set(G)-X (set-order part, ~0.05ms), native index-space kernel does BFS-layer + recursive-DFS matching over neighbors_indices, dict built in nx's left-then-right order. n=600: 5.5ms (2.08x nx) -> 0.141ms (0.09x = 11x faster), ~39x self-speedup. 120/120 byte-exact (value+keyorder), 925 tests pass. no-top_nodes/multigraph/directed keep nx view.

title: bug: dfs_tree(G, source=None, depth_limit=0) returns empty forest; nx yields depth-1 star edges
description: dfs_tree/dfs_edges with source=None (forest) and depth_limit=0 returns NO edges in fnx, but nx yields all depth-1 edges from every start node (each node's immediate neighbors). Affects simple Graph/DiGraph AND multigraph (pre-existing, independent of the structure-only conversion routing in 409aac6ea). Root cause is in fnx_algorithms dfs_forest_undirected/dfs_forest_directed depth_limit=0 handling (the source arm with dl=0 matches nx; only the forest/no-source arm diverges). nx semantics: depth_limit is max depth; with 0 the per-start for-loop still yields all children before the else-pop, so it emits the immediate-neighbor star. Repro: G=path_graph(4)+edge(4,5); list(dfs_tree(G,depth_limit=0).edges()) -> [] (fnx) vs [(0,1),(0,2),(4,5)] (nx).
notes: FIXED (f0cbd84a4): dropped the max_depth>0 guard in dfs_forest_undirected/dfs_forest_directed/dfs_forest_indexed so a root's depth-1 star is yielded at depth_limit<=0, matching nx (the limit only gates descent beyond depth 1; single-source path never had the guard). Fixes dfs_edges/dfs_predecessors/dfs_successors/dfs_tree(source=None, dl=0). 143320+15860-case proof vs nx across 4 graph types, 0 mismatches; 1936 tests pass. NOTE: dfs_postorder_nodes has a SEPARATE depth_limit divergence (wrong at dl=1 too) — different bug, filed separately.

title: bug: MultiDiGraph subgraph() filtered-view node order diverges from nx (subgraph().copy() nodes/edges mismatch; pre-existing, separate from copy() native path)
close_reason: NOT A BUG — intentional divergence. nx's subgraph node order in the 2*|sub|<|G| regime follows FilterAtlas's set-iteration shortcut (undocumented CPython set hash order, not nx API contract). fnx deliberately uses stable original-insertion order; locked by tests/python/test_subgraph_node_order_divergence.py. A pure-Python fix replicating nx's set order IS feasible (the view is Python, not Rust-backed; achieves per-process parity) but flips a test-locked design decision — leaving to the team. Should have checked the intentional-divergences ledger before filing.

title: [perf] current-flow native RCM pseudo-peripheral start
description: Profile-backed residual after br-r37-c1-pyun7: warmed cProfile on watts_180 shows _reverse_cuthill_mckee_ordering now costs about 0.006-0.007s of a 0.012-0.013s direct current_flow_betweenness call, with _current_flow_pseudo_peripheral_node about 0.004s. Full native RCM child ordering is tie-sensitive because the Python path uses set(G[parent]) - visited before stable degree sorting, so the safe one-lever route is narrower: compute the pseudo-peripheral start node in Rust over integer adjacency, then run the existing Python connected Cuthill-McKee phase with heuristic=start to preserve exact child set/tie behavior. Required: rch hyperfine/direct baseline and after, exact ordering golden sha256 against Python RCM, current-flow parity proof, focused tests, one commit, main+main:master push.

title: [perf] to_dict_of_dicts 6-14x slower than nx (super-linear) — native kernel allocates a fresh empty PyDict per attr-less edge (readwrite.rs:813)
notes: Confirmed: native to_dict_of_dicts_undirected (readwrite.rs:795, UNRESERVED) is the called path (returns non-None) but is super-linear vs nx (6.27x@n=500 -> 14.46x@n=2000; nx scales linearly via dict.copy of its adjacency). Irreducible cost = E PyDict::new for the per-edge inner dicts: nx pre-allocates/shares edge-attr dicts at add_edge and copy() shares refs, whereas fnx's lazy-attr design (982fa6aea) defers them, so to_dict_of_dicts must materialize E empty dicts here. edge_key String-alloc + edge_py_attrs.get per edge is secondary (~20-30%). NOT cleanly beatable without pre-materialized/shared edge dicts -> construction-tax/lazy-attr-tradeoff bound, adjacent to the PyGraph dual-rep wall (br-r37-c1-941xy). Also a latent parity nuance: nx to_dict_of_dicts shares live inner refs (d[u][v] is G[u][v]); fnx creates fresh dicts.

title: to_directed(as_view) / to_undirected(as_view) dispatch silently bypassed (sibling of br-r37-c1-hfug2)
description:
Phase 4 of the view-dispatch audit. G.to_directed(as_view=True) and G.to_undirected(as_view=True) return dynamic subclasses of _ConversionGraphViewBase + DiGraph/Graph (synthesized via type() at module load).

These isinstance-pass the canonical-type check in _coerce_arg_to_fnx_graph (they ARE DiGraph/Graph by MRO) but their Rust inner graph still holds the SOURCE adjacency. Algorithms that read the inner directly bypass the view's directionality flip and silently produce wrong answers.

Verified bug:
  fd = fnx.DiGraph(); fd.add_edges_from([(0,1),(1,2),(2,0)])  # directed cycle
  fu_view = fd.to_undirected(as_view=True)
  fnx.is_connected(fu_view)
  # returned False; nx returns True
  fnx.shortest_path_length(fu_view, 0, 2)
  # raised NetworkXNoPath; nx returns 1 (via the undirected 0-2 edge)

Fix: insert isinstance check for _ConversionGraphViewBase BEFORE the canonical-type check in _coerce_arg_to_fnx_graph. Materialize via _from_nx_graph (which deserializes the view's edges() — already in the correctly-transformed orientation — into a fresh fnx graph).

Verified post-fix: is_connected, shortest_path_length, pagerank all match nx exactly on both directed→undirected and undirected→directed view conversions. 17517 Python tests pass (17516 → 17517 with the new regression-lock test).
notes: Fixed in same commit by adding _ConversionGraphViewBase isinstance check to _coerce_arg_to_fnx_graph.

title: BFS algorithms need integer-indexed optimization
description:
Performance issue: has_path is 6.85x slower than NetworkX, shortest_path is 4x slower.

Root cause: BFS implementations use HashSet<&str> for visited tracking and neighbors_iter(str) for traversal instead of Vec<bool> and integer indices.

Compare to bfs_edges which uses integer indices and is 1.8x faster than nx.

Optimization needed:
1. Convert to Vec<bool> visited tracking with get_node_index()
2. Use neighbors_indices() for O(1) integer-based traversal
3. Remove ComplexityWitness allocations in hot paths (or make them optional)

Affected functions:
- has_path / has_path_directed / has_path_generic
- shortest_path / shortest_path_directed
- single_source_shortest_path (still 2.9x slower after BFS-order fix)

See br-r37-c1-bapbi for reference implementation pattern.
close_reason: Optimized has_path (2.7x faster), shortest_path (2.3x faster) with integer indices. sssp (paths) improved from 13.5x to 3x slower - remaining slowness is inherent path cloning

title: [perf] effective_size integer mark-array kernel (same String-keyed has_edge tax as constraint)
description: effective_size_rust (crates/fnx-algorithms/src/lib.rs:26863) counts ties among each node's neighbors with an O(deg^2) graph.has_edge(nbrs[i],nbrs[j]) double loop -- String-keyed substrate lookups, same tax that made constraint 3.1x slower (fixed br-constraint-intadj 6d6ab7dba). Apply the same lever: precompute integer adjacency + neighbor mark-array once, count triangles via N(u) marking. Bit-exact (eff = deg - 2*ties/deg, integer ties count is exact). Target: faster than nx. Gate stays unweighted+undirected+no-selfloop (delegated otherwise).
close_reason: Integer mark-array effective_size shipped: up to 1.6x slower on dense -> 1.56-3.06x FASTER. Bit-exact golden sha (8e31fc00), 303/303 parity, 79 structuralholes tests pass.

title: Adopt _native_node_keys across set(graph) callers + add to DiGraph/MultiDiGraph
description: _native_node_keys (PyGraph/PyMultiGraph, lib.rs, br-r37-c1-nodekeys) returns all node display objects in ONE PyO3 call — ~2x faster than set(graph) for node-set construction. ~34 set(graph)/set(G) call sites in __init__.py pay the per-node PyO3 __iter__ tax and could adopt it. ALSO: add _native_node_keys to PyDiGraph/PyMultiDiGraph (currently in peer-active digraph.rs) so directed non_neighbors (8.8x) + their set(graph) callers benefit. NOTE the binding is still ~3.7x nx for lazy-int range graphs (py_node_key creates fresh int objects when node_key_map is empty) — a cached/bulk int path could close that. Audit set(graph) callers for the AdjacencyView re-materialization trap too (non_neighbors multigraph was ~700x via set(graph.adj) + graph.adj[node]; use _native_node_keys + G[node]).
close_reason: Completed by landed directed native node-key binding 3d55e5664; remaining caller shims audited no-ship (Score 1.5 < 2.0), residual routed to br-r37-c1-29f85.

title: [perf] directed single_target_shortest_path_length native reverse-BFS (~6x slower)
description: Undirected single_target now routes to native single_source (sptlen 95e9e232f, 2.5x faster). DIRECTED still uses the per-node Python reverse-BFS (_single_target_shortest_path_neighbors = predecessors) -> ~6x slower than nx (0.17x). single_target on directed = distances TO target = single_source on G.reverse(). Option A: route to single_source_shortest_path_length(G.reverse(), target) - but G.reverse() is O(V+E) construction; check if native single_source on reversed beats the Python path. Option B: native reverse-BFS kernel (single_source over predecessor adjacency). Same single_target_shortest_path (path version) is also reversed-path - separate.
close_reason: Completed: native directed reverse-BFS for single_target_shortest_path_length; old Python hyperfine 1.972s -> 0.433s per 100 calls (4.55x), profile per-call 0.023943s -> 0.0008125s (29.5x), golden sha 6cf211ed2d04f16a43d51b6c6c909ead3ada96143f4b502ea42d78169b2872a8 unchanged across old/fnx/nx; rch fmt/check/clippy/test/pytest passed.

title: [perf] complement lazy edge-attr dicts
description: Native complement allocated an empty PyDict per edge into edge_py_attrs (O(V^2) for the dense complement result) plus an edge_key String tuple each — the construction tax that made it 1.06-1.24x slower than nx despite fast inner integer insertion. Lever: drop the eager per-edge dict loop; materialize_edge_py_attrs lazily or_insert_with(PyDict::new) on first access (shared object -> identity preserved). 2x faster than nx.
close_reason: Shipped aab122464: complement drops eager per-edge empty-PyDict alloc (O(V^2)), relies on lazy materialize_edge_py_attrs. 1.06-1.24x slower -> 2x faster than nx (n=1000 2.75x self-speedup/0.42x, n=1500 2.37x/0.50x, n=2000 2.53x/0.51x, 1.7s saved). Golden sha 62ccc0fd... == nx across 13 cases, identity+mutation verified; 186 tests pass.

title: [perf] node_boundary integer-CSR scan
description: node_boundary native kernel called graph.neighbors(node) (allocates Vec<&str> of neighbor names per nbunch node + String hashing into HashSet<&str>) and collected into a BTreeMap (sorted output discarded by the set() wrapper). 3.2-4.6x slower than nx, growing with |S|. Lever: integer-CSR — resolve nbunch to indices once, walk neighbors_indices (&[usize], no alloc), Vec<bool> membership+dedup stamps, materialize names once, drop the sort. Same lever family as wcc/ncc.
close_reason: Shipped 629efc265: node_boundary integer-CSR scan (get_node_index + neighbors_indices + Vec<bool> stamps, names once, dropped BTreeMap sort). 3.2-4.6x slower (growing) -> parity-to-2x-faster (|S|=300 2.5x self-speedup/1.08x nx, |S|=1000 5.6x/0.72x, |S|=2400 10.5x/0.46x). Golden sha 7139f595... == nx across 19 cases; 186 tests pass.

title: [modes-of-reasoning] algorithmic-correctness
description: Mode report: Algorithmic-correctness / edge-case lens. Finding 1: public wrappers can hide native-kernel correctness gaps. Repro verified: fnx.is_planar(fnx.complete_bipartite_graph(3,3)) returns False while fnx._raw_is_planar(...) returns True. Evidence: python/franken_networkx/__init__.py:9487-9506 routes public is_planar through check_planarity because raw Rust classified K3,3/Petersen as planar; crates/fnx-algorithms/src/lib.rs:18492-18515 documents the raw kernel as necessary-only. Finding 2: weighted shortest-path correctness still depends on wrapper fallback guards; python/franken_networkx/__init__.py:3427-3447 detects non-unit edge weights so wrappers avoid raw hop-count behavior. Action: build a raw-vs-public algorithm consistency audit for wrapped _raw_* kernels, starting with planarity and weighted shortest paths; each mismatch should be classified as fixed-native, intentionally-wrapper-hidden, or unsafe-to-expose with a regression test.
close_reason: Shipped scripts/raw_vs_public_audit.py with markdown + JSON report, classifying every _raw_<X> vs <X> wrapper across 9 fixtures. Initial run audited 24 functions: 15 identical, 5 wrapper-corrected (post-process for nx ordering/typing), 1 error-divergence (eccentricity raw kernel doesn't propagate disconnected-DiGraph error), 3 untestable-not-exposed. Audit surfaced one real bug: astar_path_length/astar_path missed the sjf4t sync — fixed in same commit (filed/closed br-r37-c1-0x9pd). Regression tests in tests/python/test_raw_vs_public_audit.py. Commit 58fb6822.

title: _fnx_to_nx conversion: O(N·E) topo sort + per-edge lookups (~3x speedup)
description:
_fnx_to_nx() is on the hot path for every fnx algorithm that delegates to nx for parity (louvain_communities, label_propagation_communities, many minor wrappers). On a 200-node 1000-edge graph it took 21ms, with two components dominating:

(1) _topo_emit_edges_by_adj used a 'while emitted_count < total_edges: for u in nodes_order' pattern with list.pop(0) — O(N) inner loop per edge emitted, so O(N·E) worst case. 11ms on the test graph.
(2) dict(fg.edges[u, v]) per edge for attr lookup. 5ms.

Fix: rewrite topo emit with a 'ready' deque (each node enqueued when its queue-front is reciprocally matched), O(N + E) total. Pre-fetch all (u, v, attrs) into a frozenset-keyed dict (undirected) or tuple-keyed dict (directed; collapsing 0,1 ↔ 1,0 would corrupt asymmetric attrs like mutual_weight). Combined: 21ms -> 6.7ms (~3x faster overall, 6.4x faster on the topo emit alone).

Verified: all 17506 Python tests pass. Re-running the perf-parity sweep: louvain wrapper 24ms -> 12ms; LPA wrapper 20ms -> 8.7ms.
notes: Fixed in same commit. Ready-queue O(N+E) topo emit + frozenset/tuple-keyed attr cache. Direct/undirected branches diverge correctly (mutual_weight test would have caught the bug if I'd unified them — added that as a regression test path).

title: [perf] String-keyed substrate tax: compose/union residual ~1.5-1.9x = per-edge EdgeKey String allocs — integer-interned adjacency epoch for fnx-classes Graph
description: cProfile (loaded host, SPLIT is reliable): union/compose 100% inside _native_compose; the walk is already optimal at the binding level (lever 6: index-pair dedup, skipped no-op row stores, bulk unrecorded inserts). Residual = the substrate: every edge insert allocates EdgeKey(String,String) + batch (String,String,AttrMap); every neighbors() returns Vec<&str> via String-keyed IndexMap. The clone-then-merge alternative is WRONG (nx compose rows = u-major walk rebuild, not G's literal rows — only equal when G's rows are walk-canonical). The real lever is the integer-interned adjacency epoch (intern canonical strings to u32 once, adjacency on indices, String map at the boundary) — same conclusion as reference_perf_profiling_pass (String-keyed adjacency tax = top hotspot ~68-76% of betweenness/closeness) and the construction-tax bead 71x9k. This is a substrate-wide rewrite epoch, not a per-fn lever; coordinate with 04z53 umbrella + TealSpring's lane.
notes:
*** COMPOSE FIXED VIA ACCESSOR AUDIT: _native_compose walks part INTEGER rows (enumeration = part-local indices; index_of map deleted; neighbors() Vec allocs gone) + edge_attrs_by_indices both sides + pre-resolved self-indices for first-touch. fnx absolute 26.8 -> 11.7ms = 0.54x (~2x FASTER than nx; was 2.8x slower at bead open). THE BEAD'S HEADLINE TARGETS ARE ALL DELIVERED: compose 0.54x, union ~1.1x, copy 0.42x, ctor 1.31-1.49x (from 2.8/2.13/1.17/3.85x). REMAINING (epoch tail): Multi flips, ctor PyO3-extraction layer, dijkstra 1.33x residual. Consider closing d58s8 + filing the tail as fresh scoped beads next session.

PASS 12 REJECTED (TealSpring, 20260607T-multigraph-index-rows): MultiGraph index-row store preserved golden SHA c155b298352ea6d0a9ba845fc16e270d88c91f460fb3d35f2069d477fcffc45c but regressed: hyperfine ctor keyed 1.412746s -> 2.131239s (0.66x), add_loop keyed 0.996492s -> 1.090595s (0.91x); direct means regressed across ctor/add_loop; source restored. Next deeper primitive: batch-local endpoint/index map plus prepared keyed edge batches for MultiGraph/MultiDiGraph, or compose accessor audit where callers already hold indices.
close_reason: EPOCH DELIVERED — every headline target met or exceeded. Final ledger (quiet/near-quiet measurements): compose 2.8x->0.54x (2x FASTER than nx), union 2.13x->~1.1x, copy 1.17x->0.42x (Graph 0.08x in-window), ctor 3.85x->1.31-1.49x, aef 7.02x(global-attr)->1.22-1.27x, to_directed 0.56x, bfs_tree 0.31x, sssp 0.30x, closeness 0.06x, bellman 0.60x, dijkstra 1.91x->1.33x. STRUCTURAL: both simple classes fully integer-substrate (eager index rows = single row store; edges maps keyed by index pairs with rekey-on-removal); revision-keyed CSR + all-int caches; 6 directed kernels on CSR; lazy-mirror contract completed (write-through materialization); 7 pre-existing parity bugs found+fixed by the epoch's own typed/order/oracle batteries (srczero, msd twin-drift, SPFA skip heuristic, mirror write-loss x2, get_edge_data gating, compose row semantics). House recipes established: spec->oracle->writers->readers->delete flip cadence; rekey-both-removal-paths; accessor-audit-after-key-flip; oracle-first; peel-with-honest-reporting; verify-pre-existing-via-stash. Tail filed: Multi flips, ctor extraction layer, dijkstra residual.

title: perf: degree_assortativity_coefficient(DiGraph) delegates to nx (~5x) — add native directed kernel
description: degree_assortativity_coefficient undirected is now 50x faster than nx (d662ffd59, integer-CSR Pearson). The DIRECTED case still DELEGATES to nx via _call_networkx_for_parity (full fnx->nx conversion + nx compute) — 4.34x-5.27x slower. nx's directed contract uses the (out_deg(src), in_deg(tgt)) pair per directed edge (NOT the undirected projection). LEVER: add a native directed degree-assortativity kernel — for each directed edge (u,v) accumulate the Pearson sums over x=out_degree(u), y=in_degree(v) using integer successor-CSR (succ_indices) + precomputed out/in degree vecs, same exact-integer-sum -> byte-identical-to-formula approach as the undirected kernel. Route the directed branch (x='out',y='in',weight=None,nodes=None,no selfloops) to it, dropping the delegation. Target: DiGraph 5.27x -> ~0.02x. The degree_mixing_dict_directed infra (fnx-algorithms:29867) already extracts directed degree pairs.
notes: FIXED (01869fb52): native directed degree_assortativity kernel (integer successor-CSR, out/in degree vecs, per-edge (out_src,in_tgt) Pearson). Dropped the nx delegation. DiGraph 4.3-5.3x slower -> ~250-500x FASTER (0.002-0.004x). 3000-case proof (self-loops, degenerate) allclose 1e-9, NaN/±inf degenerate cases delegated to nx for exact parity. 1429 tests pass. Pairs with the undirected integer-CSR kernel (d662ffd59) — degree_assortativity now native+fast for both Graph and DiGraph.

title: bug+perf: all_pairs_lowest_common_ancestor non-deterministic + 4.2x slower than nx
description: TWO issues found via interleaved sweep: (1) CORRECTNESS/DETERMINISM: fnx all_pairs_lowest_common_ancestor VALUE-map varies run-to-run (4 distinct shas/4 runs on gnp300 DAG) and diverges from nx (8+ pairs). Root: when common_ancestors has multiple sinks (multiple valid LCAs), the pick depends on next(iter(common_ancestors)) set-iteration order; nx is deterministic (int hashes) but fnx's set layout/order differs run-to-run for some pairs. nx is the reference (deterministic). list(G) and ancestors() are stable; the instability is in the set-intersection/next(iter) selection for ambiguous pairs. (2) PERF: 4.2x slower than nx (388ms vs 93ms n=300). cProfile: 100% of gap is G.successors() called ~42k times in the descent loop (Python wrapper: vars/isinstance/hash + _native_successor_row_dict). FIX: snapshot succ once {n:list(G.successors(n))} -> 388ms->76ms (5x self-speedup, faster than nx). Order-preserving so behavior-equivalent to current fnx. BUT must FIRST make selection deterministic+nx-matching (reproduce nx set order, or canonical max-topo-index LCA) before the perf snapshot can claim byte-exact. Stash 'cc-lca-deadend-revert' has the perf-only patch. Sequence: fix determinism -> add snapshot.
close_reason: Shipped dd5102e2d (main+master). BOTH issues fixed: (1) DETERMINISM/PARITY — ancestor sets now built via nx's exact reverse-BFS discovery order (predecessor snapshot) instead of fnx's randomised native ancestors() HashSet; value-map was 5 distinct shas/5 runs -> now 1, and byte-exact vs nx (0 failures / 20 random DAGs + string + pairs=). (2) PERF — successor snapshot kills the ~42k G.successors() descent calls: 387ms->74ms (5.4x self-speedup), 1.24x FASTER than nx. 70 lca/ancestor tests pass; validated against actual committed code in isolated worktree build.

title: Conversion views: RecursionError on empty graphs + size(weight) returns 0
description: Two defects in _ConversionGraphViewBase (to_directed/to_undirected views): (1) the nodes/edges/adj/succ/pred properties used 'self.__dict__.get(_X_view) or super().Y'. The view objects define __len__, so an EMPTY view (0 nodes/edges) is falsy -> the 'or' fell through to the inherited canonical accessor, which on the synthetic view recursed (number_of_edges -> edges property -> empty/falsy -> super().edges -> EdgesView.__len__ -> number_of_edges) and raised RecursionError. Repro: fnx.to_directed(fnx.Graph()) then list(.edges()). (2) size(weight='weight') returned 0.0 (nx: correct sum) because the canonical Graph.size routes string weight through the Rust size_impl, which reads the view's empty Rust backing. Fixes: properties test the cached view with 'is not None'; add a size() override on _ConversionGraphViewBase computing from the view's own degree(). Surfaced by test_wide_parity_matrix to_directed[empty/single] and test_common_live_views_size_preserve_networkx_return_types.
close_reason: Fixed _ConversionGraphViewBase: (1) nodes/edges/adj/succ/pred properties now resolve cached views with 'is not None' instead of truthiness — empty views are falsy via __len__ and fell through to super(), causing infinite recursion (number_of_edges<->edges); (2) added size() override computing from the view's own degree() since canonical Graph.size routes string weight through Rust size_impl blind to the view's empty backing. Commit fd6dfdd9. Conversion-view sweep 6->1 pre-existing failure; test_graph_utilities 38->36, 0 regressions.
