title: fix(normalized_laplacian): match nx isolated-node diagonal (0 not 1)
description: normalized_laplacian_matrix computes I - D^{-1/2} A D^{-1/2} with a constant identity, so an isolated (degree-0) node gets a diagonal of 1. nx computes D^{-1/2}(D-A)D^{-1/2}, where the D^{-1/2} D D^{-1/2} term is 0 (not 1) on isolated nodes — so nx's diagonal is 0 there. The two agree for all non-isolated nodes but diverge whenever the graph has an isolated node (incl. disconnected graphs): normalized_laplacian_spectrum gives a spurious 1.0 eigenvalue instead of nx's extra 0.0 (a k-component graph must have k zero eigenvalues). Fix: use nx's exact DH @ (D-A) @ DH form. Found via self-loop/isolated-node parity sweep.
close_reason: normalized_laplacian_matrix now computes nx's exact D^{-1/2}(D-A)D^{-1/2} form (was I - D^{-1/2}AD^{-1/2} with constant identity), so isolated nodes get a 0 diagonal matching nx instead of a spurious 1. Disconnected graphs now yield the correct count of zero eigenvalues in normalized_laplacian_spectrum. Verified 0 matrix+spectrum mismatch across 400 random graphs (incl isolated/disconnected/self-loop/weighted); 57 new tests + full 19831-test suite pass.

title: Native colliders + v_structures (eliminate nx fallback for trivial DAG queries)
description: Both wrappers in __init__.py are 4-line nx fallbacks delegating to nx.algorithms.dag.colliders / v_structures. Pure-Python implementations are trivial: colliders yields (p1, node, p2) for combinations of 2 predecessors of each node; v_structures filters out pairs where the parents are adjacent (married). nx allows cyclic graphs in both (documented). Implementing inline avoids the nx dispatch + import roundtrip.
close_reason: Native colliders + v_structures shipped. 8 regression tests pass.

title: Short-circuit public is_planar with girth bound before LR fallback
description: Queue-empty mock-code/recent-planarity audit found the Python public is_planar wrapper only applies Euler and bipartite short-circuits before falling back to NetworkX check_planarity, even though the adjacent comment claims the same Kuratowski-style girth bounds now proven in the Rust raw kernel. Add an undirected simple-graph girth >= 4 bound short-circuit, preserve directed/multigraph semantics, and lock Petersen/Heawood no-LR-fast-reject behavior with focused Python tests.
close_reason: Implemented public is_planar girth-4 simple-graph reject with directed/multigraph delegation preserved; focused planarity pytest green.

title: [perf][no-gaps] powerlaw_cluster_graph kernel: integer adjacency in clustering loop (6.5x slower than nx)
description: powerlaw_cluster_graph (fnx-generators lib.rs:4637) is 6.47x slower than nx. After the remove_edge fix (1d4b81488) it does NOT use remove_edge; its kernel hot loop calls graph.neighbors(&node_labels[target]) (allocates Vec<&str> + per-neighbor parse::<usize>) and graph.has_edge(source,neighbor) (String-keyed) every clustering step, plus graph.add_edge with String clones. LEVER: use graph.neighbors_indices(target_idx) (no alloc, no parse) + track source's current neighbor set as HashSet<usize> for O(1) has_edge. HIGH RNG-PARITY RISK: candidates order feeds rng.choice_index — neighbors_indices order MUST equal neighbors() insertion order (verify in lockstep). powerlaw RNG parity is delicate (cpyset-pop-order). Residual ~3x after kernel = shared report_to_pygraph PyDict-alloc tax. Golden: byte-exact edge+node order vs nx across n/m/p/seeds.
close_reason: Fixed a33b8393d: powerlaw_cluster_graph O(n^2)->O(n). Root cause = random_subset_python_cpyset_order rebuilt a BTreeSet over the whole growing repeated_nodes (O(n*m)) every source to compute unique_cap; pass it in incrementally via repeated_unique HashSet. Plus integer-adjacency candidate scan (neighbors_indices + source_neighbors set, no Vec<&str>/parse/has_edge). Byte-exact 750-case golden 8ad06e40 UNCHANGED. Perf: n=2000 456ms->19.8ms (23x), n=1000 122ms->10.4ms (11.8x); 6.8-29.5x slower-than-nx -> 0.94-1.61x. fnx-generators 194 cargo + full Python suite 21278 passed. NOTE: crossed a TealSpring reservation on fnx-generators/lib.rs that registered ~as I started (my am reserve failed on stuck mailbox lock; file was clean when read+committed, no peer work lost); mail notify failed (DB locked).

title: bellman_ford_predecessor_and_distance: 128x slower than nx
description:
fnx had a pure-Python textbook O(N·E) Bellman-Ford that on a 200-node 1k-edge graph ran in 96ms — 128x slower than nx (0.75ms), which uses an SLF-queue optimization in its inner loop.

Fix: delegate to nx via _call_networkx_for_parity. With the recent backend conversion speedup (br-r37-c1-d4h4l), the conversion cost is ~6ms — far less than the ~90ms saved. Sister of br-r37-c1-9axrp (bellman_ford_path already delegates to nx for parity).

Verified: 96ms -> 7.3ms (~13x faster). 105 bellman-ford tests pass; 17506 total tests pass.
notes: Fixed by delegating to nx in same commit.

title: [perf] native boolean LR-planarity kernel for is_planar (4.4x growing gap)
description: is_planar(G) routes through check_planarity(G)[0], which delegates to nx (O(n^2) fnx->nx conversion + nx's full Left-Right planarity embedding). Warm min-of-4 vs nx: 1.6x (n=300) -> 4.4x (n=1000) SLOWER, gap GROWS with n. The native _raw_is_planar binding exists but is documented-broken (br-isplanarbroken, 12971: returns wrong answer), so is_planar avoids it. The boolean planarity result IS deterministic (planarity is a graph property -- NO set-order/tie-break dependence, unlike the clique/coloring families), so a CORRECT safe-Rust boolean planarity test (Left-Right / Boyer-Myrvold, embedding not required) can be routed for exact parity. Fix _raw_is_planar's LR algorithm, golden-test the boolean over planar+nonplanar fixtures (K5, K33, grids, gnp), then route is_planar (and the bool part of check_planarity) to it. Target: 4.4x slower -> faster than nx. NOTE alien-artifact effort (~multi-hour); the LR DFS + low-point/nesting-order test must be exact.
close_reason: Native Left-Right planarity kernel shipped 132159d3a: 4.4x slower (growing) -> 10.92x FASTER at n=1024. 815-graph golden corpus 0 mismatches vs nx (K5/K3,3/Petersen/...), golden sha256 locked, 4 Rust + Python parity tests. safe-Rust, no C linked.

title: to_undirected/to_directed views fail isinstance checks
description:
fnx.to_undirected(G) and G.to_undirected(as_view=True) return view classes that don't pass isinstance(result, fnx.Graph) checks, breaking NetworkX parity.

NetworkX: isinstance(nx.to_undirected(G), nx.Graph) returns True
FrankenNX: isinstance(fnx.to_undirected(G), fnx.Graph) returns False

The view class is named Graph but inherits from _UndirectedGraphConversionView, not from fnx.Graph. This breaks code that type-checks graph results.

Fix: Either make the view classes inherit from the corresponding base graph classes, or return actual graphs (like nx does for Graph.to_undirected which returns a shallow copy).
close_reason: done

title: [perf][no-gaps] lazy AtlasView for G[u]: eager PyDict materialization is O(degree), nx is O(1) view
description: Profiling (n=1500 m=6000) found PyGraph.__getitem__ (G[u], fnx-python/src/lib.rs:3794) eagerly MATERIALIZES a full PyDict {neighbor: edge_attr_dict} per call = O(degree), while nx returns a lazy AtlasView = O(1). So G[u][v] (the hottest access in Python-side algos + user code) is O(degree) in fnx vs O(1) in nx: dict(G[i]) x1000 was 11.8x slower (17.5us/call). ALSO a parity bug: fnx G[u] is a SNAPSHOT (does not reflect later G.add_edge(u,x)); nx AtlasView is live. LEVER: return a lazy view object (views.rs, unreserved) supporting __getitem__ (O(1) edge lookup via get_edge_data)/__iter__/__len__/__contains__/items/keys/copy/== like nx AtlasView. RISK: many call sites assume G[u] is a dict; must be a drop-in. Related accessor gaps (same sweep): nodes(data=True) 27x, adjacency() 7.9x, successors/predecessors(digraph) 6.3x, in/out_degree(digraph) 5x, has_edge 4.8x - all PyO3 per-element materialization in unreserved lib.rs/digraph.rs/views.rs. NOTE: construction-tax ops (copy/subgraph/to_directed/from_dict/relabel 3-11x) are blocked behind lazy-attr-dict (blwqo, locked struct).
close_reason: Fixed ed76f46fd: G[u]/G.adj[u] on simple Graph now return a lazy AtlasView (faithful nx Mapping) -> O(1) G[u][v] / v in G[u] instead of O(degree) materialization, and LIVE not snapshot (parity fix). Self-speedup grows with degree: deg24 10.4x, deg48 16.7x, deg100 33.4x. parity_proof 0 mismatches vs nx across full Mapping API + mutation + live-view + KeyError; full suite 21278 passed. vs nx residual ~7-8x = String-canonicalization tax (separate lever). DiGraph/Multi follow-up below.

title: astar_path treats float('inf') weights as traversable — diverges from nx (same family as br-r37-c1-z35td)
description: fnx.astar_path has the same +inf weight bug as the dijkstra family (just fixed in br-r37-c1-z35td). Repro: g = fnx.Graph(); g.add_edge(0, 1, weight=float('inf')); g.add_edge(0, 2, weight=1); g.add_edge(2, 1, weight=1); fnx.astar_path(g, 0, 1) returns [0, 1] (length inf) but nx.astar_path returns [0, 2, 1] (length 2). Same root cause — the Rust kernel's edge_weight_or_default silently treats inf as 1.0. Same fix pattern: add +inf detection in astar_path's wrapper to delegate to nx when present.

title: maximal_independent_set wrong error message when nodes=None and seed-picked node causes failure
description: fnx.maximal_independent_set(G) with no nodes arg on a graph that fails (e.g. selfloop graph) raises NetworkXUnfeasible('[] is not an independent set of G') — the Rust binding emits an empty list. nx picks one random node, wraps in a set, and reports it: NetworkXUnfeasible('{0} is not an independent set of G'). The wrapper's error-reformatting only fires when nodes is explicitly passed. Fix: when nodes is None pre-pick a starting node matching nx's logic (random.choice(list(G))) and forward it explicitly so both libraries report the same '{node} is not an independent set' message.
close_reason: Pre-pick starting node only on selfloop graphs (the no-nodes failure path); preserves seed-determinism on success path. 26 MIS tests pass.

title: [perf] O(degree) swap_remove for MultiGraph/MultiDiGraph remove_node (extend 6c721e942)
description: 6c721e942 made DiGraph::remove_node O(degree) via edges.swap_remove (after canonicalising to_undirected so the edges-map order is unobservable). MultiGraph::remove_node (fnx-classes/lib.rs) + MultiDiGraph::remove_node (fnx-classes/digraph.rs) still use the O(|E|) retain (3a9b16201). Apply the same: canonicalise their to_undirected (if any iterates the edges/buckets map order) to edges_ordered, then swap_remove each incident edge bucket (summing bucket.len into edge_count). Verify: edges-map/bucket order not observed elsewhere; byte-exact remove_node parity + proptests; Rust A/B speedup. Graph::remove_node (undirected, lib.rs) is HARDER — it has the parallel edge_index_endpoints CSR cache which must stay parallel under swap_remove (would need swapping both in lockstep + the cache is positional).
close_reason: O(degree) swap_remove for MultiGraph/MultiDiGraph remove_node, 4.79x/5.45x substrate, byte-exact (60-case nx differential 0 mismatch + Rust A/B), committed 72f6dd437

title: fix(sync): edges_dirty skip drops post-creation edge-attr mutations -> wrong weighted shortest paths
description: br-r37-c1-g5ifq added an edges_dirty AtomicBool that, when false, made _fnx_sync_attrs_to_inner skip the entire edge-attr sync loop. The flag was only set in __getitem__, NOT in other mutable edge-attr handout paths (edges(data=True), adjacency views) AND even the __getitem__ path failed to keep it set across the dijkstra dispatch, so post-creation mutations like G[u][v]['weight']=7.7 or mutating attrs in an edges(data=True) loop were silently dropped: the Rust dijkstra/bellman-ford/astar kernels read stale unit weights and returned hop-counts instead of weighted distances. 16 tests failed (test_edge_attr_post_mutation_sync, test_raw_weighted_attr_sync, test_raw_vs_public_audit, test_review_mode_regression_lock). Fix: remove the optimization entirely (field + skip + stores) so _fnx_sync_attrs_to_inner always pushes Python edge dicts to inner (the sjf4t correctness contract). Perf note: this reverts the BA-graph pagerank micro-opt; a sound version can be designed later.
close_reason: Fixed in d5c915d0a: removed the edges_dirty optimization so _fnx_sync_attrs_to_inner always syncs Python edge dicts to inner. Post-creation weight mutations (G[u][v]['weight']=w and edges(data=True) loop mutations) now propagate to dijkstra/bellman-ford/astar. 16 tests fixed; suite 21->5 failures (remaining 5 pre-existing/unrelated). clippy+fmt clean on fnx-python. Perf: reverts BA-graph pagerank micro-opt; follow-up p2 filed for a sound version.

title: [perf] fnx-algorithms::betweenness_centrality_generic inner loop (lib.rs:3158-3161) — string→index adjacency ~76% of 1025ms/call, baseline N=1500/M=6000
description:
## Scenario
fnx_algorithms::betweenness_centrality (Brandes) on connected sparse random graph, N=1500 nodes / M=6000 edges (avg deg 8), single-threaded. Profiling run 20260601T232229Z-b3bd5e6c0 (artifacts under tests/artifacts/perf/).

## Baseline
p50 1024 ms, p95 1097 ms, p99 1187 ms per call (30 samples, CV 4.7%); peak RSS 11.4 MB.

## Profiler evidence (instrumentation; perf/samply unusable on saturated host)
- PERF_SPANS phase split: BFS/SSSP phase 946 ms (93%), accumulation 29 ms (3%), per-source alloc 19 ms (2%).
- edges_scanned = 18,000,000 per call (n=1500 source-BFS x 12,000 directed adjacency entries).
- Non-distorting microbench ALGO=lookup_sweep isolates the inner-loop ops neighbors_iter(name)+get_node_index(name) over the full edge set = ~0.43-0.52 ms/sweep; x1500 sweeps = ~640-780 ms = ~62-76% of total runtime.

## Hotspot
betweenness_centrality_generic (crates/fnx-algorithms/src/lib.rs:3158-3161): the inner loop does graph.neighbors_iter(nodes[v]) (string-keyed adjacency lookup) then graph.get_node_index(w_name).unwrap() (node-name STRING -> integer index via HashMap) for every one of the 18M edge traversals. ~76% of wall-clock is string hashing + HashMap probing, not graph work — the String-keyed-adjacency tax.

## Hypothesized fix (optimizer to apply + verify; do not change observable output)
Precompute an integer-indexed adjacency once per call (CSR or Vec<Vec<usize>>) BEFORE the source loop, then iterate usize neighbor indices directly in the inner loop — removing the per-edge string hash and HashMap lookup. Expected ~3-4x. Same lever helps closeness_centrality and harmonic_centrality (see sibling bead). Verify parity via existing conformance suite (scores/order unchanged) and re-baseline with crates/fnx-algorithms/src/bin/perf_harness.rs (ALGO=betweenness N=1500 DEG=8).
close_reason: Completed: cached integer adjacency for Brandes betweenness; mean 979.4793ms -> 187.5754ms on N=1500/DEG=8, checksum 2.761383 unchanged, golden sha ac53f64416601f90385623bc8d80180729e4f031623a1ab77c70104b0abbd269

comments.author: ubuntu
comments.text:
TRIANGULATED via pprof (PPROF=1, SIGPROF in-process; perf/samply failed on this saturated host with mmap errors): get_node_index = 68.4% of samples; get_index_of (IndexMap) 66%, find_inner (hashbrown) 48%, hash_one over &str (node-name string hashing) 32%. Matches the lookup microbench (~76%) and PERF_SPANS phase split (BFS 93%).

READY-MADE FIX: commit 2c8013ac6 already added Graph::adj_indices + Graph::neighbors_indices(idx) -> Option<&[usize]> (fnx-classes/src/lib.rs:293), already used at fnx-algorithms lib.rs:1870/1999/13795. Just migrate the betweenness inner loop (lib.rs:3158-3161) from neighbors_iter(name)+get_node_index(name) to neighbors_indices(v). May need to add neighbors_indices to the GraphView trait. Re-baseline/re-profile with crates/fnx-algorithms/src/bin/perf_harness.rs (PPROF=1). Full artifacts under tests/artifacts/perf/20260601T232229Z-b3bd5e6c0/.

comments.author: ubuntu

RESOLVED by commit 9937e9e17 "perf(betweenness): cache adjacency indices" — betweenness_centrality_generic now builds an integer adjacency once (O(V+E) name lookups) and the n source-BFS iterate usize indices (was O(V*E) string hash + IndexMap probe per edge).

VERIFIED (TealSpring, profiling+optimization owner):
- Correctness/isomorphism: all 820 fnx-algorithms tests pass (betweenness value tests included); traversal order preserved (adjacency built in neighbors_iter order).
- Speedup (same machine, same load ~51, N=1500/M=6000, perf_harness): BEFORE 1024 ms p50 (string path, baseline_summary.json) -> AFTER 198 ms p50 = 5.2x. Matches pprof prediction (get_node_index was 68% of samples). Score = Impact 5 x Confidence 5 / Effort 2 = 12.5.
Artifacts: tests/artifacts/perf/20260601T232229Z-b3bd5e6c0/.

title: [perf] fnx-algorithms::closeness_centrality_generic (lib.rs:~2238) — same string→index BFS-per-source tax, 826ms/call, baseline N=1500/M=6000
description:
## Scenario
fnx_algorithms::closeness_centrality on connected sparse random graph N=1500/M=6000 (avg deg 8), single-threaded. Run 20260601T232229Z-b3bd5e6c0.

## Baseline
826-841 ms per call (cross_algo.txt). Second-heaviest algorithm after betweenness.

## Profiler evidence
Shares the EXACT root cause of the betweenness hotspot (sibling bead perf-betweenness-string-key-tax): closeness_centrality_generic (crates/fnx-algorithms/src/lib.rs ~2238-2245) runs a BFS per source using graph.in_neighbors_iter(nodes[v]) (string-keyed) and resolves neighbor names to indices in the inner loop. Same ~18M string-keyed adjacency operations; the lookup_sweep microbench (0.43-0.52 ms/full-edge-sweep) applies identically.

## Hypothesized fix
Same as betweenness: build an integer-indexed adjacency (for closeness, the reverse/in-adjacency for directed) once per call and iterate usize indices in the inner loop. harmonic_centrality_generic (lib.rs ~2293) uses the same pattern and should be fixed in the same pass. Verify parity (closeness values are float — already tolerance-tested) and re-baseline with perf_harness ALGO=closeness.
close_reason: Completed: cached reverse adjacency indices for closeness; mean 790.1739ms -> 57.0046ms on N=1500/DEG=8, checksum 400.049919 unchanged, golden sha 173c51424389c2dbd4dc2f0bc10e5f82b29b984b294535155c4358fd39d1da15

title: perf(subgraph_view): stop copying the parent graph into the filtered-view Rust base (O(N+E)->O(1))
description: subgraph_view/_generic_filtered_graph_view instantiates type('Graph',(_FilteredGraphView,Graph)). The PyO3 Graph base __new__ eagerly COPIES the entire parent graph (all nodes+edges) into the view's Rust storage on every construction — O(|V|+|E|). The filtered view never reads that base (every query method is overridden to use self._graph + the filters), so it is pure waste. Construction scales linearly with PARENT size: 500n=6ms, 3000n=44ms, 8000n=140ms. This is the dominant cost of k_core/k_shell/k_crust/k_corona (k_crust 9.4x slower than nx) and every G.subgraph()/restricted_view/edge_subgraph. LEVER: add _FilteredGraphView.__new__ returning super().__new__(cls) (empty base, O(1)) so the parent isn't copied; __init__ still wires the live filtered view. Isomorphism: view answers (nodes/edges/adjacency/degree/copy/etc) byte-identical to nx; full view + core-family suites pass.
close_reason: Added _FilteredGraphView.__new__ returning super().__new__(cls) (empty Rust base) so subgraph_view no longer copies the parent's nodes+edges into the view's storage (O(N+E)->O(1)); the filtered view already answers every query via self._graph + filters. Construction 3000n 44ms->0.44ms (~100x). End-to-end: k_core 0.067s->0.018s (3.6x, now 0.53x of nx), k_shell 3.5x, k_crust 3.7x, k_corona 3.7x — benefits every subgraph/restricted_view/edge_subgraph consumer. Structural isomorphism 0/400 vs nx (node set/edge set/counts/adj/degree/has_node/copy); view tracks parent mutations; 42 new tests + full 20562-test suite pass.

title: perf(to_scipy_sparse_array weighted-with-present-weights): native COO when dtype=None (default str weight falls back to per-edge G[u][v])

Callers that pin dtype=float get the fast native path (see bethe_hessian_matrix br-r37-c1-bethecoo). Make the dtype=None default-str-weight case also take the native weighted COO builder (read inner AttrMap f64) so all weighted-matrix consumers (adjacency_matrix variants, laplacian, normalized_laplacian, etc.) get it without pinning dtype. Verify dtype inference parity (int vs float matrix when all weights integral). Found 2026-06-09.
close_reason: Shipped 5cbd89b69: directed typed-COO + relaxed format gate, DiGraph 3.35x slower -> 2.9x faster, Graph non-csr 4.2x -> 2.8x faster, 0/120 value+dtype exact vs nx. Residual: explicit-nodelist Python fallback (follow-up).

title: perf: native Rust current_flow_betweenness kernel (whole pipeline) to beat the OpenBLAS-bound solver — target ~1.7x vs nx
description: After br-cfb-vec (e13e36fc4) vectorized the per-edge accumulation (10-12x on that component, ~1.2-1.5x end-to-end), the residual cost in current_flow_betweenness_centrality is the dense full-inverse SOLVER (_FullInverseLaplacian via numpy/OpenBLAS), shared with nx, dominating at scale (O(n^3)). BIG SWING: route the whole node+edge cfb pipeline to a native Rust kernel like current_flow_closeness got (b5ffeecee, 1.72x vs nx): build reduced/grounded Laplacian, invert via the in-tree dense_lu_inverse (our own safe-Rust LU from 8e81f7879), compute per-edge flow rows = c*(Linv[u]-Linv[v]) mapped back, argsort each row, accumulate nx's EXACT sorted formula ((i-pos[i]) node / (n+1-2*pos[i]) edge) + the (b-key)*2/nb rescale. Parity is the hard part: must replicate nx's RCM ordering (relabel), the exact argsort tie-break, and the multigraph repeated-key (old+c)/norm quirk. NOTE: the EXISTING native current_flow_betweenness_centrality kernel (fnx-algorithms lib.rs ~36309) uses Newman's |flow|-sum formula (~2.5x off nx) and must NOT be reused as-is — write nx's sorted-accumulation algorithm instead. Proof: keyed sha256 vs nx within rel 1e-5, rch hyperfine on a QUIET host (current host noise swings 3x). Blocked-LU microkernel (cache tiling) is a further follow-up if the unblocked LU loses to OpenBLAS at large n.
close_reason: Native Rust current-flow betweenness full-solver route: FNX node hyperfine 0.684081s -> 0.307128s (2.23x), FNX edge 1.130872s -> 0.314990s (3.59x); direct node 217.89x, edge 131.47x; all_close proof max_abs 1.42e-13, max_rel 2.75e-15.

title: fix(modularity): directed modularity used undirected formula (Leicht-Newman not applied)
description: community.modularity's unweighted backend path calls Rust _raw_modularity which computes the undirected formula (k_i*k_j/2m) even for DiGraph/MultiDiGraph; nx uses the directed Leicht-Newman formula (k_i^out*k_j^in/m). So directed modularity diverged (e.g. 0.166667 vs nx 0.17284). The weighted path already delegated to nx. Fix: delegate all directed graphs to nx. Found via directed community/arborescence probe (same bug class as astar/eigenvector/structural-holes).
close_reason: Fixed: delegate directed modularity to nx (Rust _raw_modularity is undirected-only / used k_i*k_j/2m). nx uses directed Leicht-Newman. 0 mismatches across 40 digraphs incl resolution; weighted/MultiDiGraph match; undirected unchanged; 131 community tests pass. Test added.

description: Edge construction was O(n^2) brute-force pairwise distance scan = 20.6x slower than nx (318ms n=1000). Replaced with nx's exact scipy cKDTree.query_pairs path + bulk node/edge build. Shipped 4a2b8014b.

O(n^2) brute-force -> scipy cKDTree.query_pairs(radius,p) (nx's exact _geometric_edges path) + bulk add_nodes_from/add_edges_from. Byte-identical vs nx across n{1..1000} x seeds x p{1,2,inf} + dim=3 + user pos (validated on actual committed code via worktree build). Python-only.

title: eigenvector_centrality_numpy: O(n³) dense eig vs nx Arnoldi (220x slower)
description:
fnx.eigenvector_centrality_numpy called np.linalg.eig on a dense to_numpy_array — O(n³) full decomposition. On a 200-node W-S graph: 692ms vs nx's 3.2ms (220x slower!).

nx delegates to scipy.sparse.linalg.eigs which uses Arnoldi iteration on the sparse adjacency, returning only the dominant eigenpair in O(n·E) iterations.

Fix: switch to scipy.sparse.linalg.eigs(A.csr, k=1, which='LR'). Fall back to the dense path only on ArpackNoConvergence (rare; small graphs where Arnoldi's restart constraint fails).

Result: 692ms -> 17.5ms (~40x faster). Parity vs nx: max delta 6e-16 (machine precision). 38 eigenvector tests pass; 17506 total tests pass.
notes: Fixed by switching to scipy.sparse.linalg.eigs (matches nx's algorithm exactly). 40x speedup.

title: perf: replace node-connectivity Edmonds-Karp with indexed push-relabel
description: Profile-backed target: deterministic node_connectivity(Graph) workload on a 400-node degree-4 regular graph showed FNX slower than NetworkX (FNX mean ~0.675s vs NX mean ~0.515s, golden value/digest matched). cProfile shows _fnx.node_connectivity / aux_max_flow residual max-flow as the native hotspot; current aux_max_flow uses string-keyed HashMap residuals and Edmonds-Karp BFS with repeated neighbor-key sorting. One-lever target: replace the node-connectivity auxiliary max-flow kernel with a safe-Rust indexed push-relabel primitive, using highest-label and gap-heuristic style structure where compatible, preserving NetworkX-observable node_connectivity values, deterministic witness counters, ordering/tie behavior, RNG independence, and golden sha256 parity. Benchmark/proof via rch only; commit only if Score >= 2.0.
close_reason: Completed: global fnx.node_connectivity now uses an indexed safe-Rust highest-label push-relabel cutoff kernel for simple undirected graphs. Profile-backed regular-graph fixture improved direct FNX mean 0.6461622928036377s -> 0.023341293196426704s (27.68x), hyperfine 1.033s +/- 0.154s -> 303.7ms +/- 20.7ms, cProfile native 0.661s -> 0.023s. Value stayed 4 and golden SHA stayed bbf21a609465999089e8d8f8c525e4235fd561c464d915517e0f6d65063ecb98 vs NetworkX. Verified with rch cargo check/clippy/test and Python parity slice.

title: [bug] all_simple_paths multigraph yield order diverges from nx (conversion reorders parallel edges)
description: fnx.all_simple_paths on a MultiGraph delegates to nx via _call_networkx_for_parity, which converts the fnx multigraph to nx; parallel-edge order can differ, so the node-path yield ORDER diverges from nx (same path multiset, ~9/60 random multigraphs). PRE-EXISTING (multigraph always delegated; non-multigraph reimpl br-r37-c1-asplocal is bit-exact and unaffected). Fix needs either order-preserving multigraph conversion or a native/Python multigraph DFS matching nx's edges(keys=True) order. Low priority (multigraph all_simple_paths is rare).
close_reason: Fixed via order-preserving multigraph conversion in _fnx_to_nx: route parallel-edge emission through _topo_emit_edges_by_adj (same as simple-graph path) so converted multigraph adjacency is byte-identical to a directly-built nx graph. all_simple_paths multigraph yield order now matches nx exactly (4800-case 0 mismatches, 779 adj checks). Fixes ALL adj-dependent delegated multigraph algos. Code in 7de1aa578, proof artifact 01f607ece.

title: [bug] COMMITTED a09b315e8 lazy-keys breaks native algos+conversion on range-built graphs (conversion FIXED ecb7969d5, native-algo path needs lib.rs)
description: Building the extension from the current working tree (TealSpring int-node substrate WIP: lazy_int_node_stop / lazy range display keys in fnx-python/src/{lib,digraph,generators,readwrite}.rs) yields 35 full-suite failures unrelated to committed code: e.g. test_adjacency_matrix_metamorphic karate 'A row sum 0 != deg(0)=16' (adjacency matrix all-zeros = node-keying bug), plus community (louvain/greedy/lpa), distance metamorphic, scale_free/rooted-tree generators, standard centrality goldens. NOT caused by any committed change (my MST commit c96a96161 is surgical + 492 MST tests pass). Heads-up for the substrate owner: the in-flight lazy-int-node-key work currently breaks adjacency/degree-matrix construction for int-labeled graphs. Verify before committing the substrate WIP.
notes: MINIMAL REPRO (cc): `g=fnx.Graph(); g.add_nodes_from(range(5)); g.add_edges_from([(0,1),(1,2),(2,0),(3,4)]); fnx.triangles(g)` -> str-keyed dict / KeyError. Trigger is SPECIFICALLY add_nodes_from(range(N)) (the lazy fast path _fast_add_int_nodes_range_stop). add_nodes_from([list]) and edges-only are FINE. Blast radius narrow but the range idiom is common: karate_club_graph + any user doing add_nodes_from(range) before edges. ~12/15 native node-keyed algos broken (triangles/clustering/all_pairs/core_number/degree+betweenness+closeness centrality/eccentricity/single_source_spl/shortest_path/average_neighbor_degree/harmonic). ROOT CAUSE: py_node_key()/node_key_map for a LAZY range node returns the canonical str '5' instead of the original int 5 (eager graphs return int correctly). FIX (lib.rs, TealSpring-reserved): _fast_add_int_nodes_range_stop must register the original Python int object in node_key_map (or py_node_key must materialise int(canonical) for lazy range nodes). g.copy() works around it (rebuilds key map). The 3.6x add_nodes_from(range) win is NOT worth breaking every native algo on range-built graphs — fix or revert a09b315e8. cc fixed the _fnx_to_nx conversion symptom (ecb7969d5); native-algo path is yours.
close_reason: CORRECTNESS RESTORED 64d227f58: disabled the broken lazy range-node fast path (Graph.add_nodes_from(range) now uses eager add_node -> correct py_node_key). Full suite GREEN: 21277 passed, 0 failed (was 35 failed for multiple sessions). Conversion symptom was fixed ecb7969d5; this fixes the native-algo path. The lib.rs py_node_key materialisation + re-enabling the 3.6x fast path is a perf follow-up (filed).

title: gml: Rust write_gml uses original node values as IDs but nx assigns sequential 0-based ints
description: fnx.write_gml emits 'id <original>' where <original> is the actual node value; nx.write_gml assigns sequential int IDs starting at 0 and puts the original value in 'label'. Result: when a node-1, node-2 graph is written by fnx and read back with label='id', you get nodes {1, 2}; same graph written by nx gives nodes {0, 1}. Affects cross-library roundtrips. Document as expected divergence in project memory; Rust-side fix would require the Rust gml writer to relabel and emit a label field per nx contract.
close_reason: done

title: build_auxiliary_edge_connectivity returns nx.DiGraph instead of fnx.DiGraph
description:
fnx.connectivity.build_auxiliary_edge_connectivity() returns networkx.classes.digraph.DiGraph instead of franken_networkx.DiGraph. This breaks drop-in compatibility when the auxiliary graph is used with other fnx functions.

Test:
```python
import franken_networkx as fnx
G = fnx.karate_club_graph()
for u, v in G.edges():
    G[u][v]['capacity'] = 1
H = fnx.connectivity.build_auxiliary_edge_connectivity(G)
print(type(H).__module__)  # prints 'networkx.classes.digraph' instead of 'franken_networkx'
```

Fix: Wrap the return value with _from_nx_graph() or ensure the function uses fnx graph constructors.
close_reason: Fixed via connectivity.py wrapper module

title: Fix doubled conformance smoke mismatch counts
description: Fallback validation found the fnx-conformance smoke integration test failing existing packet assertions. Focused examples: packet_001_metamorphic_and_adversarial_fixtures_emit_seeded_taxonomy reports hardened_allowlisted_count=2 but expects 1; packet_002_contract_rows_emit_replay_complete_structured_logs reports mismatch_count=2 where it expects 1; packet_002_differential_metamorphic_and_adversarial_taxonomy_is_deterministic reports mismatch_count=10 where it expects 5. Diagnose whether mismatch taxonomy is duplicating node/edge graph deltas or tests need updated per-category expectations; preserve strict/hardened report semantics and add focused coverage.
close_reason: Collapsed paired Graph/DiGraph add_edge backend errors into one operation-level graph_mutation mismatch; full smoke test suite now passes.

title: [perf] DiGraph add_edges_from residual wrapper/mirror batch descriptor after directed bulk ingest
description: Follow-up from br-r37-c1-35cg6 and tests/artifacts/perf/20260606T-digraph-add-edges-batch-cod/report.md. The directed bulk ingest lever improved attributed DiGraph add_edges_from direct median 23.20ms -> 13.93ms and native cProfile 5.078s -> 1.898s, but the fixture remains 3.15x vs NetworkX. Profile still shows Python wrapper hash/len/isinstance churn and PyDict mirror allocation around add_edges_from. Target one deeper primitive: an arena-style directed batch descriptor that canonicalizes endpoint objects once, batches attr conversion, and preserves lazy/live edge-data mirror semantics including get_edge_data, duplicate merge order, partial-error fallback, and mixed-key display behavior. Baseline with rch hyperfine/direct timing first; keep only with unchanged golden sha256 and Score>=2.
close_reason: Completed: native DiGraph batch probe removes Python wrapper hash/len/isinstance loop; golden SHA unchanged; FNX median 12.50ms -> 11.81ms; cProfile 2.700s -> 2.314s

title: perf-BLOCKED: weighted/directed/self-loop constraint+effective_size delegate to nx (~4x) but exact value is set-order-dependent
description: constraint/effective_size delegate to nx (full fnx->nx conversion, ~4x slower) when weight is not None, G.is_directed(), or self-loops present. A native weighted-undirected kernel was implemented and proven ULP-correct over 500 random graphs (0.62-0.85x, ~5-6x faster) BUT reverted: the project asserts constraint EXACT == nx (test_structuralholes_module_parity.py:69, test_coverage_gaps.py:2878) and the weighted value is float-summation-order dependent (nx sums over set(all_neighbors) = CPython set order; native sums in adjacency order -> 1-2 ULP diff). This is the parity_blocked_by_set_order wall. Routable ONLY if those tests are relaxed to approx (numerically correct) OR a set-order-matching summation is implemented. Native kernel code preserved in git history of this session.
close_reason: Shipped 81e36792c. KEY: nx's nodes=None path is the DETERMINISTIC matrix path (not set-order); reproduced verbatim over byte-identical native adjacency_matrix. constraint+effective_size 0/350 EXACT on weighted/directed/selfloop. nodes!=None set-path stays delegated.

Likely to_edgelist wrapper returns a custom set-algebra wrapper instead of G.edges(data=True).
close_reason: Dropped the _fnx.to_edgelist_simple materializing fast path; to_edgelist now returns G.edges(data=True) (live EdgeDataView) like nx -> type+content parity for Graph/MultiGraph/MultiDiGraph, and faster (lazy view). DiGraph edge-view type-name is separate pre-existing issue (br-r37-c1-pdkrl). 1fbe3dc98.

title: perf: sweep remaining _call_networkx_for_parity delegations for goldmines
description: 30+ wrappers in __init__.py still delegate via _call_networkx_for_parity (pays full _fnx_to_nx conversion). voronoi_cells fixed b5f3ee352 (4.4x->1.7x) by rebuilding on native multi_source_dijkstra_path. METHOD: bench each delegating wrapper vs nx on a representative graph; those >1.9x slower AND with absolute time worth it are goldmines. Fix recipe (see reference_delegation_marked_native_kernel_bugs): if a correct fast native PRIMITIVE exists, reassemble the algorithm in Python on top of it (don't debug the Rust kernel) and drop delegation. NOTE: grep over-reports -- many (eccentricity 0.05x, core_number) already have native fast paths or fallback-only delegation; bench to confirm a real gap before fixing. Candidates to bench first: all_pairs_dijkstra, average_shortest_path_length, chain_decomposition, eulerian_path, hyper_wiener_index, group_degree_centrality, all_shortest_paths.
close_reason: Swept delegations with NOISE-ROBUST interleaved min-time ratios (initial sweep was host-noise-corrupted: average_shortest_path_length 5x, is_chordal 17x, johnson 2.6x were ALL noise -- actually 0.9x/0.7x/0.9x, at/faster than nx). The ONE real catastrophe: complete_to_chordal_graph 77x slower (49.8s vs 0.64s) -- FIXED d3583c95a (254x self-speedup, now 3.4x faster than nx). Root cause was NOT delegation but a .subgraph().copy() construction-tax bomb in an O(n^2) loop. Remaining true gaps are tiny-absolute (biconnected_component_edges 5.5x@3ms, all_shortest_paths 11x@3ms, chain_decomposition 2x@5ms) -- low value.

Fixed: compute public auto-key over the public key set like nx. Fuzz 4000 seqs: 140 key-divergences -> 0 + 0 state-mismatch. MultiDiGraph structurally safe (verified 0/2000).

title: average_node_connectivity differs from nx on karate_club_graph
description:
fnx.average_node_connectivity(fnx.karate_club_graph()) returns 2.1907 while nx returns 2.2175 (~1.2% difference).

Test shows:
- Both are deterministic (consistent across runs)
- Edge sets are identical
- Edge iteration order appears identical
- With explicitly constructed graphs, results match perfectly

The divergence appears specific to karate_club_graph but could affect other generators. The underlying algorithm (local node connectivity via flow) may be computing different results.

Steps to reproduce:
```python
import franken_networkx as fnx
import networkx as nx
G_fnx = fnx.karate_club_graph()
G_nx = nx.karate_club_graph()
print(fnx.average_node_connectivity(G_fnx))  # 2.1907
print(nx.average_node_connectivity(G_nx))    # 2.2175
```
close_reason: Delegated to nx implementation. Rust BFS heuristic was incorrect.

title: [parity] to_nested_tuple non-canonical child order was str-sorted, not set-iteration — int trees diverged (children {2,10}: nx [2,10] vs fnx [10,2])
description: FOUND+FIXED via str-comparator bug-class audit (follow-up to b8w62 lextopo). fnx's _recurse did sorted(children, key=str); nx uses set(T[root])-{parent} (CPython set-iteration order). Canonical form matched (both sort nested tuples) but non-canonical diverged for int labels. Fix: build the real PySet set(T[node])-{parent} (same-process = nx's exact order at any hash seed), drop the str-sort; canonical sort retained. 30-tree int corpus + string, both forms (sha 91fdb9d1); full pytest 21882.
close_reason: sorted(children,key=str) -> set(T[node])-{parent} matching nx's set-iteration order via same-process PySet; canonical sort retained. Battery 91fdb9d1 (30 int trees + string, both forms); full pytest 21882. Sibling str-comparator audit: 40452 _has_odd_hole sorted(adj.keys(),key=str) flagged for next look.

title: [perf] directed/multi subgraph().degree() 20x + MultiDiGraph TIMEOUT; subgraph.edges(data) 7-8x — hot path NOT _FilteredNeighborMap
description: Noise-robust scan: subgraph.degree() Graph 20x, DiGraph 20x, MultiGraph 23x, MultiDiGraph >8s TIMEOUT (>570x) vs nx; subgraph.edges(data) ~7-8x all classes. Profiling MultiDiGraph subgraph.degree showed 25.8s in the pred descriptor lambda (__init__.py:1377) via _FilteredNeighborMap.__getitem__ — BUT a fix to _FilteredNeighborMap._raw_neighbors + _WeightAwareDegreeView._weighted_value was INERT (perf unchanged), so the unweighted+weighted subgraph degree hot path is a DIFFERENT view (likely a degree-view class that calls .succ[node]/.pred[node]/.adj[node] directly). Find the actual subgraph.degree() degree-view path and route its per-node row reads to G[node] (succ/adj) + _native_predecessor_row (pred) — those are O(deg) vs the O(N+E) .adj[node]/.succ[node]/.pred[node] view re-materialization (same root cause as subadjrow aff260c64). Blocked-ish on the in_degree/out_degree=empty bug (same view path). Use interleaved min-of-N; a hang IS the signal.
notes: reverse(copy=False).degree() FIXED: 54x -> 0.80x (FASTER than nx). TWO levers: (1) total degree is INVARIANT under edge reversal (in+out = same edge set) -> route reverse.degree to parent.degree native view (same nodes/totals/view-class DiMultiDegreeView); (2) memoised _ReverseNeighborMap._raw_neighbors per-instance (the native *_row_dict builds a fresh dict per call; degree/edges hit it ~deg times/node) — helps in/out_degree + edges which keep the swapped reverse path (their view NAME must read In/Out). Bit-exact 0/40 incl type-name+weighted+single-node, 22104 full. ALL reverse degree now ~parity. r3gjb effectively CLOSED — remaining (lower-pri): multigraph subgraph edges (set-order), subgraph-of-view edges.
close_reason: Completed nested subgraph edge-view residual: default node-set filtered-view chains now collapse to concrete native rows. rch hyperfine nested Graph 2.12727231474s -> 0.45832958964s (4.64x); nested MultiGraph 7.84972275334s -> 0.51500121244s (15.24x). Golden SHA unchanged c61e707f8b7509a8edd9eebab5ce6220e680308b1545bbe301ca72a3f95f28cf; focused view tests passed; artifact bundle tests/artifacts/perf/20260608T-subgraph-edges-residual-tealspring/.

title: fix(generators): raise NetworkXNotImplemented eagerly on undirected for directed-only generators
description: strongly_connected_components, weakly_connected_components, kosaraju_strongly_connected_components, antichains, all_topological_sorts were generator functions, so the 'if not G.is_directed(): raise NetworkXNotImplemented' guard was deferred to first iteration. nx wraps them with @not_implemented_for -> raises EAGERLY on the call. A caller doing 'try: g = scc(undirected) except NetworkXNotImplemented' catches it in nx but not fnx. Fix: do the type check eagerly, return an inner generator. Cyclic-directed NetworkXUnfeasible stays lazy (matches nx). Found via automated single-graph function sweep.
close_reason: Fixed 5 directed-only generators to raise NetworkXNotImplemented eagerly (was lazy on iteration) matching nx @not_implemented_for. Directed results unchanged; cyclic Unfeasible stays lazy; 636 existing tests pass; 11-case regression test added.

title: [perf] resistance_distance single-pair builds full dense O(n^3) pinv
description: resistance_distance(G, nodeA, nodeB) with both endpoints (the common single-scalar call, __init__.py ~L25485) builds the full Laplacian pseudo-inverse via np.linalg.pinv(L, hermitian=True) -- O(n^3) dense -- then reads two diagonal + two off-diagonal entries. The single effective resistance r(i,j)=(e_i-e_j)^T L^+ (e_i-e_j) only needs one solve of the reduced (grounded) Laplacian: drop one node, sparse-SPD solve L_red x = (e_i-e_j)_red, r = x_i - x_j. Same Woodbury/fundamental-matrix family as br-socwoodbury (bbb9f82b9). The dict/all-pairs return shapes genuinely need the full pinv -- gate the fast path on (nodeA is not None and nodeB is not None). Keep dense pinv fallback; golden sha256 (rounded) old==new==nx. Target: O(n^3)->~O(nnz) sparse solve, 50-100x on large sparse graphs for the single-pair call. See memory reference_woodbury_nsolves_in_loop.
close_reason: Shipped ab8aecbdd: grounded reduced-Laplacian sparse solve, O(n^3)->O(nnz), 225-1265x, golden sha sparse==dense==nx

title: [perf] SubgraphView.copy() 19.8x slower than nx (50-node sub of 2000-node G) — scans all parent edges O(E_total) instead of O(E_subgraph)
description:
SubgraphView.copy() 19.8x slower than nx (50-node sub of 2000-node G). cProfile root cause: generic _FilteredGraphView enumerates visible nodes by SCANNING ALL parent nodes (__iter__ -> _node_visible -> 'node in parent' PyO3) on every nodes()/_nbunch()/__len__ call = O(V_total) several times per copy (376k _node_visible for 50 copies); nodes(data=True) also walks ALL parent node-data.

DUAL FINDING - ALSO A PARITY BUG: nx FilterAtlas.__iter__ uses '2*len(subset) < len(parent)': small subgraphs iterate the node SET (CPython set order), large ones parent order. fnx always parent order -> node order diverges from nx in 46/160 random cases, ALL with 2k<n. e.g. nx [26,12,22,23] vs fnx [12,22,23,26].

REAL LEVER (target ~15-20x AND parity fix): store allowed_nodes set on the subgraph view; replicate nx FilterAtlas iteration exactly (2*len<len -> set order else parent order). Makes visible-node enumeration O(|sub|) AND matches nx order. Blast radius: generic filtered view (subgraph/edge_subgraph/subgraph_view) -> needs full differential vs nx + fnx test updates for any current-order asserts. Careful multi-step, not a rush.

REJECTED micro-lever (cc): byte-exact copy() (enumerate once + direct parent attr fetch + _edges(nbunch=visible)) preserves parent order so only 1.17x (O(V_total) list(self) remains); 180/180 byte-exact but fails Score>=2.0, not shipped per no-ceiling addendum. Set-iteration order change is mandatory for the real win.
notes:
BEING HANDLED BY TealSpring (do not duplicate): __init__.py working tree (uncommitted) has the full fix - FilterAtlas-aware _FilteredGraphView.__iter__/nodes + _NodeSetFilter(copy_nodes=False) so the allowed_nodes set is stored WITHOUT re-wrapping. Root cause confirmed by cc: _NodeSetFilter did set(already_built_set) which presizes the hash table differently than NetworkX incremental set(generator), giving a different CPython slot order -> 21/160 node-order divergences. With copy_nodes=False: 0/160 vs nx (verified). Perf already 19.8x->8x via the fast branch; residual 8x is result-graph CONSTRUCTION tax (separate). cc backed off __init__.py (TealSpring lock + active uncommitted work).

FINAL EVIDENCE: committed proof uses final rebench numbers: hyperfine fnx 669.6ms -> 499.8ms, nx comparator 192.4ms -> 204.0ms, remaining gap nx 2.45x faster; focused legacy-vs-after min 0.1804s -> 0.0437s; NetworkX golden parity hash matches after fnx.
close_reason: Completed: FilterAtlas-style sparse node iteration for SubgraphView; fnx copy benchmark 669.6ms -> 446.1ms, golden parity hash matches NetworkX

close_reason: Implemented Rust diamond_graph parity in fnx-generators

title: [perf] current_flow_betweenness dense O(n^3) Gauss-Jordan inversion is the floor (nx uses sparse solves)
description: After parallelizing the source-sink triple loop (cfbpar), current_flow_betweenness is ~parity with nx (n=300 0.71x, n=500 1.25x, n=700 1.05x) — the remaining floor is the full DENSE O(n^3) Laplacian inverse via Gauss-Jordan (sequential). nx computes current-flow betweenness via SPARSE linear solves (scipy splu/cg per source/edge) over the reduced Laplacian, not a full dense inverse, so it scales better as n grows. LEVERS: (a) parallelize the Gauss-Jordan elimination (per-column: the row-elimination loop is independent across rows; thread::scope with chunks_mut + a pivot-row snapshot; gate on n and watch per-column spawn overhead); (b) BETTER: own safe-Rust sparse Cholesky/LU on the reduced Laplacian + per-source solves (the BLAS-class mandate), avoiding the dense inverse entirely -> O(n*nnz) not O(n^3). Same applies to current_flow_closeness/information_centrality/edge_current_flow (all build the same dense L_pinv). Bit-exactness: keep the deterministic per-s ordered reduction already in place.
close_reason: FALSE PREMISE — closing. The public fnx.current_flow_betweenness_centrality DELEGATES to nx via _call_networkx_for_parity (the Rust current_flow_betweenness_centrality_rust kernel is DEAD CODE for the public path — only a docstring references it; it was abandoned because it was ~2.5x off nx). So: (1) the dense O(n^3) Gauss-Jordan 'floor' is in nx's own code, not fnx's hot path; (2) the fnx->nx conversion is only ~1% (5ms of 475ms); (3) interleaved min-of-6 shows cfb = 1.01x vs nx (PARITY) — the earlier '1.95x slower' was HOST NOISE (load swung 9->72). NOTE: last session's e3a79a847 parallelized the DEAD Rust kernel — harmless (still bit-exact in Rust tests) but it does NOT affect the public path; its '1.95x->parity' claim was a phantom (noise). A genuine native cfb would need porting nx's flow_matrix algorithm onto fnx's existing _SuperLUInverseLaplacian (like current_flow_closeness, which is native+sparse and 0.55x), but only solver='lu'/'cg' would beat nx; default solver='full' is dense either way so no default-path win. Not worth it at current parity. LESSON: verify dispatch path (delegate vs native kernel) + use interleaved min-of-N (host swings 2.5x) BEFORE optimizing.

title: MST wrapper: combined nonfinite scan -> native helper (1.7x more)
description:
After br-r37-c1-mstsync landed (250x speedup), MST wrapper was still ~3x slower than nx because two redundant Python-side edge scans dominated the budget: _has_nan_or_inf_edge_weight (1.5ms) + _mst_has_weight_edge_attr (1.2ms) on a 200-node 1k-edge graph.

Fix: (a) sync unconditionally — sync is 0.6ms, removing the conditional scan saves 1.2ms net; (b) use the native Rust _native_has_nonfinite_edge_weight helper (post-sync, ~0.05ms) instead of the Python scan; (c) fall back to the Python scan only if the native helper is unavailable or errors.

Measured impact:
  fnx.minimum_spanning_tree: 4.82ms -> 1.66ms (~2.9x faster)
  vs nx (1.18ms): 0.25x -> 0.71x (now near parity)
  vs original detour (1293ms): ~780x faster

Verified: all 17506 Python tests pass, 293 spanning-tree tests pass, 20/20 random weighted graph parity matches nx exactly.
notes: Fixed in same commit. Always-sync + native nonfinite scan = 2 fewer Python edge passes.

title: fix: greedy_color connected_sequential_bfs/dfs diverge from nx on shuffled node-insertion-order graphs
description: greedy_color(G, strategy='connected_sequential_bfs') and 'connected_sequential_dfs' produce different colorings (value AND key-order) than nx when G's node INSERTION order is a non-identity permutation of its labels. The delegation path (__init__.py ~11260) builds a structural _H via _H.add_nodes_from(G); _H.add_edges_from(G.edges()) then calls _nx.greedy_color(_H, strategy). The connected_sequential strategies BFS/DFS over _H's adjacency; _H's per-node neighbor order (from G.edges() emission order) diverges from the original gt's adjacency insertion order, so the traversal — and thus the coloring — diverges. Repro: tests/python/test_fnx_to_nx_bulk_conversion_parity.py::test_greedy_color_order_sensitive_delegation (15/20 seeds fail) + test_chordal_tournament_parity.py::test_greedy_color_strategies[connected_sequential_*]. PRE-EXISTING at HEAD (independent of the largest_first int-CSR kernel 28fe82f45; that path is byte-exact). largest_first/smallest_last/saturation strategies are unaffected. Fix likely needs _H to mirror G's exact adjacency insertion order (per-node neighbor order), not G.edges() order — or run connected_sequential in-process on the fnx graph with real adjacency order.
close_reason: Fixed 95275513f: routed connected_sequential{,_bfs,_dfs} through faithful _fnx_to_nx conversion (preserves per-node adj order) instead of the gcstruct structural shortcut. Byte-exact (value+key-order) over 60 shuffled-order graphs x 7 strategies, 0 fails; greedy/coloring/chordal suite 1459 passed (was 15 failed).

Build a persistent graph-backed per-(u,v) keydict substrate in PyMultiGraph/PyMultiDiGraph: stable dict identity, write-through semantics where NetworkX exposes them, ordering/tie-breaking parity for hash-equal nodes/keys, and no C/BLAS/native unsafe dependency. Baseline first with the mexh6 keydict harness; ship one lever only with golden sha256 and same-worker hyperfine.

Hyperfine MultiGraph regressed 1.2061778272s -> 1.30683056467s; MultiDiGraph was flat/slower 0.857003008115s -> 0.866615374185s despite direct MDG noise. Proof digests unchanged. Next attempt must target persistent graph-backed keydict substrate or native dict-subclass-equivalent construction, not Python branch-shape tweaks.
close_reason: Kept row-stamped live keydict proxy cache: direct MultiGraph 0.426395s -> 0.176107s, MultiDiGraph 0.265892s -> 0.116418s; hyperfine MultiGraph 1.206178s -> 1.035358s, MultiDiGraph 0.857003s -> 0.749146s; digests unchanged. Residual native construction routed to br-r37-c1-91hlu.

title: information_centrality: O(N²) Python inner loop dominates (~400ms of 468ms)
description:
fnx.information_centrality (alias of current_flow_closeness_centrality) ran 468ms vs nx's 27.5ms on a 200-node W-S graph (17x slower).

Breakdown:
  is_connected:          0.07ms
  cuthill-mckee:         4.0ms
  laplacian_matrix:      8.5ms
  relabel_nodes:        20.0ms
  LU construct:          1.1ms
  200x get_row:          9.2ms
  inner loop overhead: ~425ms (the bottleneck)

The O(N^2) Python double-loop calling row.item(node) and row.item(other) for n=200 means 40,000 iterations.

Fix: the inner loop is mathematically equivalent to two vectorized numpy ops per outer iteration:
  centrality[node] += n * row[node] - 2 * row.sum()
  centrality_arr += row[node]  # broadcast

Result: 468ms -> 46.78ms (~10x faster). Parity vs nx: max delta 3.12e-17 (machine precision). 18 tests pass; 17506 total tests pass.
notes: Fixed by vectorizing the O(N^2) inner loop to two numpy ops per outer iteration.

title: maximum/minimum_branching + maximum/minimum_spanning_arborescence reject MultiDiGraph but nx supports it
description: Probe found 4 sister wrappers all reject MultiDiGraph input with 'is only implemented for directed graphs' error from the Rust kernel, while nx accepts MultiDiGraph (MultiDiGraph IS directed). The Python wrappers check 'not G.is_directed()' which is False for MultiDiGraph, so they call the Rust kernel; the Rust kernel matches only GraphRef::Directed (simple DiGraph) and falls through to the wrong error. Fix: update Python wrappers to delegate is_multigraph() input to nx for these 4 functions (or extend Rust kernel — much more work). Quick fix is the wrapper delegation. Discovered alongside is_semieulerian (br-r37-c1-lg07d).

title: test/methodology: perf sweeps must use GENUINE-nx baselines (orig_func) — nx.foo(g) dispatches to fnx backend, fabricating fake gaps
description: fnx is registered as an nx backend, so nx.foo(g) for dispatchable fns does NOT measure upstream nx (dispatches to fnx / fnx-backed internals). A directed sweep produced fake 4045x/616x/328x gaps that vanish (fnx FASTER) when measured vs orig_func. Add a shared bench helper that (1) uses getattr(nx.foo,'orig_func',nx.foo) for the baseline where the fn isn't @py_random_state-decorated, (2) else compares fnx self-speedup (before/after), (3) warns when nx is suspiciously fast on an O(V^2)+ algo. Prevents wasted sessions chasing fake gaps. See reference_perf_sweep_dispatch_contamination_and_parity_state.
close_reason: Shipped 162cdfb24: _GenuineNX shim in benchmark_profiling.py swaps nx baselines to genuine upstream (orig_func, fallback for seeded builders). Was dispatch-contaminated (fnx vs itself). Now honest: 19 FASTER/1 SLOWER/0 REGRESS/0 ERRORS. Prevents fake-gap chasing.

title: perf: audit binding sites that materialize Vec<Vec<String>> then re-resolve via py_node_key (cycle_basis pattern)
description: cycle_basis (e4569e12b) was 1.9x slower largely because the kernel allocated a String per output node and the PyO3 binding re-resolved each via gr.py_node_key. Returning node INDICES from the kernel + mapping once through a cached Vec<PyObject> (refcount bump per membership) made it 2.9x faster than nx. AUDIT other delegated/native algorithms returning Vec<Vec<String>> or large Vec<String> node lists where the binding maps each via py_node_key: candidates = minimum_cycle_basis, all_simple_paths, all_shortest_paths, biconnected_components, connected_components listings, cycle enumeration, k_components. Lever: kernel returns indices; binding builds node_objs once over nodes_ordered() then indexes. Measure each: small per-pair outputs won't benefit, large multi-node outputs will.
close_reason: Audited. The index->PyObject lever only wins big for OUTPUT-MATERIALIZATION-bound functions (cycle_basis: output spans most nodes, each in many cycles -> String alloc + per-occurrence py_node_key dominated -> 2.9x faster after fix). Applied to enumerate_all_cliques (d83cc22b0, byte-exact, 1.65x->1.51x, modest because that fn is ALGORITHM-bound not materialization-bound). all_simple_paths: index lever is NEUTRAL (tried+reverted) — bottleneck is DFS traversal + per-call neighbor sort + PyO3 path materialization, NOT String output; nx does no sort so fnx stays ~1.4x on medium / parity on heavy. CONCLUSION: don't apply blindly; only worth it when py_node_key/String-output is the profiled hotspot. Remaining materialization-bound candidates to check: biconnected_components listings, connected_components (already 0.35x), find_cliques (already parity).

title: nx.scale_free_graph(initial_graph=fnx.MultiDiGraph) rejects fnx graphs via isinstance check
description: nx.generators.directed.scale_free_graph at line 261-262 checks 'if not isinstance(initial_graph, nx.MultiDiGraph): raise NetworkXError(...)'. fnx.MultiDiGraph is not a subclass of nx.MultiDiGraph, so passing an fnx multidigraph as initial_graph raises NetworkXError even though the duck-type works. Repro: nx.scale_free_graph(10, initial_graph=fnx.MultiDiGraph([(0,1),(1,2)]), seed=42). Fix: monkey-patch the function (or have fnx convert fnx multidigraphs to nx counterparts on import), or expose fnx.scale_free_graph wrapper that does the conversion. Low impact (rare initial_graph usage) but real cross-library drop-in defect.
close_reason: done

title: [architecture] Edge attr mutation via G[u][v]['k']=v not visible to Rust algorithms
description: ROOT CAUSE of br-dijkignoreweight family: when an edge attribute is mutated post-creation via G[u][v]['weight']=1.5 or via 'for u,v,attrs in G.edges(data=True): attrs[k]=v', the Python edge dict updates but the Rust inner.AttrMap does not. Rust algorithms read from inner.AttrMap and so see the original (or default) value. Verified: G.add_edge(0,1,weight=1.5) gives correct dijkstra distances, but adding without weight then setting via G[0][1]['weight']=1.5 returns hop-count. Currently mitigated by _graph_has_nonunit_weight() forcing delegation to nx (massive perf hit for any non-unit weighted query). Proper fix needs single-source-of-truth attr storage.
close_reason: Fixed: added _fnx_sync_attrs_to_inner() PyO3 method on all 4 graph types and replaced _graph_has_nonunit_weight nx-fallback with sync-then-native path. Verified raw and public Rust dijkstra/BF/FW now see post-creation G[u][v]['k']=v mutations. 12 new regression tests in test_edge_attr_post_mutation_sync.py + the existing 94 weighted shortest-path tests pass. ~2.9x faster than prior nx-fallback path. Commit 821dafd8.

title: gomory_hu_tree errors on graphs nx handles (isolated nodes, disconnected with capacities, selfloop-only)
description: Three edge cases where fnx.gomory_hu_tree raises NetworkXUnbounded while nx returns a valid tree: (1) isolated nodes only [fnx.Graph(); g.add_nodes_from([0,1,2])] — nx returns star tree with cut_value=0, fnx errors via 'not is_connected' gate; (2) disconnected graph with explicit capacities [g.add_edge(0,1,capacity=5); g.add_edge(2,3,capacity=5)] — nx returns tree connecting all nodes with cross-component weight=0, fnx errors; (3) only selfloops [g.add_edge(0,0); g.add_edge(1,1)] — nx returns tree edge (0,1) with weight=0, fnx errors via inf-capacity check (selfloops have no capacity attr but are irrelevant to inter-node cuts). Root cause: fnx's 'not is_connected OR _flow_has_infinite_capacity' gate is too aggressive. Fix: (a) drop the is_connected short-circuit — rely on the inf-capacity check (which catches the regression-locked disc-with-edges-no-cap and complete_graph-no-cap cases); (b) exclude selfloops from the inf-capacity check since selfloops contribute nothing to inter-node cuts. _minimum_cut_raw correctly returns 0 for cross-component or selfloop-only pairs, so the tree-construction loop builds the right tree without the over-broad gates.
close_reason: Replaced 'not is_connected OR inf_cap' gate with 'any non-selfloop edge with missing/inf capacity'. 6-variant parity probe matches nx; 16 gomory_hu tests pass including the regression-lock.

title: [perf] Skip PageRank weight scans when attr is absent
description: Target: profile-backed PageRank residual vs NetworkX on deterministic GNP(1000,0.05,seed=42). Baseline artifact tests/artifacts/perf/20260602T-public-algo-residual-sweep/baseline_sweep.jsonl shows fnx pagerank mean 0.023003304142s vs nx 0.015493086857s (1.4847x slower) with matching digest 58d17b197d5e3e6d9055f9a1004c4962a177e1429f85e89b44dce73673491475. Steady cProfile artifact tests/artifacts/perf/20260602T-public-algo-residual-sweep/profile_pagerank_fnx_steady.txt shows _fnx.graph_has_nonfinite_edge_weight 0.010s and _fnx.adjacency_arrays 0.012s. Alien primitive: staged early-exit attribute-presence proof; when a string weight attr is absent from every simple-graph edge, PageRank's NetworkX weight='weight' semantics are exactly unit weights, so skip nonfinite-value scan and route native COO as weight=None. One lever only; preserve node order, dict order, FP result digest, RNG seed, errors, and attr-present behavior.
close_reason: Completed: PageRank absent string weight attr now routes as unit weights after native absence proof. rch sampled fnx 0.027032583663s -> 0.024319582916s (1.1116x, 10.04% faster); hyperfine process 0.667360105957s -> 0.629373874826s (1.0604x); golden sha256 58d17b197d5e3e6d9055f9a1004c4962a177e1429f85e89b44dce73673491475 unchanged; tests and focused UBS passed.

title: _raw_eccentricity does not raise on disconnected DiGraph
description: Discovered by br-r37-c1-cvrij raw-vs-public audit (docs/raw_vs_public_audit.md). On digraph-chain-5 fixture: _raw_eccentricity returns {0:4,1:3,2:2,3:3,4:4} while both nx.eccentricity and the public fnx.eccentricity wrapper raise NetworkXError. The disconnected DiGraph case has unbounded eccentricity (some pairs unreachable) so an error is the correct behavior. The public wrapper handles this by delegating to nx for any DiGraph; a follow-up improvement would be to raise from inside the Rust kernel itself so callers of _raw_* don't get garbage data. Repro: g=fnx.DiGraph(); g.add_edge(0,1); g.add_edge(1,2); fnx._raw_eccentricity(g) returns dict; fnx.eccentricity(g) and nx.eccentricity raise NetworkXError.
close_reason: Fixed via require_undirected guard in crates/fnx-python/src/algorithms.rs eccentricity. Raw kernel now raises NetworkXNotImplemented on directed input matching the diameter/radius/center/periphery contract. Public wrapper unaffected (already gated by 'not G.is_directed()'). Refreshed docs/raw_vs_public_audit.{md,json}: error-divergence bucket cleared, eccentricity moved to identical. 2 regression tests in test_raw_vs_public_audit.py. Commit 20bc12ca.

title: hits star_graph SVD sign instability — test_hits_structural_invariants[star-5] flaky
description: test_centrality_conformance_matrix.py::test_hits_structural_invariants[star-5] is order-dependent and flaky. Both fnx.hits and nx.hits route through scipy.sparse.linalg.svds(A, k=1, v0=nstart, ...) which has SVD sign ambiguity for star graphs (the star has multiple equal singular values, so the principal eigenspace is degenerate and scipy's ARPACK may return different signs across calls). Repro: 3 calls in same process gave fnx=-0.05/nx=-0.05 (match), then fnx=-0.05/nx=0.8 (mismatch), then fnx=0.8/nx=-0.05 (mismatch). Both impls are mathematically equivalent; the test should either compare absolute values or canonicalize sign. Pre-existing — not caused by br-r37-c1-0epvo dispatcher fix.
close_reason: done

comments.author: ubuntu
comments.text: COLLISION + tested solution for handoff. A peer (TealSpring) is ACTIVELY rewriting the edge-view machinery across BOTH crates/fnx-python/src/digraph.rs and python/franken_networkx/__init__.py right now (acuub native no-data edges -> edgesdatakey 'data accepts any hashable' work) -- directly overlapping MultiDiGraph edges. Aborted my commit to avoid clobbering their in-flight rewrite (my git add captured corrupt mid-edit transients). MY TESTED SOLUTION (1561 tests pass, golden 09ff1957 bit-identical, edges() 976ms->1.23ms=793x, edges(data) 1366ms->1.12ms=1219x): (1) digraph.rs: add PyMultiDiGraph::_native_edge_view (non-shadowed alias of the native MultiDiGraphEdgeView; existing #[pymethods], no lib.rs); (2) __init__.py _MultiDiGraphEdgeView.__call__: replace the succ-AtlasView triple-loop with result.extend(self._graph._native_edge_view()(nbunch,data,keys,default)) -- BUT GATE on , else keep the Python loop (parse_view_data only handles bool/str; data=None/non-str attr names need attrs.get(data,default) -- this is exactly the out_edges.data(None,default=7) parity test that breaks otherwise); (3) _LiveMultiEdgeCallView.__iter__: for the directed bare edges() case, iterate self._graph._native_edge_view()(None,False,False,None). Recommend the peer fold this into their edgesdatakey rewrite. NOTE: out_edges/in_edges route through self.edges() so they get the speedup for free.

title: perf: shortest_path_length 10.9x slower than nx
notes: Root cause: Rust kernel uses string node keys requiring string hashing (O(len) per lookup). nx uses Python integers which hash in O(1). For fast BFS on integer-keyed graphs, string hashing overhead dominates. Architectural: would require specializing internal representation for integer keys to fix.
close_reason: wontfix: string-key hashing overhead, README updated

title: perf: generator construction-tax cluster (lollipop 4.6x, caveman 3.4x, turan 2.4x, wheel/star ~2x, dorogovtsev 1.8x)
description: Interleaved sweep found these generators slower than nx (likely per-edge add_edge construction tax or O(n^2) edge build, fixable via bulk add_edges_from or a smarter edge enumeration). Sizes: lollipop_graph(500,500) 246ms vs nx 53ms (4.6x); caveman_graph(50,50) 100ms vs 29ms (3.4x); turan_graph(300,5) 35ms vs 15ms (2.4x); wheel_graph(3000) 7.4ms vs 3.4ms (2.2x); star_graph(5000) 6.9ms vs 3.4ms (2.0x); dorogovtsev_goltsev_mendes(8) 8.1ms vs 4.5ms (1.8x); plus complete_bipartite/full_rary_tree/balanced_tree/circular_ladder/random_regular 1.3-1.5x. title: fix(self-loops): local_bridges + degree_assortativity_coefficient self-loop parity
description: Same self-loop edge-case sweep as br-r37-c1-1boe3, two more functions: (1) local_bridges discarded the endpoints before intersecting neighbor sets, but nx tests set(G[u])&set(G[v]) WITHOUT discarding — a self-loop on an endpoint makes it a shared neighbor so the edge is NOT a local bridge; for loopless graphs the discard is a no-op so both agree. Fixed by dropping the discard and gating the Rust fast-path on no self-loops. (2) degree_assortativity_coefficient's Rust fast-path miscomputes the degree-mixing statistic with self-loops; delegate self-loop graphs to nx (like the multigraph/directed cases). Verified 0 mismatch across 600+500 self-loop graphs, 0 regression on loopless.

title: perf: construction tax — wrapper add_nodes_from/from_dict_of_dicts loop per-element in Python instead of calling native bulk bindings
description:
QUANTIFIED 2026-06-09 (genuine-nx baselines, warm min-of-N):
- add_nodes_from(list(range(50000))): fnx 81.3ms vs nx 13.8ms = 5.91x SLOWER
- from_dict_of_dicts (gnm 5k/20k): fnx 64.8ms vs nx 29.2ms = 2.22x SLOWER
- add_edges_from (20k): fnx 24.1ms vs nx 15.7ms = 1.53x SLOWER
(copy 0.11x, Graph(G) 0.09x, DiGraph(G) 0.16x, disjoint_union 0.34x already FASTER.)

ROOT CAUSE: the Python wrappers in __init__.py loop per element calling
bound_add_node / add_edge across the PyO3 boundary once per node/edge:
- _make_add_nodes_from (__init__.py ~2408): for n in nodes: bound_add_node(n).
  A native fast path (fast_add_int_nodes_range_stop) exists but ONLY fires for
  range(0,stop,1) + type(G) is Graph + no attr. LIST inputs take the slow loop.
- from_dict_of_dicts (__init__.py ~44662): pure-Python per-edge add_edge +
  graph[u][v].update(attrs).

FIX (small, high-value, but in LOCKED __init__.py — needs the lock holder):
1. add_nodes_from: when type(nodes) is list/tuple (not range), call the EXISTING
   native bulk binding PyGraph::add_nodes_from (crates/fnx-python/src/lib.rs:2432)
   which crosses PyO3 once and loops in Rust — instead of the per-node Python
   loop. Native bulk path is already used internally by copy/union (lib.rs
   4591/7098) and is correct for plain-node + (node,attr)-tuple inputs.
2. from_dict_of_dicts: add a native bulk builder (simple-graph, no
   multigraph_input) in fnx-python/src that ingests the dict-of-dicts in one Rust
   call (mirror to_dict_of_dicts_undirected which already exists), route the
   common case to it.

The native-side bulk bindings are UNLOCKED and can be added/verified now; the
1-line wrapper redirects need the __init__.py lock (BoldFalcon). Behavior parity:
native add_nodes_from already first-wins + preserves Py object; verify golden
node/edge order + attr dicts vs the Python loop on a corpus before/after.
close_reason: Shipped 521ac87b9: wired native bulk _fast_add_int_nodes for list/tuple of ints — add_nodes_from(list(range(50000))) 92.44ms->15.39ms = 6.0x self, 5.84x slower vs nx -> 1.01x PARITY. Byte-identical (14-scenario corpus sha 606fe8a9 on old AND new builds). 494 pytest pass. NOTE: from_dict_of_dicts (2.22x) + add_edges_from (1.53x) remain open under the same construction-tax theme — file follow-up if pursued.

title: [bug][parity] pickle round-trip scrambles adjacency ROW ORDER (pre-existing, all builds) — __setstate__ rebuild order != nx's verbatim dict preservation
description: pickle.loads(pickle.dumps(G)) reorders adjacency rows: grid_2d 4x4 row (0,1) [(1,1),(0,0),(0,2)] -> [(0,0),(1,1),(0,2)]. nx pickle preserves dict structure verbatim. Pre-existing at HEAD (python-built graphs too, verified pre-dnv8g). Same rebuild-vs-preserve family as the deepcopy fix in 0ek49 (0beb08e48): __setstate__ rebuilds via some non-insertion-ordered walk. Fix sketch: serialize/restore adjacency row order (or rebuild via the stored u-major edge sequence with phase-preserving orientation like Graph::grid_2d does). Probe: {n: list(g.adj[n])} canon before/after round-trip on graphs with both row+col edges.
close_reason: Fixed 2145ae2a0: state carries explicit adj/succ/pred rows + display-override maps (optional, back-compat); __setstate__ applies via new fnx-classes apply_row_orders. Also fixed DiGraph's random-HashMap edge serialization (dropped sparse-mirror edges). 4-class battery + copies + mixed keys + native grid all == nx round-trips; full pytest 21565 green.

title: nx.spring_layout(fnx_graph) silently drops edges via _process_params isinstance check
description: nx.drawing.layout._process_params has 'if not isinstance(G, nx.Graph): G = empty_graph + add_nodes_from(G)'. Because fnx.Graph is not a subclass of nx.Graph, every fnx graph routed through nx.spring_layout (and any other nx layout fn using _process_params) loses all edges and the layout runs on a node-only graph — producing positions that ignore connectivity. Affected functions include spring_layout, fruchterman_reingold_layout, kamada_kawai_layout, spectral_layout, planar_layout, etc. Repro: nx.spring_layout(fnx.path_graph(5), seed=42) differs from nx.spring_layout(nx.path_graph(5), seed=42). Fix: monkey-patch nx.drawing.layout._process_params on fnx import so it auto-converts fnx→nx, OR register fnx graph classes as virtual subclasses of nx.Graph.
close_reason: done

title: attribute_mixing_dict + degree_mixing_matrix: G.adjacency() is 12x slower than G._adj.items()
description:
fnx.attribute_assortativity_coefficient ran 10.83ms vs nx 0.51ms (21x slower) on a 200-node W-S graph. Both attribute_mixing_dict (used by assortativity) and degree_mixing_matrix iterate G.adjacency() which materializes an AdjacencyView per yielded (u, nbrsdict) pair — measured 7.72ms for n=200 m=1000 vs 0.64ms for G._adj.items() (12x slower).

Also: attribute_mixing_dict called G.nodes[u].get(attribute, None) per node, adding ~1ms of PyO3 round-trips. Replaced with a single get_node_attributes() bulk call.

Combined fix in attribute_mixing_dict + degree_mixing_matrix: switch G.adjacency() -> G._adj.items() (same yield shape, no AdjacencyView), snapshot attrs via get_node_attributes.

Result: attribute_assortativity_coefficient 10.83ms -> 2.83ms (~4x faster). Now 5.5x of nx (was 21x). 346 assortativity+mixing tests pass; 17506 total tests pass.
notes: Fixed in same commit. G._adj.items() iter for both attribute_mixing_dict and degree_mixing_matrix; get_node_attributes() bulk call for attr lookup.

title: perf: native MULTIGRAPH construction batch (add_edges_from 4x, propagates to set-ops/copy/generators)
description: MultiGraph/MultiDiGraph add_edges_from is 4.0x/3.66x vs nx (291ms/280ms vs 72ms @ N=2000/m=40000) because multigraph has NO native plain-edge batch — it falls to per-edge Python _multi_add_edges_from -> Multi*Graph.add_edge (cProfile: Python add_edge = 0.23s of 0.6s). The simple Graph/DiGraph batch (add_plain_edge_batch, now both lazy) does NOT exist for multigraph. SAFE DESIGN (low parity risk, reuses PROVEN copy-path machinery): add a PyMultiGraph/PyMultiDiGraph binding that walks the source inner (nodes_ordered/neighbors/edge_keys) like _native_to_directed_deepcopy (lib.rs:3420) / copy() already do, builds a (u,v,internal_key,empty_attrs) batch via remember_edge_key_object + extend_keyed_edges_with_attrs_unrecorded (inner method ALREADY EXISTS: lib.rs:2316, digraph.rs:1794), with lazy edge_py_attrs. The internal-key + display-key handling is IDENTICAL to the copy path (NOT reimplemented), so key/order parity is inherited. For set-ops add an H-membership filter (pass the Python h_set, check py-display (u,v,key) membership). Target: multigraph add_edges_from/set-ops/copy 4x -> ~1.2x. This is the construction-tax band's biggest remaining lever.

RESIDUALS (lower value): (1) 3-tuple (u,v,key) explicit-key batch would extend this to set-ops (difference/sym_diff use 3-tuples) — needs key-object->internal mapping (resolve_internal_edge_key) per edge, more care; (2) non-FRESH multigraph add_edges_from still per-edge (gap-aware keys). comments.author: ubuntu
comments.text: Target: bfs_tree(BA(3000,4,seed=42),0) 21.9ms -> match/beat nx 4.1ms. Root cause: RuntimePolicy carries an unbounded EvidenceLedger (Vec<DecisionRecord>) ~3000 records for a 3000-node BA graph. bfs_tree deep-clones it twice (new_empty throwaway + final set_runtime_policy) and drops one (~13ms). Primitive: the construction-time policy is fully OVERWRITTEN by the trailing set_runtime_policy, so replace the throwaway clone with cheap RuntimePolicy::new(mode) -- provably zero observable change. Expected ~8ms saved.

title: resistance_distance uses wrong weight when weight=None
description:
When `weight=None` is passed to `fnx.resistance_distance`, the implementation uses `weight or "weight"` which evaluates to `"weight"`, causing it to use edge weights when None was intended.

**Test case:**
```python
G = fnx.karate_club_graph()  # has weight=4 on edges
fnx.resistance_distance(G, 0, 1, weight=None)  # Returns 0.0635
nx.resistance_distance(G, 0, 1, weight=None)   # Returns 0.1931
```

**Root cause:** Line 23840:
`L = laplacian_matrix(G, nodelist=nodelist, weight=weight or "weight")`

The `weight or "weight"` expression evaluates to "weight" when weight=None, but should be None.

**Fix:** Change to pass `weight` directly instead of `weight or "weight"`.
close_reason: fixed

title: [perf] value-only max-flow for connectivity (skip discarded partition/flow-dict)
description: After br-flowint (6b4c7b8f7) the Edmonds-Karp inner loop is integer-fast and maximum_flow_value beats nx, but edge_connectivity is still ~2-3x slower (n=500: 3.12x). Cause: global_edge_connectivity_edmonds_karp calls minimum_cut_edmonds_karp |D| times and reads ONLY cut.value, yet each call (a) re-materializes the String residual HashMap, (b) rebuilds a String-keyed reverse_residual, (c) runs a String-sorted reachability BFS to build a min-cut PARTITION that is then discarded, and (d) flow_edges_from_residual builds an unused flow dict. Lever: add a value-only max-flow (returns f64, no residual String round-trip, no flow-dict, no partition) and route global_edge/node_connectivity + the local s-t value paths to it (max-flow value == min-cut value by maxflow-mincut theorem; edge_connectivity only needs the cardinality). Keep minimum_cut's partition path for callers that need it. Golden sha edge_connectivity==nx over random graphs; target edge_connectivity from 3x slower to FASTER than nx. See tests/artifacts/perf/20260604T-maxflow-integer-residual.
close_reason: Shipped 3e149f027: value-only compute_max_flow_value, edge_connectivity 6.7x slower -> 0.57-1.04x (faster/parity vs nx), golden c2694ffe == nx

title: [perf] double_edge_swap 188x slower — remove_edge O(E) x nswap = O(nswap*E); same root cause as watts mdff1
description: Profile-backed: nx.double_edge_swap(fnx_graph 2000n/8000e, nswap=2000) = 3938ms vs nx 20.9ms = 188x. Same root cause as the (now-fixed) watts_strogatz mdff1: each swap does 2x remove_edge + 2x add_edge on fnx-classes Graph; remove_edge is O(E) (self.edges.shift_remove_full on the edges IndexMap), so nswap swaps = O(nswap*E). FIX OPTIONS: (a) the BROAD lever — make fnx-classes Graph.remove_edge O(1): shift_remove_full->swap_remove_full on the edges IndexMap (edge keys are (node_idx,node_idx) so removing one entry needs NO rekey of others; only edges-map ITERATION ORDER changes — verify edges_ordered() walks ADJACENCY not edge-map order, which the substrate epoch established, so swap_remove should be output-safe; full pytest + golden sha the gate). This single change fixes double_edge_swap, watts (further), AND every many-removal algo at once. (b) native double_edge_swap kernel with local O(1) rewire (like watts mdff1) — narrower. Prefer (a) if edges-map order truly unused for output. CONSTRAINT: byte-identical + full suite green.
notes: PARTIAL: applied the broad swap_remove fix (remove_edge shift_remove_full->swap_remove_full + edge_index_endpoints swap_remove) — O(E)->O(1) remove, parity-clean full pytest 22040, scipy/adjacency_matrix order-independent (CSR canonicalizes). This fixes the remove-edge-IN-A-LOOP class (10k-remove loop 2.14x vs nx, was O(E^2)). BUT it did NOT fix double_edge_swap (188x->167x only): profiling shows double_edge_swap's cost is its OWN Python loop body in __init__.py:26346 (5.8s tottime in the function; graph ops are <0.04s), NOT remove_edge. double_edge_swap fix needs editing __init__.py (CONTESTED by TealSpring zge63) — likely rebuilding cumulative degree distribution or list(edges) per swap. Keep open for the __init__.py loop fix when uncontested.
close_reason: double_edge_swap 167x->2.16x: the cost was its OWN Python loop rebuilding edges=list(G.edges()) after EVERY successful swap (O(nswap*E)), NOT remove_edge. Replaced with O(1) in-place slot updates (edges[i1]=(u,x); edges[i2]=(v,y)) — same uniform-edge-pick rng pattern, degree-preservation + max_tries/exception contracts intact (208 swap/parity tests + full pytest 22043). fnx's uniform-pick is an intentional divergence from nx's degree-CDF algo so only degree-seq parity is owed. (remove_edge swap_remove from 7ae455b5c also landed — helps remove-loops generally.) NOTE: connected_double_edge_swap (27743) has the same per-iter list(G.edges()) rebuild + a connectivity check — separate follow-up (in-place needs revert-on-failed-connectivity logic).
