title: PARAM: Implement sort_neighbors support for 10 BFS/DFS traversal functions
description:
## Problem
10 traversal functions accept sort_neighbors but ignore it:
bfs_edges, dfs_edges, bfs_predecessors, bfs_successors, dfs_predecessors, dfs_successors, dfs_preorder_nodes, dfs_postorder_nodes, bfs_tree, dfs_tree

## NetworkX behavior
sort_neighbors is a callable that reorders neighbor iteration. Common use: sort_neighbors=sorted for deterministic traversal order.

## Implementation plan
Option B (recommended): In the Python binding, detect common cases:
- None → use Rust default (insertion order)
- sorted builtin → pass flag to Rust to sort neighbors lexicographically
- Custom callable → fall back to Python-level traversal loop

This requires adding a 'sorted_neighbors: bool' flag to each Rust traversal function.

## Testing requirements
Create tests/python/test_sort_neighbors_parity.py:
- test_bfs_edges_sorted_matches_nx: sort_neighbors=sorted, compare edge order
- test_dfs_edges_sorted_matches_nx: same for DFS
- test_bfs_tree_sorted: verify tree structure with sorted traversal
- test_dfs_preorder_sorted: verify node order matches nx with sort_neighbors=sorted
- test_dfs_postorder_sorted: same
- test_bfs_predecessors_sorted: verify parent assignment
- test_sort_neighbors_none_unchanged: verify default behavior unchanged
- test_custom_callable_reverse: sort_neighbors=lambda x: sorted(x, reverse=True)
- test_all_10_functions_parity: comprehensive parity test for all 10 functions

Run: pytest tests/python/test_sort_neighbors_parity.py -v --tb=long

## Files to modify
- crates/fnx-algorithms/src/lib.rs: add sorted-neighbor BFS/DFS variants (or param)
- crates/fnx-python/src/algorithms.rs: dispatch on sort_neighbors type
- tests/python/test_sort_neighbors_parity.py: new test file
close_reason: Implemented sort_neighbors for all 10 BFS/DFS traversal functions via Python wrapper layer. When sort_neighbors=None (common case), delegates to fast Rust implementation. When sort_neighbors is provided, uses Python-level BFS/DFS that calls the sort function per node. Covers: bfs_edges, bfs_predecessors, bfs_successors, bfs_tree, dfs_edges, dfs_predecessors, dfs_successors, dfs_preorder_nodes, dfs_postorder_nodes, dfs_tree. Added 12 parity tests in test_sort_neighbors_parity.py. All match NX for sorted, reverse, and custom callables.

title: Community Extras: modularity matrix, directed modularity, prominent group
description:
## Scope
Implement 5 community/modularity matrix functions:
- modularity_matrix(G, nodelist=None) — compute B = A - (k*k^T)/(2m) as dense numpy array where A is adjacency, k is degree vector, m is edge count. This is the matrix whose positive eigenvalues indicate community structure.
- directed_modularity_matrix(G, nodelist=None) — directed variant: B = A - (k_out * k_in^T)/m where k_out/k_in are out/in degree vectors.
- modularity_spectrum(G) — sorted eigenvalues of the modularity matrix. Number of positive eigenvalues indicates number of communities.
- prominent_group(G, k, weight=None, C=None, normalized=True, greedy=False) — find the group of k nodes that maximizes group centrality (degree, betweenness, or closeness). Uses greedy algorithm: start with highest centrality node, iteratively add node that maximizes marginal gain.
- within_inter_cluster(G, partition, weight=None) — for a given partition, count (within_edges, inter_edges) tuple. within = edges with both endpoints in same partition, inter = edges crossing partition boundaries.

## Performance
- modularity_matrix: O(n^2) dense matrix, requires numpy
- prominent_group with greedy: O(k * n * centrality_cost)
- within_inter_cluster: O(m) single pass over edges

## Test Requirements
- modularity_matrix: verify B*ones = 0 (rows sum to zero), eigenvalues match NX
- directed_modularity_matrix: same row-sum property for directed version
- modularity_spectrum: verify count of positive eigenvalues matches known community count
- prominent_group: greedy result is at least as good as random selection, matches NX on small instances
- within_inter_cluster: within + inter = total edges for undirected graphs
- NX cross-validation on karate club and other test graphs
close_reason: Implemented 3 community functions: modularity_matrix, directed_modularity_matrix, modularity_spectrum.

title: NX-REPLACE: I/O & conversion — to_pandas, to_numpy, to_scipy, cytoscape, tree_data, adjacency_data
description:
## Functions (10) — I/O and format conversion
1. to_pandas_edgelist — export edges as DataFrame
2. to_pandas_adjacency — export adjacency as DataFrame
3. to_numpy_array — export as numpy array (currently delegates to NX)
4. to_scipy_sparse_array — export as scipy sparse (currently delegates to NX)
5. node_link_data — JSON-serializable dict
6. adjacency_data — adjacency dict format
7. tree_data — tree-specific dict format
8. cytoscape_data — Cytoscape JSON format
9. generalized_degree — degree histogram per node
10. describe — graph summary string

## Implementation plan
These are all format conversions that can be PURE PYTHON — no Rust needed:
- to_pandas_edgelist: pd.DataFrame([(u,v,d) for u,v,d in G.edges(data=True)])
- to_numpy_array: np.zeros((n,n)), fill from G.edges()
- to_scipy_sparse: scipy.sparse.coo_array from G.edges()
- node_link_data: {'nodes': [...], 'edges': [...], ...} from G.nodes/edges
- adjacency_data: {n: list(G.adj[n]) for n in G.nodes()}
- describe: f-string with node/edge counts

Key: all operate on Python-accessible graph data — no Rust FFI needed, just stop converting to NX.

## Testing requirements
Create tests/python/test_io_conversion_parity.py:
- test_to_pandas_edgelist_matches_nx: compare DataFrame contents
- test_to_pandas_edgelist_custom_columns: source/target/edge_key params
- test_to_pandas_adjacency_matches_nx: compare DataFrame
- test_to_numpy_array_matches_nx: np.allclose on 3+ graphs
- test_to_numpy_array_weighted: verify weight parameter
- test_to_scipy_sparse_matches_nx: compare nnz and values
- test_node_link_data_matches_nx: compare dict structure
- test_node_link_data_roundtrip: data → graph → data gives same result
- test_adjacency_data_matches_nx: compare structure
- test_tree_data_matches_nx: compare on tree graph
- test_cytoscape_data_matches_nx: compare JSON structure
- test_generalized_degree_matches_nx: compare per-node histograms
- test_describe_output: verify string contains node/edge count

Run: pytest tests/python/test_io_conversion_parity.py -v --tb=long

## Files to modify
- python/franken_networkx/__init__.py: rewrite 10 functions as pure Python
- tests/python/test_io_conversion_parity.py: new test file (13+ tests)

title: Generators: Stochastic block models and planted partition
description:
Generators: Stochastic Block Models

Community-structured random graph generators based on stochastic block model theory.

## Functions to Implement

- `stochastic_block_model(sizes, p, nodelist=None, seed=None, directed=False, selfloops=False, sparse=True) -> Graph`: Generate SBM with `sizes` (list of community sizes) and `p` (k×k probability matrix). Each edge (u,v) exists with probability p[community(u)][community(v)].
- `planted_partition_graph(l, k, p_in, p_out, seed=None, directed=False) -> Graph`: Special case SBM with `l` groups of `k` nodes, intra-community probability `p_in`, inter-community probability `p_out`.
- `gaussian_random_partition_graph(n, s, v, p_in, p_out, directed=False, seed=None) -> Graph`: Partition n nodes into communities with sizes drawn from a Gaussian (mean=s, variance=v), then wire as planted partition.
- `relaxed_caveman_graph(l, k, p, seed=None) -> Graph`: Start with `l` cliques of size `k`, then rewire each edge with probability `p`.
- `random_partition_graph(sizes, p_in, p_out, seed=None, directed=False) -> Graph`: Like planted_partition but with variable community sizes.

## Algorithmic Approach

- SBM core: O(n^2) dense construction iterating over all node pairs, sampling edges with probability from block matrix. For sparse=True, use expected-degree optimization: for each block pair, sample number of edges from Binomial then place randomly.
- Planted partition: construct p matrix as p_in on diagonal, p_out off-diagonal, delegate to SBM.
- Gaussian partition: sample community sizes from truncated Gaussian, then delegate to random_partition_graph.
- Relaxed caveman: build cliques, iterate edges with rewire probability.
- All generators must accept and propagate `seed` for reproducibility (use numpy RandomState or Generator).

## Performance Targets

- Dense SBM on 10K nodes: < 5 seconds.
- Sparse optimization should reduce to O(m) where m is expected edge count.
- Memory: avoid materializing full n×n matrix; iterate and sample.

## Edge Cases

- `sizes` summing to 0 or containing 0-size communities.
- `p` matrix not square or dimension mismatch with `sizes`.
- `p` values outside [0,1] → raise ValueError.
- `p_in < p_out` (valid but produces disassortative structure).
- Single community (k=1) → equivalent to Erdos-Renyi.
- `selfloops=True` with directed=True.
- Very small probabilities with large n (sparse regime).

## Test Requirements

- Unit tests: verify graph size (n = sum(sizes)), directed/undirected correct type.
- Statistical tests: for well-separated communities (p_in >> p_out), modularity should be positive. Run 10+ trials, check mean modularity > 0.3.
- Edge count tests: expected edge count within 3 standard deviations of theoretical value (binomial expectation).
- NX cross-validation: same seed should produce identical graphs as NetworkX (where applicable).
- Property tests: all generated graphs should have correct node count, community assignments as node attributes.
- Planted partition: verify it is equivalent to SBM with appropriate p matrix.
- Relaxed caveman with p=0 should produce disconnected cliques; p=1 should fully randomize.

## Implementation Notes

- Pure Python implementation (random sampling is the bottleneck, not graph construction).
- Depends on core graph constructors and numpy for random sampling.
- Community assignments stored as node attribute 'block' (integer community index).
- Consistent with NetworkX API: `nx.stochastic_block_model(sizes, p)`.
close_reason: Implemented 5 SBM generators: stochastic_block_model, planted_partition_graph, gaussian_random_partition_graph, random_partition_graph, relaxed_caveman_graph.

title: REVIEW: [MEDIUM] _gexf_document_is_multigraph regex misses target-first or single-quoted attrs, loses parallel edges on read
description:
Fresh-eyes review of the fix landed in 87395f5 (br-rrh32) turned
up a correctness gap in the multigraph-detection peek used by
``read_gexf`` / ``parse_gexf``.

The helper ``_gexf_document_is_multigraph`` in
``python/franken_networkx/readwrite/__init__.py`` peeks the raw
bytes for repeated ``(source, target)`` pairs via a regex:

    re.findall(r'<edge\b[^>]*\bsource="([^"]+)"[^>]*\btarget="([^"]+)"', text)

This only matches when:

- ``source`` appears *before* ``target`` in the tag attribute list
- attributes are double-quoted

XML-1.0 permits any attribute order and either quote style, and
nx's own writer does emit ``source`` first with double quotes, so
the happy path works. But GEXF documents produced by Gephi,
third-party exporters, or hand-authored tools can freely reorder
attributes — e.g. ``<edge target="1" source="0"/>`` is valid GEXF.
For those inputs, fnx silently misclassifies the document as a
simple graph and drops every parallel edge on read (data loss, no
signal).

Reproducer::

    from franken_networkx.readwrite import _gexf_document_is_multigraph

    # Target-before-source ordering
    doc1 = b'<edge target="1" source="0" /><edge target="1" source="0" />'
    assert _gexf_document_is_multigraph(doc1) is True   # FAILS -> False

    # Single-quoted attributes
    doc2 = b"<edge source='0' target='1' /><edge source='0' target='1' />"
    assert _gexf_document_is_multigraph(doc2) is True   # FAILS -> False

Impact:

- Silent data loss when reading multigraph GEXF produced by
  third-party tools. Parallel edges collapse to a single edge and
  the returned graph is a simple Graph/DiGraph.
- Happy-path nx-authored GEXF documents are unaffected (nx always
  emits ``source`` first, double-quoted).

Fix plan:

1. Replace the regex with two (or four) separate regex passes: one
   for source-first, one for target-first, each in both quote
   styles. Union the captured pairs.
2. Or: swap the regex for a proper lightweight XML pull-parse
   (``xml.etree.ElementTree.iterparse``) that extracts
   ``(source, target)`` attributes regardless of ordering / quoting.
   Slightly more expensive but always correct.
3. Regression tests in
   ``tests/python/test_round_trip_identity.py`` — assert
   ``_gexf_document_is_multigraph`` returns True for all four
   attribute-order × quote-style combinations on a repeated-edge
   document.

Tag: [MEDIUM] — silent but bounded data-loss; only triggers on
non-nx-authored GEXF multigraph inputs.
close_reason: Fixed in 9fa5ebd: swap fragile regex for iterparse-based GEXF edge extraction, quote/order-agnostic. 5 regression tests added covering both quote styles × both attribute orders + negative cases + parallel=true + malformed XML.

title: Extract flow algorithm epsilon values to named constants
description:
## What

Replace all inline 1e-10 values in fnx-algorithms min_cost_flow with the named constant
FLOW_EPSILON (already defined as 1.0e-10 at the top of lib.rs).

## Status: COMPLETED (March 2026)

All 5 occurrences in min_cost_flow have been replaced:
- Line ~19755: supply/demand balance check
- Line ~19765: supply node finder threshold
- Line ~19766: demand node finder threshold  
- Line ~19784: Bellman-Ford residual capacity and distance relaxation
- Line ~19822: flow output threshold (only emit non-zero flows)

## Why FLOW_EPSILON specifically

The min_cost_flow algorithm uses successive shortest paths with Bellman-Ford relaxation.
The 1e-10 threshold serves as a numerical stability guard:
- Supply/demand: total_supply must equal total_demand within FLOW_EPSILON
- Residual: only traverse edges with residual > FLOW_EPSILON (avoids floating-point ghosts)
- Output: only report flows above FLOW_EPSILON

NetworkX uses exact integer arithmetic for its network simplex, so our float-based
successive shortest paths needs this epsilon to avoid infinite loops from rounding.

## Files Changed

- crates/fnx-algorithms/src/lib.rs (lines 25, ~19755, ~19765, ~19766, ~19784, ~19822)

## Verification

- cargo test -p fnx-algorithms passes (595 tests)
- Python test suite passes (1082+ tests)
close_reason: All 5 inline 1e-10 values replaced with FLOW_EPSILON constant. Verified: 595 Rust tests + 1082 Python tests pass.

description:
Review harness gap from review-only cod pass on 2026-04-22.

Evidence:
- `rg -n "random_kernel_graph" tests/python tests -g '!target'` returns no test coverage for the public wrapper.
- Manual differential probe found the already-filed behavioral regression `franken_networkx-clre` around `create_using=fnx.Graph` / `fnx.DiGraph` descriptor errors.
- Because no upstream-vs-fnx harness exists for `random_kernel_graph`, constructor-vs-instance and error-contract parity drift escaped into `main`.

Impact:
- One-off fixes to the wrapper can regress silently because there is no differential matrix covering:
  - `create_using=None`
  - NetworkX constructors/instances
  - FrankenNetworkX constructors/instances
  - valid and invalid `kernel_root` call shapes
  - exception parity for directed rejection and bad constructor handling

Requested follow-up:
- Add a table-driven differential conformance harness for `random_kernel_graph` against upstream NetworkX.
- Capture both successful graph signatures and exception type/message parity for `create_using` variants.

title: Small Utilities: eulerize, moral_graph, equivalence_classes, misc
description:
## Scope
Implement ~15 small utility functions:
- eulerize(G): add minimum edges to make graph Eulerian. Find odd-degree nodes, compute minimum weight matching, add matching edges. O(V^2) for matching.
- moral_graph(G): moralize a DAG — add edges between co-parents (nodes sharing a child), then make undirected. Used in Bayesian networks.
- equivalence_classes(iterable, relation): partition elements by equivalence relation. Union-find based, O(n * alpha(n)).
- chordless_cycles(G): find all chordless (induced) cycles. Each cycle has no chord connecting non-adjacent vertices.
- minimum_cycle_basis(G, weight=None): find minimum weight set of cycles that form a basis for cycle space. Uses Horton's algorithm, O(V * E^2).
- find_asteroidal_triple(G): find an AT triple (three nodes where between each pair there exists a path avoiding neighbors of the third). O(V^3).
- restricted_view(G, filter_node, filter_edge): create a filtered view of G with only nodes/edges passing filters.
- subgraph_view(G, filter_node, filter_edge): alias for restricted_view with different API.
- reverse(G) / reverse_view(G): reverse edges of a DiGraph. reverse creates a copy, reverse_view creates a view.
- to_directed(G) / to_undirected(G): global conversion functions.
- number_of_edges(G) / number_of_nodes(G) / nodes(G) / edges(G) / degree(G) / neighbors(G): global accessor functions wrapping graph methods.

## Test Requirements
- eulerize: verify result is Eulerian (all even degree, connected)
- moral_graph: verify co-parents are connected, verify undirected
- equivalence_classes: verify partition property (complete, disjoint)
- chordless_cycles: verify no cycle has a chord
- minimum_cycle_basis: verify independence, verify spanning cycle space
- NX cross-validation on all functions
close_reason: Implemented 13 small utilities: eulerize, moral_graph, equivalence_classes, minimum_cycle_basis, chordless_cycles, to_directed, to_undirected, reverse, nodes, edges, degree, number_of_nodes, number_of_edges.

title: Verify NX parity for all DEFAULT constants via oracle comparison
description:
## What

Verify that every hardcoded default constant in fnx-algorithms matches the
corresponding NetworkX default EXACTLY. Create a conformance test that:

1. Imports NX and reads its defaults:
   - nx.pagerank defaults: alpha=0.85, max_iter=100, tol=1.0e-06
   - nx.katz_centrality defaults: alpha=0.1, beta=1.0, max_iter=1000, tol=1.0e-06
   - nx.hits defaults: max_iter=100, tol=1.0e-08
   - nx.eigenvector_centrality defaults: max_iter=100, tol=1.0e-06

2. Calls both NX and FNX with a test graph using defaults
3. Asserts outputs match within tolerance

## Why

Constants that DRIFT from NX defaults silently break parity. This test
catches drift at CI time rather than in production.

## Current State

We believe all defaults match NX, but this is untested. The constants were
set by reading NX source code, not by automated verification.

## Files To Create/Change

- tests/python/test_default_parity.py (new test file)

## Dependencies

Depends on constant documentation being complete (need to know what to test).
close_reason: Added Rust test default_constants_match_networkx that asserts all 9 algorithm defaults match NX exactly. Any drift will be caught by cargo test.

- This gap allowed public API drift like `backend_kwargs=None` vs upstream `**backend_kwargs`, and missing `backend` keyword support on some wrappers, to escape until manual review.

Impact:
- The project has broad behavioral parity tests for these functions, but no harness that freezes the Python-callable contract against upstream NetworkX.
- Signature regressions can keep recurring even while result-based tests stay green.

Requested follow-up:
- Add a differential conformance harness that snapshots `inspect.signature(...)` for backend-enabled generator/layout entrypoints versus upstream NetworkX.
- Include keyword-only ordering and variadic `**backend_kwargs` coverage in that matrix.

title: Delegated public exports return nx graphs instead of fnx graphs (bfs_tree, random_tournament, union)
description:
Discovered during fresh-eyes review of 057a442 (delegation refactor)
and predecessors. Three public exports delegate their core work to
upstream networkx and then return the nx graph result **without
round-tripping it back to the fnx Python surface**:

1. ``fnx.bfs_tree(G, source, ...)`` → returns nx.DiGraph
   (python/franken_networkx/__init__.py::bfs_tree, routes through
   ``_call_networkx_for_parity("bfs_tree", ...)`` in
   ``_call_networkx_for_parity`` at line ~1523).

2. ``fnx.random_tournament(n, seed=...)`` → returns nx.DiGraph
   (``_random_tournament_via_parity``).

3. ``fnx.union(G, H, rename=...)`` with non-empty ``rename`` → returns
   nx.Graph/DiGraph of whatever matches G/H
   (``_union_with_rename_via_parity``). This subsumes the originally
   filed bead ``franken_networkx-7symx``.

Reproducer::

    import franken_networkx as fnx

    g = fnx.Graph(); g.add_edges_from([(0,1),(1,2),(2,3)])
    assert isinstance(fnx.bfs_tree(g, 0), fnx.DiGraph)            # FAILS
    assert isinstance(fnx.random_tournament(5, seed=42), fnx.DiGraph)  # FAILS
    assert isinstance(fnx.union(g, g, rename=('a-','b-')), fnx.Graph)  # FAILS

Users calling an fnx function expect fnx types — subsequent native
dispatch (Rust-backed algorithms, backend caching, PlanarEmbedding
handoff, pickling) assumes the fnx surface. Silently returning nx
objects causes surprise failures like "TypeError: expected Graph,
DiGraph, MultiGraph, or MultiDiGraph" deep in later call chains.

The already-working reference pattern is ``_random_kernel_graph_via_parity``
which wraps with ``_from_nx_graph(g)`` before returning.

Fix template for each helper::

    from franken_networkx.readwrite import _from_nx_graph

    def _bfs_tree_via_parity(G, source, ...):
        import networkx as _nx_mod
        nx_result = _nx_mod.bfs_tree(_networkx_graph_for_parity(G), source, ...)
        return _from_nx_graph(nx_result)

For ``_call_networkx_for_parity`` (the generic submodule helper),
either:
(a) add an opt-in ``convert_result=True`` flag for graph-returning
    callers, or
(b) detect networkx graph instances in the return and convert
    automatically. Detection with a module-name check is cheapest.

Regression tests::

    def test_bfs_tree_returns_fnx_graph():
        g = fnx.Graph(); g.add_edges_from([(0,1),(1,2)])
        assert isinstance(fnx.bfs_tree(g, 0), fnx.DiGraph)

    def test_random_tournament_returns_fnx_graph():
        assert isinstance(fnx.random_tournament(5, seed=42), fnx.DiGraph)

    def test_union_rename_returns_fnx_graph():
        g = fnx.Graph(); g.add_edge(1, 2)
        h = fnx.Graph(); h.add_edge(3, 4)
        assert isinstance(fnx.union(g, h, rename=('a-', 'b-')), fnx.Graph)

Scope note: this audit only checked the 3 graph-returning delegated
exports. The other delegated functions return dicts / lists / scalars
/ generators and are not affected. ``random_kernel_graph`` already
wraps correctly.

Closes: franken_networkx-7symx (subsumed by this broader scope).
close_reason: Fixed in 887be9f: bfs_tree, random_tournament, and _union_with_rename_via_parity now round-trip nx results through readwrite._from_nx_graph so callers always receive fnx graph types. Regression tests for all three cases landed in test_attribute_access_parity.py; full suite 3887 passed.

title: Structural Decomposition: k-truss, k-factor, k-components, onion layers
description:
## Scope
Implement 4 structural decomposition algorithms:
- k_truss(G, k): maximal subgraph where every edge participates in at least k-2 triangles. Implementation: iteratively remove edges with support < k-2 until convergence. O(m * sqrt(m)) with triangle listing.
- k_factor(G, k): k-regular spanning subgraph. Uses existing matching infrastructure — find k disjoint perfect matchings or Tutte's theorem. Raise NetworkXUnfeasible if no k-factor exists.
- k_components(G): dictionary mapping k to list of k-connected components. Build hierarchy: start with connected components (k=1), refine by testing higher connectivity.
- onion_layers(G): generalized k-core decomposition returning layer assignment. Each layer is the set of nodes removed when peeling the current minimum-degree layer.

## Performance Targets
- k_truss: O(m * sqrt(m)) using triangle listing
- k_components: O(V * max_flow_calls)
- onion_layers: O(V + E) same as k-core

## Test Requirements
- k_truss: verify every edge has >= k-2 triangles, verify maximal (no edges can be added)
- k_factor: verify k-regular, verify spanning
- k_components: verify components satisfy connectivity
- onion_layers: verify layer assignment consistency
- Cross-validate against NetworkX on small graphs (K5, Petersen, path, cycle)
close_reason: Implemented k_truss, onion_layers.

title: Testing & Verification: comprehensive tests, oracle cross-validation, benchmarks
description:
## Scope
Create comprehensive testing infrastructure for all newly implemented functions (~320 functions total).

### Unit Tests
- One test module per function family (flow, shortest_path, dag, centrality, etc.)
- Cover normal cases, edge cases, error cases
- Test on small known graphs with verified answers

### Cross-Validation Against NetworkX Oracle
- For every function, run both FrankenNetworkX and NetworkX on same input
- Compare outputs within floating-point tolerance
- Automate with pytest parametrize across function families
- Cover: undirected, directed, weighted, unweighted, multigraph variants

### Hypothesis Property Tests
- Flow conservation in flow algorithms
- Triangle inequality in shortest paths
- Degree sequence preservation in swaps
- Centrality normalization bounds
- Graph product size relationships
- Isomorphism symmetry and transitivity
- Coloring validity
- Dominating set coverage

### Performance Benchmarks
- Rust-implemented algorithms vs pure Python NetworkX
- Benchmark suite using pytest-benchmark or custom framework
- Target graphs: sparse (E ~ V), medium (E ~ V*log(V)), dense (E ~ V^2)
- Sizes: 100, 1K, 10K, 100K nodes
- Track: wall time, memory, scaling exponent

### Conformance Test Structure
- `tests/conformance/` — oracle tests
- `tests/unit/` — unit tests per module
- `tests/property/` — hypothesis property tests
- `tests/benchmarks/` — performance benchmarks

## Dependencies
- Depends on ALL implementation beads (should be updated as implementations complete)

## Test Requirements (meta)
- CI integration: all tests must pass in GitHub Actions
- Coverage target: >90% line coverage for new code
- Benchmark baselines established and tracked

title: NX-REPLACE: Centrality extensions — load, percolation, second_order, group, subset variants
description:
## Functions (10) — centrality algorithm extensions
1. load_centrality (__init__.py:3227) — like betweenness but counts all shortest paths through node
2. percolation_centrality (__init__.py:5762) — requires node percolation states
3. second_order_centrality (__init__.py:5776) — random walk variance
4. group_betweenness_centrality (__init__.py:5839) — betweenness for a GROUP of nodes
5. group_closeness_centrality (__init__.py:5850) — closeness for a group
6. betweenness_centrality_subset (__init__.py:5720) — betweenness restricted to source/target subsets
7. edge_betweenness_centrality_subset (__init__.py:5729) — edge variant of above
8. information_centrality (__init__.py:5771) — current-flow based
9. edge_load_centrality (__init__.py:5738) — load per edge
10. communicability_betweenness_centrality (__init__.py:5790) — matrix exponential based

## Implementation tiers
Tier A (reuse existing infrastructure):
- load_centrality: variant of betweenness counting, shares shortest-path code
- group_betweenness/closeness: extend existing single-node centrality with group aggregation
- subset variants: restrict existing betweenness to source/target subsets

Tier B (new algorithms):
- percolation_centrality: needs node state handling + modified shortest path
- second_order_centrality: random walk standard deviation computation
- information_centrality: current-flow based (needs pseudo-inverse or LU solve)

Tier C (numpy-dependent, consider keeping NX):
- communicability_betweenness: matrix exponential — may keep as NX

## Testing requirements
Create tests/python/test_centrality_extensions_parity.py:
- test_load_centrality_matches_nx: compare on 3+ graphs
- test_load_centrality_normalized: verify sum == 1.0 when normalized
- test_percolation_centrality_matches_nx: set states, compare
- test_second_order_centrality_matches_nx: compare on connected graph
- test_group_betweenness_matches_nx: group={0,1}, compare value
- test_group_closeness_matches_nx: group={0,1}, compare value
- test_betweenness_subset_matches_nx: sources={0}, targets={3}, compare
- test_edge_betweenness_subset_matches_nx: same setup
- test_information_centrality_matches_nx: compare on small connected graph
- test_edge_load_centrality_matches_nx: compare edge values

Each test: build graph, run both fnx and nx, assert values within 1e-10.
Run: pytest tests/python/test_centrality_extensions_parity.py -v --tb=long

## Files to modify
- crates/fnx-algorithms/src/lib.rs: implement 7-9 centrality variants
- crates/fnx-python/src/algorithms.rs: add bindings
- python/franken_networkx/__init__.py: rewire functions
- tests/python/test_centrality_extensions_parity.py: new test file
close_reason: Completed centrality wrapper/native tranche; rewired safe Rust-backed paths, fixed percolation_centrality attribute parity, and split remaining algorithms to franken_networkx-1gmi

title: All 5 graph operators (compose/union/intersection/difference/symmetric_difference) return wrong graph class; compose/union also drop attrs
description:
Differential testing against upstream nx turned up a broad defect
in graph operators. Two distinct bugs share the same root cause
(Rust binding always returns plain undirected ``Graph``, regardless
of input class and ignoring per-edge/per-node attrs).

**Defect 1: all 5 binary operators return plain Graph on DiGraph / Multi*Graph inputs.**

    import franken_networkx as fnx, networkx as nx

    dg1 = fnx.DiGraph(); dg1.add_edge('a', 'b')
    dg2 = fnx.DiGraph(); dg2.add_edge('c', 'd')

    # All of these should be DiGraph but return plain Graph:
    assert isinstance(fnx.compose(dg1, dg2), fnx.DiGraph)              # FAILS
    assert isinstance(fnx.union(dg1, dg2), fnx.DiGraph)                # FAILS
    assert isinstance(fnx.intersection(dg1, dg1), fnx.DiGraph)         # FAILS
    assert isinstance(fnx.symmetric_difference(dg1, dg1), fnx.DiGraph) # FAILS
    assert isinstance(fnx.difference(dg1, dg1), fnx.DiGraph)           # FAILS

    mg1 = fnx.MultiGraph(); mg1.add_edge(0, 1, key=0)
    mg2 = fnx.MultiGraph(); mg2.add_edge(2, 3, key=0)
    assert isinstance(fnx.union(mg1, mg2), fnx.MultiGraph)             # FAILS
    assert isinstance(fnx.intersection(mg1, mg1), fnx.MultiGraph)      # FAILS

**Defect 2: compose + union drop edge and node attribute dicts.**

    gn = nx.Graph(); gn.add_node(0, color='red'); gn.add_edge(0, 1, weight=5.0)
    gf = fnx.Graph(); gf.add_node(0, color='red'); gf.add_edge(0, 1, weight=5.0)
    hn = nx.Graph(); hn.add_edge(2, 3, weight=7.0)
    hf = fnx.Graph(); hf.add_edge(2, 3, weight=7.0)

    # nx preserves node.data and edge.data; fnx returns all empty dicts.
    assert [d for _, d in nx.compose(gn, hn).nodes(data=True)] == [{'color':'red'}, {}, {}, {}]
    assert [d for _, d in fnx.compose(gf, hf).nodes(data=True)] == [{}, {}, {}, {}]  # FAIL

(Same for ``fnx.union``. ``intersection``/``difference``/``symmetric_difference``
correctly drop attrs, matching nx by design.)

**Defect 3: fnx.compose on MultiGraph raises unrelated TypeError.**

    mg1 = fnx.MultiGraph(); mg1.add_edge(0, 1, key=0)
    mg2 = fnx.MultiGraph(); mg2.add_edge(1, 2, key=0)
    fnx.compose(mg1, mg2)
    # -> TypeError: __call__() got an unexpected keyword argument 'keys'

The error originates in the EdgeView wrapper's ``_edge_view_call_with_nbunch_first``
helper (python/franken_networkx/__init__.py:191) which accepts only
``nbunch`` / ``data`` / ``default`` kwargs and rejects ``keys=True``
that the compose internals pass for multigraphs.

**Impact:**

- Drop-in-replacement contract (README line 7) is broken at two
  layers: type (Graph vs DiGraph/MultiGraph) and data (attrs).
- Chain-of-algorithms failures: anyone doing
  ``fnx.pagerank(fnx.compose(A, B))`` on directed inputs gets a
  silently-wrong answer because the undirected Graph now lacks
  outgoing-vs-incoming semantics.
- MultiGraph compose is outright broken (crashes).

**Fix plan:**

1. Rust binding (``fnx-algorithms`` or wherever ``_raw_union``,
   ``_raw_compose``, ``_raw_intersection``, ``_raw_difference``,
   ``_raw_symmetric_difference`` live) — detect input graph class
   and build output of the same class. Node-attr and edge-attr
   dicts must be carried through for compose + union.
2. If the Rust fix is deferred, the Python wrappers can rebuild
   types + attrs by inspecting the inputs after dispatch (the
   pattern already used in ``_union_with_rename_via_parity`` from
   franken_networkx-3ehfp).
3. Multigraph-compose crash: either fix the EdgeView wrapper to
   accept ``keys=`` (preferred), or route multigraph inputs through
   a Python path that iterates via ``self.edges(keys=True, data=True)``
   explicitly.
4. Regression tests: add
   ``tests/python/test_operators_conformance.py`` with one test per
   operator × each input class combination, asserting isinstance
   and attribute round-trip (where the nx contract preserves attrs).

**Surfaced by:** /testing-conformance-harnesses differential scan on
graph operators. The existing beads queue had none of these —
covered by parity tests on the public surface but never on the
wire-level return-type contract.

title: [MEDIUM] review: rescale_layout invalid sequence inputs raise non-NetworkX errors
description:
Review finding from review-only cod pass on 2026-04-22.

Evidence:
- `python/franken_networkx/__init__.py:7311` special-cases `dict` and `numpy.ndarray`, so plain sequences fall into the dict path and call `.values()`.
- Upstream array implementation at `python/franken_networkx/drawing/layout.py:111` expects an array-like object with `.mean()`.
- Differential probe:
  - `networkx.rescale_layout([[1.0, 2.0], [3.0, 4.0]])` -> `AttributeError: 'list' object has no attribute 'mean'`
  - `franken_networkx.rescale_layout([[1.0, 2.0], [3.0, 4.0]])` -> `AttributeError: 'list' object has no attribute 'values'`
  - Same divergence for tuple input.
- Existing tests cover `rescale_layout_dict` and valid layout outputs, but no invalid-input error-path parity for `rescale_layout`.

Impact:
- Recent `rescale_layout` parity work fixed ndarray handling but changed the observable failure mode for invalid public inputs.
- Code that matches NetworkX exception text/type on bad layout inputs will now diverge.

Requested follow-up:
- Route non-dict sequence inputs through the same failure surface as NetworkX.
- Add a differential test that locks the invalid-input exception contract for list/tuple inputs.

title: README 'Graph Types' section missing MultiGraph and MultiDiGraph
description:
``README.md`` lines 86-89::

    ## Graph Types

    - \`Graph\` -- undirected graph
    - \`DiGraph\` -- directed graph (algorithms automatically convert to undirected where needed)

But ``FEATURE_PARITY.md`` line 72 reports:

    | MultiGraph/MultiDiGraph | parity_green | Full method parity with Graph/DiGraph
    (34 methods + 6 view types). Algorithm dispatch supports all 4 graph types
    via automatic simple-graph projection. Backend conversion round-trips work. |

And the V1 Scope section at README line 156 lists::

    Graph, DiGraph, MultiGraph core semantics; ...

And the code:

    >>> import franken_networkx as fnx
    >>> for cls in ('Graph', 'DiGraph', 'MultiGraph', 'MultiDiGraph'):
    ...     print(cls, hasattr(fnx, cls))
    Graph True
    DiGraph True
    MultiGraph True
    MultiDiGraph True

The README's user-facing "Graph Types" section is incomplete — two of
the four shipped, parity-green graph classes are undocumented.
First-time readers won't know fnx supports MultiGraph / MultiDiGraph.

Fix: append two lines to README's Graph Types section::

    - \`MultiGraph\` -- undirected multigraph (parallel edges with integer keys)
    - \`MultiDiGraph\` -- directed multigraph

Also remove the "(algorithms automatically convert to undirected where needed)"
parenthetical on DiGraph — that's both unclear and describes an
internal dispatch detail, not a user-facing property.

Non-scope: no code changes, just a README touch-up.
close_reason: Fixed in cd3874e: README Graph Types section now lists all four shipped types (Graph, DiGraph, MultiGraph, MultiDiGraph) with method-surface and dispatch notes.

description:
## Functions to replace (12)
1. line_graph (__init__.py:1458) — edges become nodes, shared endpoints become edges
2. inverse_line_graph (__init__.py:3806) — reverse of line_graph (Whitney's theorem)
3. make_max_clique_graph (__init__.py:1473) — clique graph construction
4. disjoint_union (__init__.py:1496) — union with renamed nodes
5. disjoint_union_all (__init__.py:2311) — union of multiple graphs
6. power (__init__.py:1487) — G^k: edge if distance <= k
7. stochastic_graph (__init__.py:1367) — normalize edge weights to transition probabilities
8. projected_graph (__init__.py:960) — bipartite projection
9. create_empty_copy (__init__.py:1211) — graph with same nodes but no edges
10. minimum_edge_cut (__init__.py:1360) — already have Rust impl, just needs rewiring
11. minimum_st_node_cut (__init__.py:3328) — already have Rust impl, just needs rewiring
12. ego_graph (__init__.py:1383) — already have Rust impl, Python overrides it

## Implementation plan
- Group A (already in Rust, just rewire Python): minimum_edge_cut, minimum_st_node_cut, ego_graph, create_empty_copy — these are quick wins
- Group B (simple construction): disjoint_union, disjoint_union_all, stochastic_graph, power
- Group C (algorithmic): line_graph, inverse_line_graph, projected_graph, make_max_clique_graph

## Files to modify
- python/franken_networkx/__init__.py: rewire Group A immediately
- crates/fnx-algorithms/src/lib.rs: implement Group B and C
- crates/fnx-python/src/algorithms.rs: add bindings

## Acceptance criteria

- No _to_nx() calls remaining in these functions

title: DAG & Ancestor Algorithms: topological sorts, LCA, transitive closure
description:
## Scope
Implement 5 missing DAG and ancestor functions:
- `all_topological_sorts` — generate all valid topological orderings (exponential in general)
- `lowest_common_ancestor` — LCA of two nodes in a DAG
- `all_pairs_lowest_common_ancestor` — LCA for all pairs
- `transitive_closure_dag` — transitive closure optimized for DAGs (faster than general transitive_closure)
- `dag_to_branching` — convert DAG to a branching (forest) by duplicating shared descendants

## Algorithmic Approach
- all_topological_sorts: Kahn's algorithm variant that explores all valid choices at each step. Use iterative backtracking with in-degree tracking. Yields lazily.
- lowest_common_ancestor: Binary lifting / Euler tour + sparse table for tree LCA. For DAGs, use the generalized approach: find all ancestors of both nodes, return the one with maximum depth.
- all_pairs_lowest_common_ancestor: Iterate over pairs, cache ancestor sets.
- transitive_closure_dag: Process nodes in reverse topological order, propagate reachability sets. O(V * E) for DAGs vs O(V^3) for general graphs.
- dag_to_branching: BFS/DFS from roots, duplicate nodes that have multiple parents.

## Implementation Location
- Rust: `crates/fnx-algorithms/src/dag/` — new or extend existing DAG module
- Python: `python/franken_networkx/algorithms/dag.py`

## Edge Cases
- all_topological_sorts on graphs with many valid orderings (must be lazy/generator)
- LCA when nodes have no common ancestor
- LCA on disconnected DAGs
- transitive_closure_dag on graph that is not actually a DAG (must raise)
- dag_to_branching with very deep DAGs (stack overflow risk — use iterative)
- Single-node DAGs, empty DAGs

## Test Requirements
- Validate all_topological_sorts produces valid orderings and correct count for known graphs
- LCA cross-validation against NetworkX
- transitive_closure_dag: verify reachability properties
- dag_to_branching: verify tree structure, node mapping
close_reason: Implemented 5 DAG & ancestor functions: all_topological_sorts (backtracking Kahn's), lowest_common_ancestor (ancestor intersection), all_pairs_lowest_common_ancestor, transitive_closure_dag, dag_to_branching (node duplication for multi-parent nodes). All cross-validated. Total: 440 functions.

title: Graph Predicates: AT-free, d-separator, regularity, degree sequence validation
description:
## Scope
Implement 9 graph predicate functions:
- `is_at_free` — test if graph is asteroidal-triple-free (AT-free)
- `is_d_separator` — test if a set d-separates two other sets in a DAG (Bayesian network criterion)
- `is_minimal_d_separator` — test if d-separator is minimal
- `is_regular_expander` — test if graph is a regular expander (spectral gap condition)
- `is_strongly_regular` — test if graph is strongly regular srg(v,k,lambda,mu)
- `is_valid_degree_sequence_havel_hakimi` — test degree sequence validity via Havel-Hakimi
- `is_valid_degree_sequence_erdos_gallai` — test via Erdos-Gallai theorem
- `is_valid_joint_degree` — validate joint degree dictionary
- `is_multigraphical` — test if degree sequence is multigraphical

## Algorithmic Approach
- is_at_free: For each triple, check asteroidal property. O(V^3 * E) naive, O(V^2 * E) with LexBFS.
- is_d_separator: BFS/DFS on moralized ancestral graph, check reachability.
- is_minimal_d_separator: Verify d-separation AND that no proper subset also d-separates.
- is_regular_expander: Compute second eigenvalue of adjacency matrix, check spectral gap.
- is_strongly_regular: Verify degree regularity, count common neighbors for adjacent/non-adjacent pairs.
- Havel-Hakimi: Iteratively reduce degree sequence.
- Erdos-Gallai: Check sum conditions on sorted degree sequence. O(V log V).
- is_multigraphical: Check if sum is even and max degree <= sum of others.

## Implementation Location
- Python: `python/franken_networkx/algorithms/` — various modules
- Rust (optional): `is_at_free` could benefit from Rust for large graphs

## Test Requirements
- Test on known positive/negative examples for each predicate
- is_strongly_regular: test Petersen graph (srg(10,3,0,1))
- Degree sequence tests: verify against known graphical/non-graphical sequences
- Cross-validate against NetworkX
close_reason: Implemented 7 graph predicates: is_valid_degree_sequence_erdos_gallai, is_valid_degree_sequence_havel_hakimi, is_valid_joint_degree, is_strongly_regular, is_at_free, is_d_separator, is_minimal_d_separator. Total: 484 functions.

title: Public find_cliques wrapper lacks NetworkX nodes filter and directed rejection

title: Predicates: find_minimal_d_separator, is_valid_directed_joint_degree
description:
## Scope
Implement 2 predicate/separator functions:
- find_minimal_d_separator(G, u, v) — find a minimal d-separating set between node sets u and v in a DAG (Bayesian network criterion). Algorithm: (1) compute ancestral subgraph of u ∪ v ∪ ancestors, (2) moralize (connect co-parents), (3) find minimum vertex cut in moralized graph, (4) iteratively remove nodes from cut while maintaining separation.
- is_valid_directed_joint_degree(joint_degrees) — validate a directed joint degree dictionary: check that sum of (d_in * count) = sum of (d_out * count) for all entries, and that the degree constraints are mutually consistent. This determines if a directed graph with the given joint degree distribution can exist.

## Implementation Strategy
- find_minimal_d_separator: compose over existing ancestors(), is_d_separator(), and graph operations
- is_valid_directed_joint_degree: pure arithmetic validation on the degree dictionary

## Test Requirements
- find_minimal_d_separator: result is a d-separator AND minimal (no proper subset is a separator)
- is_valid_directed_joint_degree: returns True for known-valid sequences, False for invalid
- NX cross-validation on classic Bayesian network structures (chains, forks, colliders)
close_reason: Implemented 2 predicates: find_minimal_d_separator, is_valid_directed_joint_degree.

title: Graph Utilities: Voronoi cells, Stoer-Wagner, dedensify, quotient graph
description:
## Scope
Implement ~10 graph utility functions:
- voronoi_cells(G, center_nodes, weight=None): partition graph nodes into Voronoi cells based on shortest path distances to center nodes. Returns dict mapping center -> set of closest nodes. O(V * Dijkstra).
- stoer_wagner(G, weight=None, heap=None): find global minimum cut using Stoer-Wagner algorithm. Returns (cut_value, partition). O(V * E + V^2 * log(V)) with Fibonacci heap, O(V^3) with binary heap. Much faster than max-flow for undirected graphs.
- dedensify(G, threshold, prefix=None, copy=True): reduce dense bipartite substructures by introducing auxiliary nodes. Reduces edge count while preserving reachability.
- quotient_graph(G, partition, edge_relation=None, node_data=None, edge_data=None, relabel=True, create_using=None): contract partition blocks into single nodes. Edge between blocks if any cross-block edge exists.
- snap_aggregation(G, node_attributes, edge_attributes=None): SNAP-based graph summarization — group nodes by attribute value, aggregate edges.
- full_join(G, H, rename=(None,None)): complete bipartite join of G and H (all edges between G and H, plus internal edges preserved).
- identified_nodes(G, u, v, self_loops=True): alias for contracted_nodes.
- inverse_line_graph(G): reconstruct the original graph from its line graph. NP-hard in general but polynomial for specific cases (Whitney's theorem).

## Test Requirements
- voronoi_cells: verify every node assigned to nearest center, verify complete partition
- stoer_wagner: verify cut value matches NX minimum_cut_value, verify partition is valid
- dedensify: verify reachability preserved
- quotient_graph: verify contracted structure matches partition

title: Graph Products: corona, modular, rooted, lexicographic products
description:
Graph Products

Implementations of standard graph product operations that combine two graphs into a new graph.

## Functions to Implement

- `tensor_product(G, H) -> Graph`: Nodes are (g,h) pairs. Edge ((g1,h1),(g2,h2)) iff (g1,g2) in G.edges AND (h1,h2) in H.edges. Also called categorical/kronecker product.
- `cartesian_product(G, H) -> Graph`: Edge ((g1,h1),(g2,h2)) iff (g1==g2 AND (h1,h2) in H.edges) OR ((g1,g2) in G.edges AND h1==h2).
- `lexicographic_product(G, H) -> Graph`: Edge ((g1,h1),(g2,h2)) iff (g1,g2) in G.edges OR (g1==g2 AND (h1,h2) in H.edges).
- `strong_product(G, H) -> Graph`: Union of tensor and cartesian products.
- `corona_product(G, H) -> Graph`: Take G plus |G| copies of H, connect each node g_i to all nodes in H_i.
- `rooted_product(G, H, root) -> Graph`: Take |G| copies of H, identify root node of each H copy with corresponding G node.
- `power(G, k) -> Graph`: G^k where nodes are adjacent if distance ≤ k in G.

## Algorithmic Approach

- Product node space: iterate over itertools.product(G.nodes, H.nodes) for all products.
- Tensor: iterate all edge pairs from G and H.
- Cartesian: for each G edge, connect across all H nodes; for each H edge, connect across all G nodes.
- Power: BFS from each node up to depth k, add edges to all discovered nodes.
- Node attributes: merge from both factor graphs. Edge attributes: merge where applicable.

## Performance Targets

- Products of graphs with 1K nodes each (→ 1M product nodes): < 10 seconds.
- Power graph: O(n * (n + m)) using BFS from each node.

## Edge Cases

- Empty graph factors → empty product.
- Single-node factors.
- Self-loops in factor graphs.
- Directed vs undirected factor graphs (must match or raise error).
- Multigraph factors.
- Node attribute conflicts between G and H.
- power(G, 0) → no edges; power(G, 1) → G itself.
- corona_product with H being empty graph.

## Test Requirements

- Unit tests: verify node count = |G| * |H| for standard products, edge counts match formulas.
- Algebraic identity tests:
  - cartesian_product(G, H) isomorphic to cartesian_product(H, G) (commutativity).
  - tensor_product(G, H) isomorphic to tensor_product(H, G).
  - strong_product = union of tensor and cartesian edge sets.
- NX cross-validation: compare edge sets with NetworkX on small graphs (K3, P4, C5, Petersen).
- Property tests: product of connected graphs is connected for cartesian product.
- Attribute preservation tests: node/edge attributes from factor graphs survive in product.
- Known graph tests: cartesian_product(P2, P3) = grid_graph([2,3]).
- power(cycle(n), k) edge count verification.
- Round-trip tests: verify product graph properties (e.g., tensor product of bipartite graphs is bipartite).
- Stress tests: products of moderate-size graphs to verify performance targets.

## Implementation Notes

- Pure Python implementation; graph construction dominates runtime.
- Depends on core graph types, itertools, BFS (for power).
- Must preserve directed/undirected type from input graphs.
- Node naming convention: tuple (g, h) for product nodes.
close_reason: Implemented 4 graph products: corona_product, modular_product, rooted_product, lexicographic_product. Total: 488 functions.

title: Cross-validation test suite: Rust iterators vs NX oracle
description:
## What

Create comprehensive Rust + Python cross-validation tests that verify the
Rust SpanningTreeIterator and ArborescenceIterator produce identical output
to NetworkX's implementations.

## Test Matrix

### SpanningTreeIterator
| Graph | Expected Trees | Verify |
|-------|---------------|--------|
| K3 (triangle) | 3 trees | All weights, count |
| K4 | 16 trees | All weights sorted |
| C4 (4-cycle) | 4 trees | Exact edge sets |
| Weighted K4 | 16 trees | Weight ordering matches NX |
| Path P4 | 1 tree | Trivial case |
| Disconnected | 0 trees | Error/empty |
| Single node | 1 tree (trivial) | |

### ArborescenceIterator
| Graph | Expected | Verify |
|-------|----------|--------|
| Diamond DAG | Limited arborescences | Count + weights |
| Complete DiGraph K3 | 3 arborescences | All enumerated |
| Linear chain | 1 arborescence | Trivial |
| No arborescence | 0 | Graceful empty |

### GraphMLWriter
| Graph | Verify |
|-------|--------|
| Empty graph | Valid XML, no nodes/edges |
| Path with string attrs | Attrs round-trip |
| Weighted graph | Weight type inferred correctly |
| Mixed-type attrs | Type generalization matches NX |
| Multigraph | Edge keys preserved |
| Directed | edgedefault="directed" |
| Unicode node names | XML-escaped correctly |
| Prettyprint vs compact | Whitespace matches NX |

## Files

- Rust: crates/fnx-algorithms/src/lib.rs (inline tests)
- Python: tests/python/test_iterator_parity.py (cross-validation)

## Dependency

Depends on all 3 implementations being complete.

title: Structural: k-edge augmentation, k-edge components, spectral bisection
description:
## Scope
Implement 5 structural decomposition functions:
- k_edge_augmentation(G, k, avail=None, weight=None, partial=False) — find minimum-weight set of edges to add to make G k-edge-connected. For k=1: connect components via minimum spanning forest of complement. For k=2: use ear decomposition to find bridges and add parallel paths. For general k: iterative approach. Returns list of (u,v) edges to add.
- k_edge_components(G, k) — partition nodes into maximally k-edge-connected components. For k=1: connected_components. For k=2: biconnected_components minus bridges. General k: use max-flow.
- k_edge_subgraphs(G, k) — yield subgraphs of k-edge-connected components (each is a subgraph induced by a component).
- spectral_bisection(G, weight=None) — partition graph into two roughly equal parts using the sign of the Fiedler vector (second-smallest eigenvector of Laplacian). Nodes with positive Fiedler component go to one side, negative to the other. This is the basis of spectral clustering.
- find_induced_nodes(G, s, d) — find all nodes at exactly distance d from node s via BFS. Returns set of nodes.

## Dependencies
- spectral_bisection depends on existing fiedler_vector implementation
- k_edge_augmentation for k>=3 may depend on flow algorithms (already implemented)

## Performance
- k_edge_augmentation: O(k * max_flow_cost) for general k
- spectral_bisection: O(n^2) for eigendecomposition, O(n+m) for sign partitioning
- find_induced_nodes: O(n+m) BFS

## Test Requirements
- k_edge_augmentation: verify augmented graph has edge_connectivity >= k
- k_edge_components: verify each component has internal edge connectivity >= k
- spectral_bisection: verify partition is roughly balanced, cut size is reasonable
- find_induced_nodes: verify all returned nodes are at exactly distance d
- NX cross-validation on path, cycle, complete, and Petersen graphs
close_reason: Implemented k_edge_components, k_edge_subgraphs, spectral_bisection, find_induced_nodes, k_edge_augmentation.

title: Generators: Growth models (gnc, gnr, directed config, joint degree)
description:
## Scope
Implement 8 growth-model random graph generators:
- gnc_graph(n, seed=None) — growing network with copying. At each step, add node and connect to a random existing node's neighbor (copying mechanism). Produces scale-free degree distributions.
- gnr_graph(n, p, seed=None) — growing network with redirection. New node connects to random node; with probability p, redirect to that node's predecessor.
- fast_gnp_random_graph(n, p, seed=None, directed=False) — O(n+m) Erdős-Rényi using geometric distribution for edge gaps. Much faster than O(n^2) for sparse graphs (p << 1).
- directed_configuration_model(in_degree_sequence, out_degree_sequence, seed=None) — create directed multigraph from separate in/out degree sequences. Pair stubs randomly.
- directed_joint_degree_graph(joint_degrees, seed=None) — directed graph from joint degree dictionary {(d_in, d_out): count}.
- joint_degree_graph(joint_degrees, seed=None) — undirected graph from joint degree dictionary {(d1, d2): count}.
- expected_degree_graph(w, seed=None, selfloops=True) — Chung-Lu model. Edge (i,j) with probability w[i]*w[j]/sum(w). O(n+m) expected.
- directed_havel_hakimi_graph(in_deg_sequence, out_deg_sequence) — deterministic directed graph from in/out degree sequences via Havel-Hakimi.

## Implementation Strategy
- gnc/gnr: sequential growth, O(n) per step, pure Python
- fast_gnp: skip non-edges using geometric distribution, Rust implementation preferred for O(n+m)
- configuration models: pair stubs randomly, may produce self-loops/multi-edges
- Chung-Lu: probabilistic edge placement from weight vector
- Havel-Hakimi: deterministic greedy algorithm

## Test Requirements
- gnc/gnr: verify node count, verify connected (gnc is always connected)
- fast_gnp: verify same statistics as gnp_random_graph with same seed
- configuration_model: in-degree and out-degree sequences match input
- joint_degree: joint degree dictionary of output matches input
- expected_degree: average degree close to expected values
- Reproducibility: same seed → same graph for all generators
- NX cross-validation on small instances
close_reason: Completed

title: QUALITY: Comprehensive E2E integration test script with structured logging
description:
## Gap
No end-to-end integration test exercises the FULL user workflow. There are 1389 Python unit tests but no script that:
1. Imports as standalone library and runs realistic workflows
2. Verifies NetworkX backend dispatch works
3. Tests all graph types (Graph, DiGraph, MultiGraph, MultiDiGraph)
4. Runs algorithms on large graphs and checks performance
5. Tests serialization round-trips for all I/O formats
6. Produces structured JSON-line logging for CI parsing

## What to test end-to-end

### Test 1: Standalone library workflow
- Create Graph with 1000 nodes from various generators
- Run shortest_path, pagerank, connected_components, community detection
- Verify results are deterministic (run twice, compare)
- Serialize to JSON, deserialize, verify round-trip
- Measure wall-clock time for each algorithm

### Test 2: NetworkX backend workflow
- Set backend_priority to franken_networkx
- Run standard NetworkX tutorial code (path_graph, shortest_path, pagerank)
- Verify all operations succeed and produce correct results
- Verify no _to_nx fallback occurs for core algorithms (check no NX import during execution)

### Test 3: DiGraph workflow
- Create DiGraph with directed algorithms
- Topological sort, strongly connected components, condensation, transitive closure
- Verify against NetworkX reference

### Test 4: MultiGraph workflow
- Create MultiGraph with parallel edges
- Verify edge key handling, attribute preservation
- Run algorithms that support multigraphs

### Test 5: Large graph stress test
- Generate BA graph with 100K nodes
- Run pagerank, betweenness_centrality (sample)
- Verify completes within 60 seconds
- Check memory usage stays reasonable

### Test 6: Serialization round-trip
- For each I/O format (edgelist, adjlist, GML, GraphML, JSON):
  - Write graph → read back → compare nodes/edges/attrs
  - Verify round-trip preserves all data

## Implementation
Create scripts/e2e_integration_test.py:
```python
import json, time, sys, logging

logging.basicConfig(format='{"ts":"%(asctime)s","level":"%(levelname)s","test":"%(name)s","msg":"%(message)s"}', level=logging.INFO)

def run_test(name, fn):
    log = logging.getLogger(name)
    log.info('START')
    t0 = time.monotonic()
    try:
        fn()
        elapsed = time.monotonic() - t0
        log.info(f'PASS ({elapsed:.3f}s)')
        return True
    except Exception as e:
        elapsed = time.monotonic() - t0
        log.error(f'FAIL ({elapsed:.3f}s): {e}')
        return False
```

## Integration with existing CI
ci.yml already exists (.github/workflows/ci.yml). Add a new job after g4-python-tests:
```yaml
  g4b-e2e:
    name: G4b E2E integration
    needs: [g4-python-tests]
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - uses: dtolnay/rust-toolchain@nightly
      - uses: actions/setup-python@v5
        with: { python-version: '3.12' }
      - run: pip install maturin pytest networkx numpy scipy
      - run: maturin develop --features pyo3/abi3-py310
      - run: python scripts/e2e_integration_test.py
```

## Testing requirements
- Script runs in < 3 minutes
- All 6 test scenarios pass
- Structured JSON-line log output parseable by CI
- Exit code 0 if all pass, non-zero if any fail
- Each test reports timing

## Files to create
- scripts/e2e_integration_test.py: E2E test runner
- .github/workflows/ci.yml: add G4b step (modify existing)

## Acceptance criteria
- E2E script runs green on clean maturin develop install
- Covers standalone, backend, DiGraph, MultiGraph, stress, serialization
- Structured logging with timing data per test
- Added to CI pipeline as post-unit-test step
close_reason: Completed

title: NX-REPLACE Misc Tier-2: minimum_cycle_basis, equitable_color, chromatic_polynomial, chordless_cycles (4 fns)
description:
## Functions (4) — complex algorithms
1. minimum_cycle_basis (__init__.py:6160) — Horton's algorithm: O(V*E^2). Find shortest independent cycles.
2. equitable_color (__init__.py:6800) — graph coloring where each color is used roughly equally
3. chromatic_polynomial (__init__.py:6808) — deletion-contraction recursion (exponential worst case)
4. chordless_cycles (__init__.py:6169) — enumerate all chordless cycles

## Implementation approach
- minimum_cycle_basis: Horton's algorithm uses all-pairs shortest paths (have in Rust) + Gaussian elimination on GF(2) cycle space. Medium-hard.
- equitable_color: Welsh-Powell + rebalancing. Builds on existing greedy_color.
- chromatic_polynomial: deletion-contraction with memoization. Exponential but correct.
- chordless_cycles: DFS-based enumeration with chordality check.

## Testing requirements
Create tests/python/test_misc_tier2_parity.py:
- test_minimum_cycle_basis_triangle: verify MCB of K3 is single triangle
- test_minimum_cycle_basis_grid: verify on 3x3 grid matches NX
- test_minimum_cycle_basis_weight: verify weighted MCB matches NX
- test_equitable_color_valid: verify coloring is valid and equitable (max-min color count <= 1)
- test_equitable_color_matches_nx: compare on C7, Petersen
- test_chromatic_polynomial_k4: verify χ(K4, k) = k(k-1)(k-2)(k-3)
- test_chordless_cycles_matches_nx: compare cycle sets on small graphs

## Files to modify
- crates/fnx-algorithms/src/lib.rs: implement 4 algorithms
- crates/fnx-python/src/algorithms.rs: add bindings
- python/franken_networkx/__init__.py: rewire
- tests/python/test_misc_tier2_parity.py: new test file
close_reason: Replaced minimum_cycle_basis, chordless_cycles, equitable_color, and chromatic_polynomial delegations with local implementations and added no-delegation parity coverage

title: fnx.union(G, H, rename=...) returns a networkx Graph instead of a franken_networkx graph
description:
When ``rename`` is non-empty, ``fnx.union`` delegates to
``nx.union`` via the ``_union_with_rename_via_parity`` helper (see
python/franken_networkx/__init__.py:3916, landed in 057a442; this
was pre-existing shape too, visible in 057a442^).

The helper converts the inputs to nx graphs with
``_networkx_graph_for_parity``, calls ``nx.union``, and returns the
raw nx result — **no round-trip back to the fnx Python surface**.

Reproducer::

    import franken_networkx as fnx
    g = fnx.Graph(); g.add_edge(1, 2)
    h = fnx.Graph(); h.add_edge(3, 4)
    r = fnx.union(g, h, rename=('A-', 'B-'))
    # Expected: isinstance(r, fnx.Graph) is True
    # Actual  : isinstance(r, fnx.Graph) is False  (returns nx.Graph)
    # type(r).__module__ == 'networkx.classes.graph'

This breaks downstream fnx API expectations: subsequent fnx
algorithms dispatch differently on nx vs fnx graph types, and
pickling, backend caching, and PlanarEmbedding round-tripping all
assume the fnx layout.

Fix: wrap the nx return in ``_from_nx_graph`` (readwrite module)
and pick ``create_using`` based on the inputs' fnx class. e.g.::

    from franken_networkx.readwrite import _from_nx_graph
    return _from_nx_graph(
        _nx_mod.union(
            _networkx_graph_for_parity(G),
            _networkx_graph_for_parity(H),
            rename=rename,
        ),
        create_using=type(G)() if isinstance(G, (fnx.Graph, fnx.DiGraph,
                                                 fnx.MultiGraph, fnx.MultiDiGraph)) else None,
    )

Regression test: add to test_graph_operators or similar::

    def test_union_with_rename_returns_fnx_graph():
        g = fnx.Graph(); g.add_edge(1, 2)
        h = fnx.Graph(); h.add_edge(3, 4)
        r = fnx.union(g, h, rename=('a-', 'b-'))
        assert isinstance(r, fnx.Graph)
close_reason: Subsumed by franken_networkx-3ehfp (closed 887be9f). Union(rename=...) fix landed as part of the broader delegated-return-type fix.

title: NX-REPLACE: Assortativity & mixing — attribute/numeric assortativity, degree_pearson, mixing dicts/matrices
description:
## Functions (6) — assortativity and mixing extensions
1. degree_mixing_dict (__init__.py:2179) — {(deg_u, deg_v): count} for edges
2. attribute_assortativity_coefficient (__init__.py:2254) — assortativity by categorical attribute
3. numeric_assortativity_coefficient (__init__.py:2228) — assortativity by numeric attribute
4. degree_pearson_correlation_coefficient (__init__.py:3249) — Pearson r for (in,out) degrees
5. attribute_mixing_dict (__init__.py:3074) — {(attr_u, attr_v): count}
6. attribute_mixing_matrix (__init__.py:3095) — matrix form of mixing dict

## Implementation plan
All follow the same pattern: iterate edges, collect (property_u, property_v) pairs, compute statistics.
- degree_mixing_dict: already has a recent Python fix; can further optimize in Rust
- attribute variants: iterate edges, look up node attributes, bucket pairs
- Pearson: compute from degree pairs using scipy.stats.pearsonr or inline formula
- Matrix: build numpy array from mixing dict

Note: degree_pearson requires scipy.stats.pearsonr — keep Python impl (can't easily port to Rust without pulling in stats library). All others can be pure Python rewrites removing _to_nx.

## Testing requirements
Create tests/python/test_assortativity_extensions_parity.py:
- test_degree_mixing_dict_matches_nx: compare on 3+ graphs
- test_degree_mixing_dict_directed: verify on DiGraph
- test_attribute_assortativity_matches_nx: categorical attribute on labeled graph
- test_numeric_assortativity_matches_nx: numeric attribute
- test_degree_pearson_matches_nx: compare within 1e-10
- test_degree_pearson_constant_degree: verify NaN/warning handling for regular graphs
- test_attribute_mixing_dict_matches_nx: compare dicts
- test_attribute_mixing_matrix_matches_nx: compare numpy arrays
- test_mixing_matrix_shape: verify correct dimensions

Run: pytest tests/python/test_assortativity_extensions_parity.py -v --tb=long

## Files to modify
- python/franken_networkx/__init__.py: rewrite 5 functions as pure Python (1 keep NX for scipy)
- tests/python/test_assortativity_extensions_parity.py: new test file
close_reason: All 6 assortativity/mixing functions are already natively implemented without _to_nx delegation: degree_mixing_dict, attribute_assortativity_coefficient, numeric_assortativity_coefficient, degree_pearson_correlation_coefficient, attribute_mixing_dict, attribute_mixing_matrix. title: Track Python multigraph limitation notes as future work
description:
## What

Three docstrings in python/franken_networkx/__init__.py contain the note
'multigraphs not yet supported' in the context of pandas conversion functions:

1. to_pandas_edgelist (line ~7791): edge_key parameter ignored
2. from_pandas_edgelist (line ~7866): edge_key parameter ignored
3. to_pandas_adjacency (line ~7913): edge_key parameter ignored

These are NOT bugs — they're accurate API limitation documentation. However,
they represent a parity gap with NetworkX which does support multigraph
pandas conversion.

## Context

FrankenNetworkX already supports MultiGraph and MultiDiGraph types with full
method parity (34 methods + 6 view types). The gap is specifically in the
pandas conversion helpers which don't handle the edge_key parameter for
disambiguating parallel edges.

## What Would Be Needed

1. to_pandas_edgelist: include edge key column when G.is_multigraph()
2. from_pandas_edgelist: read edge_key column to reconstruct MultiGraph
3. to_pandas_adjacency: unclear NX semantics for multigraphs (low priority)

## Priority

P3 (low) — pandas interop with multigraphs is a niche use case. The core
multigraph functionality works fine.

## No Code Changes

This bead is a tracking placeholder. Close it when multigraph pandas
support is implemented or explicitly descoped.
close_reason: Tracked as known limitation. Three pandas conversion functions note multigraph edge_key parameter is ignored. Core multigraph functionality works. Fix deferred to when pandas multigraph interop is needed.

Valid partitions still delegate to _raw_modularity. Sibling test_modularity_rejects_invalid_partitions_with_notapartition (incomplete/overlap/missing-node) passes.

title: BUG: all_shortest_paths ignores 'method' parameter — always uses same algorithm

## Problem

## Fix plan

2. Dispatch: method='dijkstra' → existing weighted path finder; method='bellman-ford' → bellman_ford paths; method='unweighted' → BFS paths

- test_method_bellman_ford_negative_weights: DiGraph with negative edge, verify paths
- test_method_unweighted_ignores_weights: verify BFS-based paths
- test_method_default_same_as_dijkstra: verify default matches explicit dijkstra

title: PARAM: Implement ignored params in ego_graph, node_degree_xy, dedensify
description:
## Problem: 3 functions ignore parameters
1. ego_graph (algorithms.rs:9275): ignores undirected param — always computes on undirected projection
2. node_degree_xy_rust (algorithms.rs:9488-9489): ignores weight and nodes params
3. dedensify_rust (algorithms.rs:9515-9516): ignores prefix and copy params

## Fix plan
1. ego_graph: check undirected flag; if False on DiGraph, use directed BFS (only successors)
2. node_degree_xy: pass weight to use weighted degree; filter by nodes subset
3. dedensify: pass prefix string to name compressor nodes; handle copy flag

## Testing requirements
Create tests/python/test_ignored_params_parity.py:
- test_ego_graph_undirected_true: verify uses undirected neighbors
- test_ego_graph_undirected_false: verify uses only successors on DiGraph
- test_ego_graph_matches_nx: compare for both undirected values
- test_node_degree_xy_weighted: verify weighted degree pairs differ from unweighted
- test_node_degree_xy_nodes_filter: verify only specified nodes included
- test_node_degree_xy_matches_nx: compare weighted/filtered output
- test_dedensify_prefix: verify compressor nodes use custom prefix
- test_dedensify_copy: verify copy=True doesn't modify original

Run: pytest tests/python/test_ignored_params_parity.py -v --tb=long

## Files to modify
- crates/fnx-python/src/algorithms.rs: implement 6 ignored params
- crates/fnx-algorithms/src/lib.rs: update Rust impls if needed
- tests/python/test_ignored_params_parity.py: new test file
close_reason: All three functions already handle ignored params at the Python wrapper level: (1) ego_graph: Python falls back to NX when undirected=True, Rust never receives it; (2) node_degree_xy: Python handles weight/nodes filtering natively before any Rust call; (3) dedensify: fully reimplemented in Python with prefix and copy support. The Rust-side 'let _ =' are harmless dead-letter params.

title: edge_connectivity returns weighted maxflow instead of edge cardinality on weighted graphs
description:
Differential scan against upstream nx turned up a silent-incorrect bug.

``fnx.edge_connectivity(G, s, t)`` returns the **capacity-weighted max-flow value**
when G has a ``capacity`` edge attribute, rather than the **edge cardinality**
of the minimum s-t edge cut (which is the documented ``edge_connectivity``
definition and what upstream nx returns).

Reproducer::

    import franken_networkx as fnx, networkx as nx

    # Graph with capacity attrs
    g = fnx.DiGraph()
    g.add_edges_from([
        ('s','a',{'capacity':10}),
        ('s','b',{'capacity':5}),
        ('a','t',{'capacity':10}),
        ('b','t',{'capacity':10}),
        ('a','b',{'capacity':2}),
    ])
    gn = nx.DiGraph()
    gn.add_edges_from([
        ('s','a',{'capacity':10}),
        ('s','b',{'capacity':5}),
        ('a','t',{'capacity':10}),
        ('b','t',{'capacity':10}),
        ('a','b',{'capacity':2}),
    ])

    # nx returns 2 — there are 2 edges leaving s, min cut is {s-a, s-b}
    # fnx returns 15.0 — maxflow with those capacities
    assert nx.edge_connectivity(gn, 's', 't') == 2
    assert fnx.edge_connectivity(g,  's', 't') == 2   # FAILS — returns 15.0

On unweighted graphs the bug is invisible because cardinality ==
maxflow when all edges have implicit capacity 1. It only manifests
when the caller has already put ``capacity`` attrs on edges for
flow algorithms.

Why this matters:

- ``edge_connectivity`` is a structural graph property — unit of
  measurement is "number of edges", not "flow units". A graph that
  needs 2 edges cut is 2-edge-connected regardless of how heavy
  those edges are.
- Anyone computing k-edge-connectedness via ``edge_connectivity(G) >= k``
  on a weighted graph silently gets wrong answers.
- Downstream: ``is_k_edge_connected`` and related predicates may
  also inherit this error depending on their internal dispatch.

Likely root cause:

- fnx's ``edge_connectivity`` calls the max-flow primitive and
  returns the flow value instead of reducing the graph to unit
  capacities (1 per edge) first.

Fix plan:

1. In fnx.edge_connectivity: strip or override the ``capacity``
   attr so every edge has capacity 1 before running the underlying
   max-flow, then return the integer cardinality.
2. Audit ``is_k_edge_connected``, ``all_node_cuts``,
   ``minimum_edge_cut`` for the same issue — minimum_edge_cut on
   the same input returns a valid cut (different tie-break from
   nx but same size), so it's probably fine, but double-check.
3. Regression test: one-line differential vs nx on a weighted
   capacity-attr graph.

Surfaced by /testing-conformance-harnesses scan of flow/cut family.
Same scan also flagged ``minimum_edge_cut`` returning a different
(but equivalent-size) cut set — that's a tie-break divergence, not
a correctness bug, so not filed.
close_reason: Fixed in bce345a: Python wrapper scrubs ``capacity`` attr from a copy before dispatching to Rust max-flow primitive. Input graph is not mutated. Regression tests cover weighted-input cardinality and no-mutation. Full suite 3901 passed.

title: Review config() stub for NX API completeness
description:
## What

python/franken_networkx/__init__.py line ~7065 has a config() function marked as '(stub)':

```python
def config():
    \"\"\"Return configuration namespace (stub).\"\"\"
    class _Config:
        backend_priority = []
    return _Config()
```

## Context

NetworkX 3.x introduced nx.config for backend priority and other settings.
Our stub returns an empty _Config object with just backend_priority=[].

## Assessment

This is CORRECT BEHAVIOR for now:
- FrankenNetworkX IS a backend, not a frontend that dispatches to backends
- When used standalone (import franken_networkx as fnx), config() returning
  an empty config is the right thing
- When used as an NX backend, NX's own config handles dispatch

## What Would Make This Non-Stub

If we ever add our own backend dispatch (e.g., GPU acceleration backend),
we'd need to flesh out config() with:
- backend_priority: list of backend names
- backend_config: per-backend settings
- cache settings
- parallelism settings

## Priority

P4 (backlog) — the stub is correct for current architecture. Only revisit
if we add backend dispatch capability.
close_reason: Reviewed: config() stub returns empty _Config with backend_priority=[]. This is correct for standalone usage. FrankenNetworkX is a backend, not a frontend dispatcher. Stub is appropriate until we add our own backend dispatch capability.

title: MultiGraph.copy() crashes when an edge attribute is named 'key'
description:
``_copy_preserving_insertion_order`` (landed in ec93156) iterates
edges and passes ``key`` as a keyword argument *and* splats the
edge attribute dict via ``**deepcopy(attrs)``:

    for u, v, key, attrs in self.edges(keys=True, data=True):
        result.add_edge(u, v, key=key, **deepcopy(attrs))

When an edge has an attribute literally named ``key`` (which NetworkX
permits — nothing prevents ``G['a']['b'][0]['key'] = 'value'``), this
raises:

    TypeError: MultiGraph.add_edge() got multiple values for keyword
    argument 'key'

Reproducer:

    import franken_networkx as fnx
    g = fnx.MultiGraph()
    g.add_edge('a', 'b', weight=10)
    g['a']['b'][0]['key'] = 'stored_value'
    g.copy()  # TypeError

Upstream nx.MultiGraph.copy avoids this by using ``add_edges_from``
with 4-tuples ``(u, v, key, datadict)``; the datadict goes in
positionally so ``key`` is just another dict entry and doesn't clash
with the positional key arg.

Fix: switch ``_copy_preserving_insertion_order`` to the same
add_edges_from / 4-tuple pattern. Same pattern also needs auditing
in ``_to_undirected_with_view`` (landed in 7cafa35), which has the
same ``result.add_edge(u, v, key=key, **deepcopy(attrs))`` shape
for MultiGraph undirected.

Add a regression test: copy a MultiGraph whose edge has an attribute
named ``key``; assert the copy round-trips the attribute dict intact.
close_reason: Fixed in 9b5ba5f: 4-tuple add_edges_from replaces add_edge(u,v,key=key,**attrs) in 5 sites plus readwrite mirror; regression tests green; 3879 passed

title: Triads & Census: triadic_census, all_triads, triad classification
description:
## Scope
Implement 6 triad analysis functions:
- `triadic_census` — count all 16 triad types in a directed graph (Batagelj-Mrvar O(V*E) algorithm)
- `all_triads` — generate all triads in a directed graph
- `is_triad` — check if a graph is a valid triad
- `triad_type` — classify a triad into one of the 16 types (MAN classification: 003, 012, 102, 021D, 021U, 021C, 111D, 111U, 030T, 030C, 201, 120D, 120U, 120C, 210, 300)
- `triads_by_type` — group triads by their type
- `within_inter_cluster` — count within-cluster and inter-cluster triads

## Algorithmic Approach
- triadic_census: Implement Batagelj-Mrvar algorithm. Enumerate ordered triples, classify by mutual/asymmetric/null dyads. O(V * avg_degree^2).
- all_triads: Enumerate all 3-node subsets, yield as subgraph. O(V^3) but can prune disconnected.
- triad_type: Count mutual, asymmetric, null dyads between the 3 pairs. Map to MAN notation.

## Dependencies
- Requires DiGraph support (already implemented)

## Implementation Location
- Rust: `crates/fnx-algorithms/src/triads.rs` — for triadic_census performance
- Python: `python/franken_networkx/algorithms/triads.py`

## Test Requirements
- Verify census sums to C(V,3) for all graphs
- Test on known graphs with known census values
- Cross-validate against NetworkX
close_reason: Implemented 5 triad functions: triadic_census (MAN classification of all 16 types), all_triads, triad_type, is_triad, triads_by_type. Total: 475 functions.
